nodes.label: A Study in Scarlet
nodes.description: A Study in Scarlet, the 1887 Sherlock Holmes novel by Arthur Conan Doyle that introduces Holmes and Watson and centres on the Lauriston Gardens murders involving Enoch Drebber and Joseph Stangerson.

nodes.label: The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes
nodes.description: The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes, the first Arthur Conan Doyle short-story collection featuring Sherlock Holmes and Watson, including A Scandal in Bohemia, The Red-Headed League, and The Blue Carbuncle.

nodes.label: The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin
nodes.description: The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar, the first Lupin short-story collection by Maurice Leblanc presenting Lupin's early escapades including his arrest aboard La Provence and the Queen's Necklace affair.

nodes.label: A Scandal in Bohemia
nodes.description: A Scandal in Bohemia, a Sherlock Holmes short story in which Holmes is hired by the King of Bohemia to recover a compromising photograph held by the opera singer Irene Adler.

nodes.label: The Red-Headed League
nodes.description: The Red-Headed League, a Sherlock Holmes short story in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes in which a bogus league paying red-haired men to copy encyclopaedias conceals a bank-tunnel robbery scheme.

nodes.label: The Blue Carbuncle
nodes.description: The Adventure of the Blue Carbuncle, a short story in 'The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes' in which Holmes traces a stolen blue carbuncle gem recovered from a goose's crop at Christmas.

nodes.label: The Arrest of Arsene Lupin
nodes.description: The Arrest of Arsène Lupin, the opening story of The Extraordinary Adventures in which Lupin is caught aboard the transatlantic liner La Provence by Inspector Ganimard after stealing jewels during the crossing.

nodes.label: Sherlock Holmes Arrives Too Late
nodes.description: Sherlock Holmes Arrives Too Late, a story from The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin featuring Herlock Sholmes, Leblanc's parodic stand-in for Holmes, who is outwitted by Lupin.

nodes.label: Lauriston Gardens murders
nodes.description: The Lauriston Gardens murders, the opening crime in A Study in Scarlet in which Enoch Drebber is found dead in an empty Brixton house with no apparent cause, initiating Holmes and Watson's first collaboration.

nodes.label: Bohemia photograph case
nodes.description: The Bohemia photograph case, the investigation in A Scandal in Bohemia in which Holmes is commissioned to recover a compromising photograph from Irene Adler to protect the reputation of the King of Bohemia.

nodes.label: Red-Headed League bank plot
nodes.description: The Red-Headed League bank plot, the criminal scheme in which John Clay and associates lure red-haired pawnbroker Jabez Wilson away from his shop each day so they can tunnel into the City and Suburban Bank beneath it.

nodes.label: Transatlantic Lupin arrest
nodes.description: The Transatlantic Lupin arrest, the event in The Arrest of Arsène Lupin in which Inspector Ganimard apprehends Lupin aboard the liner La Provence after a jewel theft during the Atlantic crossing.

nodes.label: Queen's Necklace affair
nodes.description: The Queen's Necklace affair, the case in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar, centred on the theft of Queen Marie-Antoinette's historic diamond necklace.

nodes.description: Sherlock Holmes, the world-famous consulting detective created by Arthur Conan Doyle, who investigates crimes across London and beyond with his companion Dr. Watson.

nodes.description: Dr. John H. Watson, army doctor and loyal friend to Sherlock Holmes, who serves as the primary narrator and chronicler of Holmes's cases across multiple collections.

nodes.label: Young Stamford
nodes.description: Young Stamford, the mutual acquaintance who introduces Watson to Sherlock Holmes at St. Bartholomew's Hospital in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Jefferson Hope
nodes.description: Jefferson Hope, the American cabman and avenger in A Study in Scarlet who travels from Utah to London to kill Enoch Drebber and Joseph Stangerson in retribution for the death of Lucy Ferrier in the Salt Lake Valley.

nodes.label: Enoch Drebber
nodes.description: Enoch Drebber, a former Mormon elder and the first murder victim in A Study in Scarlet, found dead at Lauriston Gardens in Brixton as part of Jefferson Hope's revenge for the forced marriage of Lucy Ferrier.

nodes.label: Joseph Stangerson
nodes.description: Joseph Stangerson, one of the two Utah Mormons murdered in London by Jefferson Hope as revenge for the death of Lucy Ferrier, his killing part of the Lauriston Gardens murders investigated in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Irene Adler
nodes.description: Irene Adler, the American opera singer in A Scandal in Bohemia who outwits Sherlock Holmes by discovering his fire-alarm ruse and escaping with the compromising photograph of herself and the King of Bohemia.

nodes.label: King of Bohemia
nodes.description: The King of Bohemia, the royal client who hires Sherlock Holmes to recover a compromising photograph held by Irene Adler, his former lover, in A Scandal in Bohemia.

nodes.label: Jabez Wilson
nodes.description: Jabez Wilson, the red-haired pawnbroker in The Red-Headed League who is lured away from his Saxe-Coburg Square shop each day by a bogus league paying him to copy encyclopaedias while John Clay tunnels into the bank below.

nodes.label: John Clay
nodes.description: John Clay, the master criminal who invents the Red-Headed League to lure Jabez Wilson away from his pawnbroker's shop while tunnelling into the vault of the City and Suburban Bank below, in The Red-Headed League.

nodes.label: Arsene Lupin

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin, the gentleman-burglar and master of disguise created by Maurice Leblanc, who outwits police and rival detectives across a series of French adventures.

nodes.label: Ganimard

nodes.description: Ganimard, the persistent but repeatedly outfoxed French police inspector who is Arsène Lupin's principal pursuer throughout the Leblanc stories, having arrested Lupin aboard La Provence only to see him escape again.

nodes.label: Herlock Sholmes

citations.quote: What an excitement there was about the intervention of Holmlock Shears, the famous English detective!

nodes.description: Herlock Sholmes (Holmlock Shears), Leblanc's thinly veiled pastiche of Sherlock Holmes who appears in the Arsène Lupin stories as a formidable English detective consistently outwitted by Lupin.

nodes.description: Watson narrator role, the narrative function by which Dr. John H. Watson records and publishes Sherlock Holmes's cases across A Study in Scarlet, The Sign of the Four, and the major story collections.

nodes.label: 221B Baker Street

nodes.description: 221B Baker Street, the London lodgings of Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson, serving as Holmes's base of operations and consulting rooms throughout the major collections.

nodes.label: Lauriston Gardens
nodes.description: Lauriston Gardens, the Brixton street in A Study in Scarlet where Enoch Drebber is found murdered in an empty house, the scene of the opening crime that brings Holmes and Watson together.

nodes.label: London
nodes.description: London, the primary urban setting of A Study in Scarlet and the wider Sherlock Holmes canon, the city where Holmes and Watson maintain their Baker Street base.

nodes.label: SS La Provence
nodes.description: The transatlantic French ocean liner aboard which Arsène Lupin commits a jewel theft and is subsequently identified and arrested by Inspector Ganimard in The Arrest of Arsene Lupin.

nodes.label: Holmes blood-stain reagent
nodes.description: Holmes's newly devised chemical reagent capable of detecting bloodstains on surfaces, demonstrated in his Baker Street laboratory as a key forensic advance in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Wedding ring clue
nodes.description: The wedding ring left at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene by Jefferson Hope, used by Holmes as an evidential clue pointing to the murderer's domestic history in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Irene Adler photograph
nodes.description: The Irene Adler photograph, the compromising image of Irene Adler with the King of Bohemia in A Scandal in Bohemia that the King wishes to recover before his royal marriage and that Adler uses as a safeguard.

nodes.label: Red-Headed League advertisement
nodes.description: The Red-Headed League advertisement, a fraudulent notice used in The Red-Headed League to lure Jabez Wilson away from his pawnshop so that criminals could tunnel into a nearby bank vault.

nodes.label: Queen's Necklace
nodes.description: The Queen's Necklace, Marie-Antoinette's famous historic diamond necklace whose theft is the central mystery of The Queen's Necklace from The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar.

nodes.label: Red-Headed League
nodes.description: The Red-Headed League, the fictitious organisation invented by John Clay and publicised by newspaper advertisement to lure the red-headed Jabez Wilson into a decoy encyclopaedia-copying job, masking the bank-tunnel plot in The Red-Headed League.

nodes.label: Jefferson Hope revenge
nodes.description: Jefferson Hope's long-pursued revenge upon Enoch Drebber and Joseph Stangerson for the abduction and death of Lucy Ferrier, culminating in the murders at Lauriston Gardens in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Lupin arrest aboard La Provence
nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's arrest aboard the transatlantic liner SS La Provence, identified by a telegram from Inspector Ganimard, in The Arrest of Arsène Lupin.

nodes.label: Drebber and Stangerson murders
nodes.description: The Drebber and Stangerson murders, the twin killings at the heart of A Study in Scarlet in which Jefferson Hope murders two Mormon elders in revenge for the abduction and forced marriage of Lucy Ferrier.

nodes.label: Drebber found dead at Lauriston Gardens
nodes.description: The discovery of Enoch Drebber's body at Lauriston Gardens, the opening event of A Study in Scarlet in which a man is found dead in an empty Brixton house with the word RACHE written in blood on the wall.

nodes.label: Wedding ring left at the murder scene
nodes.description: The wedding ring found at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene — a vital clue linking the victim to his past — examined by Holmes in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Jefferson Hope revenge motive
nodes.description: Jefferson Hope's years-long revenge motive in A Study in Scarlet, the obsessive pursuit of Enoch Drebber and Joseph Stangerson to London to avenge the death of his beloved Lucy Ferrier, caused by their actions in Utah.

nodes.label: Blood-stain reagent test
nodes.description: Holmes's new blood-stain reagent, first demonstrated in the laboratory scene at the opening of A Study in Scarlet, a chemical test capable of detecting the presence of human blood with reliability superior to earlier tests.

nodes.label: Wedding ring advertisement trap
nodes.description: A trap used by Sherlock Holmes in A Study in Scarlet, placing a newspaper advertisement for a found wedding ring to lure the murderer into revealing himself.

nodes.label: Bohemia photograph leverage scheme
nodes.description: The Bohemia photograph leverage scheme, the threat in A Scandal in Bohemia by which Irene Adler retains a compromising photograph of herself with the King of Bohemia as a safeguard against any royal reprisals.

nodes.label: Royal reputation motive
nodes.description: The motive in A Scandal in Bohemia: the King of Bohemia seeks to recover a compromising photograph held by Irene Adler that threatens his royal reputation.

nodes.label: Staged fire alarm reveal
nodes.description: Holmes's staged fire alarm in A Scandal in Bohemia—throwing a smoke rocket into Irene Adler's drawing room and raising a cry of fire—to trick her into instinctively revealing the hiding place of the compromising photograph.

nodes.label: Fire alarm reveals the photograph hiding place
nodes.description: The staged fire alarm thrown by Holmes into Irene Adler's drawing room in A Scandal in Bohemia, causing her to instinctively rush to protect the hiding place of the compromising photograph of herself and the King of Bohemia.

nodes.label: Red-Headed League bank tunnel scheme
nodes.description: The Red-Headed League bank tunnel scheme, the criminal plan in The Red-Headed League by which John Clay and associates dig a tunnel from Jabez Wilson's Saxe-Coburg Square shop into the vault of the City and Suburban Bank.

nodes.label: Decoy encyclopedia-copying job
nodes.description: The fraudulent Red-Headed League encyclopedia-copying job used by John Clay to keep Jabez Wilson away from his shop while a tunnel was dug into the City and Suburban Bank below, in The Red-Headed League.

nodes.label: Jabez Wilson lured away from his shop
nodes.description: The plot device in The Red-Headed League by which Jabez Wilson is lured away from his pawnbroker's shop via a decoy encyclopedia-copying job, allowing John Clay to tunnel into the bank below.

nodes.label: City bank cellar
nodes.description: The cellar of the City and Suburban Bank in Covent Garden, below which John Clay and his accomplice tunnel from Jabez Wilson's pawnshop in The Red-Headed League.

nodes.label: La Provence jewel theft
nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's theft of jewels aboard the transatlantic liner SS La Provence, the opening crime in The Arrest of Arsene Lupin from The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar.

nodes.label: Bernard d'Andrezy persona
nodes.description: The Bernard d'Andrézy persona adopted by Arsène Lupin aboard the SS La Provence during the events of The Arrest of Arsene Lupin.

nodes.label: Ganimard identifies Lupin on La Provence
nodes.description: The moment in The Arrest of Arsene Lupin when Inspector Ganimard identifies the passenger Arsène Lupin aboard the transatlantic liner La Provence, leading to his arrest.

nodes.label: Lupin notoriety game

citations.quote: 'Arsene Lupin versus Holmlock Shears! France versus England.... Revenge for Trafalgar at last!... Ah, the poor wretch ... he little thinks that I am prepared ... and a Lupin armed.'
Lupin, you see, has never quite exhausted his resources
To have found the lost secret and become its master, its sole master! To inherit such an inheritance! To live in the Needle, after all those kings!

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's motive of public notoriety and rivalry, framing his contest with Holmlock Shears as France versus England and revelling in the fame his exploits bring.

nodes.label: Queen's Necklace theft
nodes.description: The theft of Queen Marie-Antoinette's diamond necklace, a celebrated crime investigated by Arsène Lupin in The Queen's Necklace from The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsene Lupin, Gentleman-Burglar.

nodes.label: Inspector Tobias Gregson

citations.section: The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge / The Adventure of the Red Circle

nodes.description: Inspector Tobias Gregson, a Scotland Yard detective described as energetic and gallant within his limitations who appears in A Study in Scarlet and later in The Adventure of the Red Circle and The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.

nodes.label: Inspector Lestrade

citations.section: The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans / The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax

nodes.description: Inspector Lestrade, a Scotland Yard detective who appears throughout the Sherlock Holmes canon as a capable but conventional police officer who frequently calls on Holmes's superior reasoning.

nodes.label: Constable John Rance
nodes.description: Constable John Rance, the police officer who first discovers the body at the Lauriston Gardens crime scene in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: John Ferrier
nodes.description: John Ferrier, a settler in the Salt Lake Valley whose backstory is central to the motivation of Jefferson Hope in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Lucy Ferrier
nodes.description: Lucy Ferrier, the adopted daughter of John Ferrier in Salt Lake Valley whose forced marriage and death provide Jefferson Hope with his motive for revenge in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: RACHE bloodstain marking
nodes.description: The word RACHE written in blood on the wall at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene, correctly identified by Holmes as the German word for revenge rather than an unfinished woman's name as Scotland Yard assumed, in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Twin pillbox poison case
nodes.description: A twin pillbox containing one poisoned and one harmless pill, used by the murderer in A Study in Scarlet to give the victim a fatal choice.

nodes.label: Cab and boot footprints at Lauriston Gardens
nodes.description: The cab-track and boot footprints found by Sherlock Holmes at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene, used to deduce key physical characteristics of the killer Jefferson Hope in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Salt Lake Valley (Hope backstory)

citations.quote: This, Miss Morstan, is Pondicherry Lodge, said Mr. Thaddeus Sholto, as he handed her out.
Pondicherry Lodge stood in its own grounds, and was girt round with a very high stone wall topped with broken glass.

nodes.description: Salt Lake Valley (Hope backstory), the Utah setting in the backstory of A Study in Scarlet where Jefferson Hope witnesses Lucy Ferrier's forced marriage to Enoch Drebber and swears revenge that he eventually takes in London.

nodes.label: King of Bohemia
nodes.description: The King of Bohemia, who engages Sherlock Holmes to recover a compromising photograph held by Irene Adler in A Scandal in Bohemia.

nodes.label: Godfrey Norton
nodes.description: Godfrey Norton, the lawyer who marries Irene Adler and whose marriage ceremony Holmes inadvertently witnesses in A Scandal in Bohemia.

nodes.label: Vincent Spaulding alias
nodes.description: Vincent Spaulding, the alias used by the criminal John Clay while working as Jabez Wilson's assistant in order to dig a tunnel to the bank in The Red-Headed League.

nodes.label: Duncan Ross
nodes.description: Duncan Ross, an associate of John Clay who manages the fraudulent Red-Headed League office in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes to keep Jabez Wilson occupied while the bank tunnel is dug.

nodes.label: Inspector Peter Jones
nodes.description: Inspector Peter Jones, the Scotland Yard detective who collaborates with Holmes and Merryweather in the bank cellar to catch John Clay in The Red-Headed League.

nodes.label: Mr. Merryweather (City and Suburban Bank)
nodes.description: Mr. Merryweather, the chairman of the City and Suburban Bank who joins Holmes and Watson in the cellar vigil in The Red-Headed League to foil John Clay's tunnel robbery.

nodes.label: Briony Lodge, St. John's Wood
nodes.description: Briony Lodge in St. John's Wood, the home of Irene Adler where the compromising photograph of her and the King of Bohemia is concealed and where Holmes conducts his staged fire-alarm investigation in A Scandal in Bohemia.

nodes.label: City and Suburban Bank
nodes.description: The City and Suburban Bank, whose vaults John Clay and the Red-Headed League plot to rob by tunnelling from Jabez Wilson's adjacent shop, as investigated by Holmes in The Red-Headed League.

nodes.label: Red-Headed League newspaper advertisement
nodes.description: The Red-Headed League newspaper advertisement placed by John Clay to lure the red-headed Jabez Wilson into a fake encyclopaedia-copying job, providing cover for the tunnel being dug into the City and Suburban Bank below, in The Red-Headed League.

nodes.label: Encyclopedia copying-job pages
nodes.description: The pages of the Encyclopaedia Britannica that Jabez Wilson is paid to copy out at the Red-Headed League office — a ruse to keep him away from his shop while John Clay tunnels into the bank below in The Red-Headed League.

nodes.label: La Provence transatlantic liner
nodes.description: La Provence, the transatlantic French ocean liner on which Arsène Lupin commits jewel thefts and is identified and arrested by Inspector Ganimard, as described in The Arrest of Arsene Lupin.

nodes.label: Queen Marie-Antoinette's diamond necklace
nodes.description: Queen Marie-Antoinette's historic diamond necklace, the object stolen and at the centre of the plot in The Queen's Necklace from The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.

nodes.label: Rozaine traveler persona
nodes.description: The Rozaine traveller persona adopted by Arsène Lupin aboard the transatlantic liner La Provence, one of his disguises during the events of The Arrest of Arsene Lupin.

nodes.label: Ganimard's telegram identifying Lupin
nodes.description: The telegram sent by Inspector Ganimard to identify Arsène Lupin as a passenger aboard the transatlantic liner La Provence, leading to his arrest in The Arrest of Arsène Lupin.

nodes.label: The Hound of the Baskervilles
nodes.description: The Hound of the Baskervilles, a Sherlock Holmes novel by Arthur Conan Doyle in which Holmes investigates the legend of a phantom hound on Dartmoor and uncovers Jack Stapleton's scheme to murder Sir Henry Baskerville with a phosphorus-painted dog.

nodes.label: Baskerville curse investigation
nodes.description: The Baskerville curse investigation, the central case of The Hound of the Baskervilles, in which Sherlock Holmes is called in to investigate the seemingly supernatural threat hanging over the Baskerville family on Dartmoor.

nodes.label: The Innocence of Father Brown
nodes.description: The Innocence of Father Brown, the first Father Brown collection by G. K. Chesterton, opening with the Blue Cross story in which Father Brown outwits the criminal Flambeau through priestly moral inference.

nodes.label: The Blue Cross
nodes.description: The Blue Cross case, in The Innocence of Father Brown, in which Father Brown frustrates Flambeau's attempt to steal a valuable sapphire cross and assists Inspector Aristide Valentin in apprehending the master thief.

nodes.label: Father Brown

citations.section: The Secret of Father Brown (framing story)
citations.quote: Father Brown, paying a long-desired but long-delayed visit.

nodes.description: Father Brown, a mild-mannered Catholic priest and amateur detective created by G. K. Chesterton, who unravels crimes through keen moral and psychological insight.

nodes.label: The Red Thumb Mark
nodes.description: The Red Thumb Mark, a Dr. Thorndyke novel by R. Austin Freeman in which Thorndyke challenges fingerprint evidence by exposing a forged red thumb print.

nodes.label: Red thumb-mark case
nodes.description: A criminal case in which a forged thumb-print is used as evidence, investigated by Dr. Thorndyke in The Red Thumb Mark.

nodes.label: Dr. John Thorndyke

nodes.description: Dr. John Thorndyke, a medico-legal expert and forensic detective created by R. Austin Freeman, who solves crimes through rigorous scientific and medical analysis.

nodes.label: The Amateur Cracksman
nodes.description: The Amateur Cracksman, the first Raffles collection by E. W. Hornung in which the gentleman-cricketer A. J. Raffles begins his burgling career from his Albany chambers, with the Ides of March story initiating Bunny into the life.

nodes.label: The Ides of March
nodes.description: The Ides of March, the opening case of The Amateur Cracksman, in which Bunny Manders first becomes Raffles's criminal accomplice in a jewellery theft.

nodes.label: A. J. Raffles

nodes.description: A. J. Raffles, a celebrated amateur cricketer who is secretly a gentleman burglar, known as the 'amateur cracksman,' whose exploits are narrated by his accomplice Bunny Manders.

nodes.label: Baskerville hound plot
nodes.description: The Baskerville hound plot, the scheme in The Hound of the Baskervilles in which Jack Stapleton uses a phosphorus-painted hound to terrorise and kill the Baskerville heirs on Dartmoor.

nodes.label: Sir Henry Baskerville
nodes.description: Sir Henry Baskerville, the Canadian heir who arrives to claim the Baskerville estate on Dartmoor and becomes the target of Jack Stapleton's hound plot in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Jack Stapleton
nodes.description: Jack Stapleton, the villain of The Hound of the Baskervilles who orchestrates the hound plot against Sir Henry Baskerville on the Dartmoor moor.

nodes.label: Baskerville Hall
nodes.description: Baskerville Hall, the ancient Devonshire seat of the Baskerville family, near the treacherous Grimpen Mire, which is the setting for the hound plot in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Grimpen Mire
nodes.description: Grimpen Mire, the treacherous boggy wasteland near Baskerville Hall on Dartmoor, where Stapleton hides the hound and eventually meets his end in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Phosphorus-painted hound
nodes.description: The phosphorus paint applied to the body of a real hound to create the terrifying spectral appearance of the Baskerville curse beast, used by Stapleton to frighten and kill his victims in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Blue Cross theft plot
nodes.description: The Blue Cross theft plot, the scheme in the opening Father Brown story in which the criminal Flambeau impersonates a priest to steal Father Brown's Blue Cross relic, only to be outwitted through the priest's moral inference.

nodes.label: Flambeau

citations.section: The Secret of Father Brown (framing story)
citations.quote: FLAMBEAU, once the most famous criminal in France and later a very private detective in England, had long retired from both professions.

citations.section: The Secret of Father Brown (framing story)
citations.quote: He had resumed his real family name of Duroc; for the other title of 'The Torch' had only been a title nom de guerre.
his friend Flambeau sat down opposite. Flambeau was gloomy.
Flambeau, a semi-official investigator

nodes.description: Flambeau, once the most famous criminal in France who later became a private detective in England after his reform, serving as Father Brown's ally across several Chesterton collections.

nodes.label: Aristide Valentin
nodes.description: Aristide Valentin, the celebrated French detective who pursues Flambeau in The Blue Cross from The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Blue Cross relic
nodes.description: The Blue Cross, a valuable relic carried by Father Brown in the story of the same name and targeted for theft by the master criminal Flambeau.

nodes.label: Priestly moral inference
nodes.description: Father Brown's method of moral inference, used in The Blue Cross to detect Flambeau's imposture by noticing that the supposed priest made statements logically absurd and contrary to rational Christian thought.

nodes.label: Red thumb-mark forgery
nodes.description: The central crime of The Red Thumb Mark, in which a blood thumb-print is forged to falsely implicate Reuben Hornby, investigated and disproved by Dr. Thorndyke.

nodes.label: Reuben Hornby
nodes.description: Reuben Hornby, a man falsely accused on the basis of a forged red thumb-mark in Dr. Thorndyke's novel The Red Thumb Mark.

nodes.label: Bloody thumb print
nodes.description: The bloody thumb print found at the diamond-theft scene in The Red Thumb Mark, proved by Dr. Thorndyke's laboratory reconstruction to be a forgery rather than the genuine impression of the accused Reuben Hornby.

nodes.label: Fingerprint comparison
nodes.description: Dr. Thorndyke's forensic comparison of finger-prints used to challenge the validity of prosecution evidence in The Red Thumb Mark.

nodes.label: Laboratory reconstruction
nodes.description: Dr. Thorndyke's laboratory reconstruction method, applied in The Red Thumb Mark to demonstrate scientifically that the bloody thumb print used as evidence against Reuben Hornby was a manufactured forgery.

nodes.label: Raffles jewellery theft
nodes.description: The Raffles jewellery theft, the inaugural burglary in The Ides of March that launches the Raffles series, in which A. J. Raffles steals jewellery from Albany to pay off Bunny's gambling debts.

nodes.description: Bunny Manders, the loyal but morally conflicted companion and narrator of A. J. Raffles's criminal escapades, who follows Raffles into burglary while acknowledging he could do little more than keep up with him.

nodes.label: Albany chambers

nodes.description: The Albany, Raffles's fashionable bachelor chambers in Piccadilly that serve as his London base throughout the Raffles saga.

nodes.description: A. J. Raffles's burglary method of gaining social access to targets by attending their events over months — such as sitting behind Lady Lochmaben at first nights — before stealing their jewels.

nodes.label: Raffles sporting theft motive

nodes.description: Raffles's sporting theft motive, the philosophy articulated in The Amateur Cracksman by which A. J. Raffles views burglary as the only game that is always exciting and always full of danger, treating crime as a sport rather than a profession.

citations.quote: By EDGAR JEPSON AND MAURICE LEBLANC

nodes.description: The Arsène Lupin saga, the body of French detective fiction by Maurice Leblanc featuring the gentleman-burglar Arsène Lupin across novels and story collections including The Extraordinary Adventures, The Blonde Lady, The Hollow Needle, and The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Duke of Charmerace persona

nodes.label: Duke of Charmerace (real, deceased)

citations.quote: 'Poor Charmerace. He was indeed grand seigneur. With him a great name was about to be extinguished.... Did I hesitate? ... No.... I continued it'

nodes.label: Chief-Inspector Guerchard

nodes.label: 34B University Street (Lupin's ducal house)

nodes.label: Duke of Charmerace identity usurpation scheme

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's pre-prepared technique of piercing a concealed opening in a chimney wall large enough to remove furniture, employed in the Gournay-Martin house burglary in the Arsène Lupin novelization.

nodes.label: Advance warning letter tactic (Lupin's signature method)

citations.quote: 'For once in a way our dear Lupin's fondness for warning people will have given him a painful jar'
'That comes of this silly fondness for warning people of a coup,' growled Jean.

nodes.description: Lupin's signature tactic of sending advance warning letters to intended victims before a theft in Arsène Lupin, a fondness Ganimard notes sometimes causes Lupin unexpected operational difficulties.

nodes.label: Disguise and deep impersonation method

citations.quote: 'there seem to be no limits whatever to Lupin's powers of disguising himself. My colleague, Ganimard, has come across him at least three times that he knows of, as a different person.'
'he is a great actor. He actually becomes for the time being the person he pretends to be. He thinks and feels absolutely like that person.'

nodes.description: Lupin's method of total impersonation in Arsène Lupin, described by Ganimard as so complete that Lupin genuinely becomes the person he is pretending to be — thinking and feeling as that character — with no apparent limit to his transformations.

nodes.label: Drugging of soldiers (first Charmerace burglary)

citations.quote: 'The corporal was Lupin, and the soldiers were part of his gang'

'a motorist, who had stopped at the inn to get some supper, had called the soldiers in and insisted on standing them drinks. They had seemed a little fuddled before they left the inn, and the motorist had insisted on driving them to the château in his car. When the drug took effect he simply carried them out of it one by one, and laid them in the wood to sleep it off.'

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's ruse in the first Charmerace burglary, in which he disguised gang members as soldiers and had a motorist stand them drinks laced with a sedative, allowing the robbery to proceed while the guards slept.

nodes.label: Lupin's love for Sonia (drives his final surrender)

citations.quote: 'Never! You shan't touch her! By Heaven, you shan't touch her!' cried Lupin frantically; and he sprang like a tiger at Guerchard.
'I hold you through her: Sonia Kritchnoff.'
'I can't be much of a man of the world, to be in love like this!'

nodes.description: Lupin's overwhelming love for Sonia Kritchnoff in his own novelization, which breaks his self-control and causes him to spring at Guerchard like a tiger when Guerchard threatens to use her as a lever against him.

nodes.label: Lupin's greed for art and thrill of outwitting society

citations.quote: 'these rich men, these swells in their luxury—when one relieves them of a bank-note, how they do howl!'
'when one cannot be a great artist or a great soldier, the only thing to be is a great thief!'

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's self-declared motive in his own novelization: contempt for wealthy society combined with the ambition that, since he cannot be a great artist or soldier, he will be a great thief.

nodes.label: Sonia's desperate poverty driving her to theft

citations.quote: 'Have you ever been hungry? Think of it ... in this big city where I was starving in sight of bread ... bread in the shops.'
'I began to steal to remain an honest woman ... and I've gone on stealing to keep up appearances.'

nodes.description: Sonia Kritchnoff's motive in Lupin's novelization: extreme poverty in Paris—starvation in sight of bread in shop windows—initially driving her to steal to remain respectable, then to maintain the appearances she had established.

nodes.label: Charmerace identity scheme investigation

citations.quote: 'You lie! You escaped from the Santé four years ago. You are Lupin! I recognize you now.' 'Prove it,' said the Duke scornfully.
'the time when he passed as the Duke of Charmerace,' snapped Guerchard.

nodes.description: The Charmerace identity scheme investigation, the central thread of the Arsène Lupin novelisation in which Lupin operates for an extended period as the Duke of Charmerace before Chief-Inspector Guerchard finally unmasks him.

nodes.label: Coronet substitution under Gournay-Martin's eyes

citations.quote: 'Once I had changed the coronet under that fat old fool Gournay-Martin's very eyes... all I had to do was to slip away. Did I? Not a bit of it!'

nodes.description: The sleight-of-hand swap of the Lamballe coronet performed by Lupin under Gournay-Martin's very eyes in Arsène Lupin, enabling Lupin to slip away undetected after substituting a fake for the real jewel.

nodes.label: Lupin surrenders and is handcuffed by Guerchard

citations.quote: 'Arsène Lupin—and nabbed by you! If you aren't in luck! Here you are!' said Lupin bitterly; and he held out his wrists. Guerchard snapped the handcuffs on them with a grunt of satisfaction.

nodes.description: The moment Lupin bitterly holds out his wrists to be handcuffed by Detective Guerchard in Arsène Lupin, after his Charmerace identity scheme is exposed.

nodes.label: Sonia's escape from the house under Guerchard's nose

citations.quote: The Duke put his head inside the drawing-room door, and said to the empty room: 'Here is Mademoiselle Kritchnoff, M. Guerchard.' He held open the door, Sonia came down the stairs, and went through it.
'You're free, mademoiselle; and you can thank the Duke for it. You owe your liberty to him.'

nodes.description: Sonia Kritchnoff's escape from under Guerchard's nose in Arsène Lupin, engineered by the Duke who introduces her to an empty room and then secures her release from custody.

nodes.label: Lupin IS the Duke of Charmerace (true identity revealed)

citations.quote: 'No,' said Arsène Lupin.
Good-morning, Lupin. 'Good-morning, M. Guerchard,' said Lupin, with an ambiguous smile and all the air of the Duke of Charmerace.

nodes.description: The revelation in the novelization Arsene Lupin that Arsène Lupin is truly the Duke of Charmerace, confirmed in the confrontation where Guerchard addresses him as 'Lupin' and he responds with the ambiguous bearing of the Duke.

nodes.label: Victoire is Lupin's accomplice (blue chalk, wrote signatures)

citations.quote: 'One of M. Formery's innocents,' said Guerchard, turning to the Duke. 'The chalk?' said the Duke. 'It's blue,' said Guerchard, holding it out. 'The same as that of the signatures on the walls. Add that fact to the woman's sudden realization of what she was doing, and you'll see that they were written with it.'

nodes.description: In Arsène Lupin, Victoire is exposed as Lupin's accomplice when Guerchard finds blue chalk in her pocket — the same chalk used to sign 'Arsène Lupin' on the walls of the burglarised houses.

nodes.label: Lupin as master impersonator and gentleman-thief protagonist

citations.quote: 'the most whimsical, the most audacious, and the most genial thief in France. For the last ten years he has kept the police at bay. He has baffled Ganimard, Holmlock Shears, the great English detective, and even Guerchard'

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's role in his own novelization as France's most whimsical, audacious, and genial thief, who has kept the police at bay for ten years through disguise and impersonation.

nodes.label: The Blonde Lady

citations.quote:
THE BLONDE LADY

Being a Record of the Duel of Wits
between Arsene Lupin and the
English Detective

nodes.description: The Blonde Lady, an Arsène Lupin story collection by Maurice Leblanc recording the duel of wits between Lupin and the English detective Herlock Sholmes over the blue diamond and related thefts.

nodes.label: Alexander Teixeira De Mattos

citations.quote: Translated by Alexander Teixeira De Mattos
See 813, by Maurice Leblanc, translated by Alexander Teixeira de Mattos.
See The Hollow Needle by Maurice Leblanc, translated by Alexander Teixeira de Mattos, and later volumes of the Lupin series.
See The Confessions of Arsène Lupin. By Maurice Leblanc. Translated by Alexander Teixeira de Mattos.
TRANSLATED BY ALEXANDER TEIXEIRA DE MATTOS

nodes.description: Alexander Teixeira De Mattos, the English translator of several Arsène Lupin novels by Maurice Leblanc, including The Blonde Lady, The Crystal Stopper, and The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Wilson

citations.quote: I am also under an occasional obligation to the unspeakable Wilson, the friend and confidant of Holmlock Shears.
Mr. Wilson, my friend and assistant ... M. Arsene Lupin.

nodes.description: Wilson, the friend, confidant, and assistant to Herlock Sholmes (the Lupin series' parody of Watson) who accompanies him to France in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: The Blonde Lady

citations.quote: Who, for instance, does not know that strange case of the blonde lady
the blonde lady, the friend and accomplice of Arsene Lupin, against whom I have positive proofs

nodes.description: The Blonde Lady, the mysterious fair-haired friend and accomplice of Arsène Lupin, later identified as Clotilde Destange, in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: Clotilde Destange

citations.quote: As for his daughter Clotilde, she was looked upon as eccentric. She spent her days, like her father, in the house, but in another part of it, and never went out.
Clotilde Destange, the murderess of Baron d'Hautrec and the purloiner of the blue diamond! Clotilde Destange the mysterious friend of Arsene Lupin! The blonde lady, in short!

nodes.description: Clotilde Destange, the eccentric daughter of the architect Destange in The Blonde Lady, who is revealed by Arsène Lupin's investigation to be the murderess of Baron d'Hautrec and the thief of the blue diamond.

nodes.label: Lucien Destange

citations.quote: He found a public library, consulted a modern biographical dictionary and copied out the reference to 'Lucien Destange, born 1840, Grand-Prix de Rome, officer of the Legion of Honour, author of several valuable works on architecture'
the house which Lucien Destange occupied with his daughter Clotilde

nodes.description: Lucien Destange, a famous architect who uses his knowledge of secret passages in buildings of his own design to assist Arsène Lupin's operations, in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: M. Gerbois

citations.quote: M. Gerbois, professor of mathematics at Versailles College, rummaging among the stores at a second-hand dealer's, discovered a small mahogany writing-desk

nodes.description: M. Gerbois, a mathematics professor at Versailles College who discovers a lottery ticket at a second-hand dealer's that brings him into conflict with Arsène Lupin in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: Suzanne Gerbois

citations.quote: Suzanne was a pretty creature, of a demonstrative temperament and easily pleased. She threw her arms round her father's neck and kissed him as rapturously as though he had made her a present fit for a queen.
Mlle. Gerbois ... acted as a hostage of her own free will.

nodes.description: Suzanne Gerbois, the daughter of Professor Gerbois who serves willingly as Arsène Lupin's hostage — carried off in a fair-haired lady's car — as part of the lottery ticket scheme in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: Baron d'Hautrec

citations.quote: old General Baron d'Hautrec, who had been French Ambassador in Berlin under the Second Empire, was sleeping comfortably in an easy-chair in the house which his brother had left him six months before, at 134, Avenue Henri-Martin.

nodes.description: Baron d'Hautrec, a former French Ambassador found murdered in his home, next to whom the Blue Diamond is stolen in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: Maitre Detinan

citations.quote: Maitre Detinan, an influential radical deputy, a man endowed with the highest integrity and a mind of uncommon shrewdness
Maitre Detinan was exceedingly sorry to say that he had never had the pleasure of meeting Arsene Lupin, but he had, in point of fact, received his instructions

nodes.description: Maître Detinan, an influential radical deputy and man of the highest integrity in The Blonde Lady, who acts as Arsène Lupin's trusted legal adviser despite never having formally met him.

nodes.label: Alice Demun

citations.quote: The governess, Alice Demun.
As an accomplice of Arsene Lupin, and of Bresson as well, she too must tell the adventure of the Baronne d'Imblevalle

nodes.description: Alice Demun, the governess at the Imblevalle household who acts as an accomplice of Arsène Lupin and Bresson in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: Maxime Bermond

citations.quote: 'Maxime Bermond!' cried M. Destange, in delight. 'My dear Maxime!... What stroke of good luck brings you here to-day?'
file devoted to Maxime Bermond, alias Arsene Lupin.

nodes.description: The Maxime Bermond persona, an alias under which Arsène Lupin is warmly welcomed by M. Destange in The Blonde Lady, officially recorded in a police file linking Maxime Bermond to Lupin.

nodes.label: Felix Davey

citations.quote: M. Felix Davey was leaving the flat which he occupied on the fourth floor of No. 8.
Ganimard believes that M. Felix Davey is merely an accomplice of Lupin's.

nodes.description: The Felix Davey persona, under which Arsène Lupin occupies a fourth-floor flat in The Blonde Lady, with the police believing Felix Davey to be merely one of Lupin's accomplices rather than Lupin himself.

nodes.label: M. Stickmann

citations.quote: 'Are you M. Stickmann?' asked the architect. 'Yes, sir.' 'My secretary writes that he is ill and sends you to continue the general catalogue of my books'
Holmlock Shears had carried the citadel.

nodes.description: The M. Stickmann persona used by Holmlock Shears in The Blonde Lady to gain entry to M. Destange's house posing as a secretary sent to continue architectural work.

nodes.label: The Blonde Lady Duel of Wits

citations.quote: Who, for instance, does not know that strange case of the blonde lady, with the curious episodes which were reported under flaring headlines as 'NUMBER 514, SERIES 23!' ... 'THE MURDER IN THE AVENUE HENRI-MARTIN!' ... and 'THE BLUE DIAMOND!'

nodes.description: The Blonde Lady Duel of Wits, the overarching case in The Blonde Lady collection in which Arsène Lupin matches wits against the English detective Herlock Sholmes over the blue diamond and Destange's secret files.

nodes.label: The Jewish Lamp theft

citations.quote: Just here, between this twelfth-century Virgin and that chased-silver tabernacle, there was a little Jewish lamp. It has disappeared.
The theft of the Jewish lamp was committed under the direction of Arsene Lupin.

nodes.description: The theft of a small Jewish lamp from between a twelfth-century Virgin and a chased-silver tabernacle, carried out under the direction of Arsène Lupin in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: Blonde Lady thefts

citations.quote: 'Is Antoinette Brehat ... none other than the blonde lady?' 'And Lupin, consequently, plotted both cases?' 'I think so.'
The blonde lady commits murder to steal the blue diamond and does not steal it. She steals it and does so to get rid of it for another's benefit.

nodes.description: The Blonde Lady thefts, a series of crimes in The Blonde Lady engineered by Arsène Lupin through the mysterious Antoinette Brehat—the blonde lady—centred on the blue diamond once belonging to the French royal crown.

nodes.label: Lottery ticket extortion scheme

citations.quote: At almost the same time, the Credit Foncier received another telegram: 'Number 514, series 23, is in my possession. ARSENE LUPIN.'
'The position is clear: I hold a ticket which I am not entitled to cash and you are entitled to cash a ticket which you do not hold.'

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's extortion scheme in which he holds lottery ticket No. 514 Series 23 stolen from M. Gerbois's Napoleon writing-desk, demanding a share of the prize as the price of surrender, in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: Baron d'Hautrec murder

citations.quote: The handle of a little steel dagger gleamed near the body. The blade was dripping with blood.

Baron d'Hautrec was subject to fits of insanity which only Soeur Auguste was able to check ... He must have thrown himself upon you in her absence; and it was in the course of the ensuing struggle that you struck at him, in self-defence.

nodes.description: The murder of Baron d'Hautrec at his Paris residence at 134 Avenue Henri-Martin, with a strand of golden hair found in his hand linking the crime to Clotilde Destange, as investigated in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: Blue Diamond

citations.quote: the blue diamond! The celebrated diamond which formed part of the royal crown of France and which was presented by the Duc d'Alais to Leonide Latouche and, on her death, was bought by Baron d'Hautrec in memory of the brilliant actress whom he had passionately loved.
It was a magnificent diamond of enormous size and incomparable purity and of that undefined blue which clear water takes from the sky which it reflects

nodes.description: The Blue Diamond, a magnificent stone of enormous size and incomparable blue clarity that was once part of the French royal crown, stolen and used as a pawn in Arsène Lupin's schemes in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: Lottery ticket No. 514 Series 23

citations.quote: 'THIRD DRAWING OF THE PRESS-ASSOCIATION LOTTERY' 'First prize, 1,000,000 francs: No. 514, Series 23.'
number 514, series 23, was the number of his ticket! He had bought it by accident, to oblige one of his friends, for he did not believe in luck; and now he had won!

nodes.description: Lottery ticket No. 514, Series 23 in 'The Blonde Lady', the winning ticket for one million francs in the Press-Association Lottery, which Lupin uses as the basis of an extortion scheme.

nodes.label: Napoleon writing-desk

citations.quote: The desk, which is made of yew and mahogany, decorated with acanthus-leaf capitals, was found in Marie Walewska's discreet little house at Boulogne-sur-Seine and has an inscription on one of the drawers: 'Dedicated to Napoleon I., Emperor of the French, by his most faithful servant, Mancion.'

nodes.description: A yew-and-mahogany writing desk with acanthus-leaf capitals that had belonged to Marie Walewska in 'The Blonde Lady', recovered as part of the lottery-ticket extortion scheme.

nodes.label: Jewish Lamp

citations.quote: It is one of those lamps which they used to employ in the old days, consisting of a stem and of a receiver to contain the oil ... the one in question formed a hiding-place in which we had made it a practice to keep a magnificent antique jewel, a chimera in gold, set with rubies and emeralds

nodes.description: An ancient Jewish oil lamp consisting of a stem and oil reservoir, a rare antique stolen by Lupin in the second episode of 'The Blonde Lady.'

nodes.label: Imitation blue diamond

citations.quote: 'The blue diamond found in the tooth-powder is an imitation diamond. The real one you kept.'

I had an exact copy made; and as, at that time, I had designs upon the Comtesse de Crozon's other jewels and as the Austrian consul was already under suspicion, the aforesaid blonde lady ... slipped the imitation diamond into the aforesaid consul's luggage.

nodes.description: An exact imitation of the real blue diamond, made in advance by Lupin and substituted in the Comtesse de Crozon's tooth-powder box in 'The Blonde Lady', so that the genuine stone could be quietly retained.

nodes.label: 134 Avenue Henri-Martin

citations.quote: the house which his brother had left him six months before, at 134, Avenue Henri-Martin

nodes.description: 134 Avenue Henri-Martin, Paris, the house Baron d'Hautrec inherited from his brother six months before his murder, the crime scene in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: 25 Rue Clapeyron

citations.quote: '25, Rue Clapeyron!' No. 25, Rue Clapeyron, is separated from the Boulevard des Batignolles by the house at the corner.

nodes.description: 25 Rue Clapeyron, Paris, the address from which Suzanne Gerbois is taken hostage by the Blonde Lady in The Blonde Lady, separated from the Boulevard des Batignolles by a corner house.

nodes.label: Chateau de Crozon

citations.quote: 'There's an inscription over the front door: Lucien Destange, architect, 1877.'
On the evening of the tenth of August, M. and Madame de Crozon's guests were gathered in the drawing-room of the magnificent chateau overlooking the Bay of Somme.

nodes.description: Château de Crozon in 'The Blonde Lady', built by the architect Lucien Destange in 1877 and the setting of the blue diamond theft from the Comtesse de Crozon during a house party.

nodes.label: Hotel Destange, Place Malesherbes

citations.quote: the magnificent house at the corner of the Place Malesherbes and the Rue Montchanin

nodes.description: The Hôtel Destange at the corner of Place Malesherbes and Rue Montchanin in 'The Blonde Lady', the magnificent private house of architect Lucien Destange and a centre of Lupin's operations.

nodes.label: 40 Rue Chalgrin

citations.quote: The two pals went off on foot, made for the Etoile and rang at the door of No 40, Rue Chalgrin, a house with a narrow frontage.

nodes.description: 40 Rue Chalgrin, a narrow-fronted house near the Étoile in Paris in 'The Blonde Lady', visited on foot by Lupin and a companion during the investigation into Herlock Sholmes's network.

nodes.label: Yacht Hirondelle

citations.quote: 'Is the Hirondelle ready?' 'Quite ready, sir.'
By three o'clock they had left the estuary of the Seine and entered the Channel.

nodes.description: The yacht Hirondelle in The Blonde Lady, Lupin's private escape vessel that leaves the Seine estuary and enters the Channel by three o'clock after his second arrest and escape.

nodes.label: Destange architect secret-passage method

citations.quote: those three houses, Wilson, were built by the same architect. It was easy to guess that, you say? Certainly it was.... And that's why nobody thought of it.
I now understand how the same architect, by contriving similar plans, enabled three actions to be performed which appeared to be miraculous, though they were really quite easy and simple.

nodes.description: Lucien Destange's architectural method, deduced by Herlock Sholmes in The Blonde Lady, by which three houses built to similar plans by the same architect contain secret passages that enabled Arsène Lupin to commit seemingly impossible crimes.

nodes.label: Telephone impersonation trap

citations.quote: At that moment, he glanced at Lupin. And the look which they exchanged showed them that the same thought had struck them both ... it was the blonde lady telephoning. She thought that she was telephoning to Felix Davey, or, rather, Maxime Bermond; and she was about to confide in Holmlock Shears!
'So go at once, dear, and I shall be with you soon.'

nodes.description: A telephone impersonation trap in 'The Blonde Lady' in which a voice is used to lure a target away, a ruse that both Herlock Sholmes and Lupin recognise simultaneously when they exchange a glance.

nodes.label: Watch manipulation to advance departure time

citations.quote: 'I put his watch on an hour.' 'How?' 'The only way in which one can put a watch on, by turning the winder. We were sitting together chatting and I told him things that interested him.... By Jove, he noticed nothing!'

nodes.description: Lupin's ruse in 'The Blonde Lady' of secretly advancing Herlock Sholmes's watch by one hour while sitting beside him, causing Sholmes to leave earlier than intended and miss a key event.

nodes.label: Lupin's love for Clotilde Destange

citations.quote: 'What do you want?' 'Mlle. Destange's liberty.' ... 'It was a question of myself, Mr. Shears. Now it involves a woman ... and a woman whom I love. You see, we have very peculiar ideas about these things in France'

nodes.description: Lupin's motive for negotiating with Herlock Sholmes in 'The Blonde Lady': his love for Clotilde Destange, which causes him to put her liberty above his own safety when Sholmes threatens her.

nodes.label: All three crime scenes built by Destange

citations.quote: One of the stones above the door, on the right, bore the inscription: 'Destange, architect, 1875.'
On one of the stones of the house, he read the words: 'Destange, architect, 1874.'

nodes.description: The discovery in The Blonde Lady that all three crime-scene houses bear the 'Destange, architect' inscription, proving Lupin exploited Destange's hidden-passage designs across multiple properties.

nodes.label: Diamond found at Chateau de Crozon is imitation

citations.quote: 'As I thought: it is a faked diamond.' 'Faked?' 'Yes, by a new process which consists in subjecting diamond-dust to enormous heat until it melts ... whereupon it is simply reformed into a single diamond.'

nodes.description: The diamond recovered at the Château de Crozon in The Blonde Lady is an imitation produced by a new process of compressing diamond-dust under sufficient heat, with the real stone still hidden.

nodes.label: Lupin's second arrest (then escape)

citations.quote: Ganimard stepped forward and, greatly excited, with all the gravity that the act demanded, brought his hand slowly down upon his adversary's shoulder and enjoyed the infinite satisfaction of saying: 'Lupin, I arrest you.'

He separated his wrists with a growing and continuous effort of every muscle. The veins on his forehead swelled. The links of the chain dug into his skin. 'Now then!' The chain snapped and broke in two.

nodes.description: In The Blonde Lady, Herlock Sholmes arrests Lupin a second time, but Lupin snaps his handcuffs by sheer muscular effort and escapes before being taken to prison.

nodes.label: Suzanne Gerbois taken as hostage

citations.quote: That evening the papers contained the news that Mlle. Gerbois had been kidnapped.

A lady with fair hair--exceedingly fair hair, the witness said--inside. The car returned from Versailles an hour later ... This second lady was wrapped up in veils and shawls. No doubt it was Suzanne Gerbois.

nodes.description: The kidnapping of Suzanne Gerbois from Versailles in a car driven by a fair-haired woman, taken as Arsène Lupin's hostage to force her father's compliance in the lottery ticket scheme in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: First Episode: The Blonde Lady

citations.quote:
FIRST EPISODE: THE BLONDE LADY
CHAPTER I. Number 514, Series 23
II. The Blue Diamond
III. Holmlock Shears Opens Hostilities
IV. A Glimmer in the Darkness
V. Kidnapped
VI. The Second Arrest of Arsene Lupin

nodes.description: The first episode of 'The Blonde Lady', titled 'The Blonde Lady', opening with chapter I ('Number 514, Series 23') and tracing the lottery-ticket extortion scheme and Lupin's rivalry with Holmlock Shears.

nodes.label: Second Episode: The Jewish Lamp

citations.quote: SECOND EPISODE: THE JEWISH LAMP

nodes.description: The second episode of 'The Blonde Lady', titled 'The Jewish Lamp', the second major instalment of Lupin's contest with Holmlock Shears.

nodes.label: Lupin's criminal gang

citations.quote: Not only was Arsene not being shadowed, but these men were members of his gang! These men were watching over his safety! They were his bodyguard, his satellites, his vigilant escort.
The power represented by an association of this kind, ruled by such a chief, seemed boundless.

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's criminal organisation in 'The Blonde Lady', a disciplined association of members who shadow their chief's movements as bodyguards, described as having boundless power under such a leader.

nodes.label: Garnets from Clotilde's brooch

citations.quote: In the hall he turned toward a small, low door, which marked the entrance to the cellar, and picked up a small red stone: it was a garnet.
the cardboard box ... containing a dozen garnets similar to those which Shears had picked up

nodes.description: Red garnet stones from Clotilde Destange's brooch, found by Herlock Sholmes in Destange's cellar and confirmed by a matching box of a dozen similar garnets in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: Golden hair found in baron's hand

citations.quote: 'This handful of hair, which I found in the victim's right hand, dug into his flesh by the points of his nails.' He showed the hair; it was hair of a brilliant fairness, gleaming like so many threads of gold.
'That is certainly Mlle. Antoinette's hair. There is no mistaking it.'

nodes.description: A handful of golden hair clutched in Baron d'Hautrec's dead fingers in The Blonde Lady, identified as Mlle Antoinette Brehat's (Clotilde's) hair and used to implicate her in the murder.

nodes.label: File M.B. in Destange's cupboard

citations.quote: The small hours had struck before he found file M. B. It consisted of fifteen pages. One was a copy of the page concerning M. Harmingeat of the Rue Chalgrin. Another contained a detailed account of works executed for M. Vatinel, the owner of 25, Rue Clapeyron. A third was devoted to Baron d'Hautrec, 134, Avenue Henri-Martin; a fourth to the Chateau de Crozon

nodes.description: File M.B. discovered by Herlock Sholmes in Destange's cupboard in The Blonde Lady, a fifteen-page dossier connecting Clotilde Destange to Lupin's hidden-passage operations.

nodes.label: Real blue diamond hidden in Lupin's cane

citations.quote: 'Take my cane, over there, in the chimney corner. Hold the knob in one hand and turn the iron ferrule with the other.' Shears took the cane, turned the ferrule and, as he turned it, perceived that the knob became unscrewed. Inside the knob was a ball of putty. Inside the putty a diamond.

nodes.description: The real blue diamond concealed inside the hollow knob of Lupin's walking cane in The Blonde Lady, revealed by unscrewing the knob and turning the iron ferrule.

nodes.label: The Crystal Stopper

citations.quote:
THE CRYSTAL STOPPER

by Maurice LeBlanc

nodes.description: The Crystal Stopper, an Arsène Lupin novel by Maurice Leblanc in which Lupin races against time to save his associate Gilbert from execution by recovering a crystal stopper concealing a blackmail list held by the corrupt deputy Daubrecq.

nodes.label: Daubrecq

citations.quote: "So you're quite sure that you saw Daubrecq the deputy?"
ALEXIS DAUBRECQ, deputy of the Bouches-du-Rhône for the past two years; sits among the independent members.

The deputy was a stout, thickset, bull-necked man, very nearly bald, with a fringe of gray whiskers round his chin and wearing a pair of black eye-glasses under his spectacles, for his eyes were weak and strained.

nodes.description: Daubrecq, a stout, bald deputy and master blackmailer in The Crystal Stopper who holds a list of twenty-seven corrupt politicians concealed within a crystal stopper, using it to extort immunity for himself.

nodes.label: Clarisse Mergy

citations.quote: Twenty-five years ago, when my name was Clarisse Darcel and my parents living, I knew three young men at Nice.
So she was the mother of Gilbert, of Gilbert the prisoner at the Santé, relentlessly pursued by the authorities and now awaiting his trial for murder!

nodes.description: Clarisse Mergy, the mother of Lupin's imprisoned associate Gilbert in The Crystal Stopper, a woman formerly known as Clarisse Darcel who allies with Lupin to recover the crystal stopper and save her son from execution.

nodes.label: Gilbert

citations.quote: It was Gilbert and Vaucheray: Gilbert, a young fellow of twenty or twenty-two, with an attractive cast of features and a supple and sinewy frame
"Gilbert," he often used to say, "you are an honest man."
Gilbert is my son, my eldest son.

nodes.description: Gilbert, a young member of Lupin's gang in The Crystal Stopper, later revealed to be the son of Clarisse Mergy under his real name Antoine Mergy, who faces execution at the Santé Prison and whose fate drives the novel's action.

nodes.label: Vaucheray

citations.quote: Vaucheray, older, shorter, with grizzled hair and a pale, sickly face.
Vaucheray, a saturnine spirit, full of bitterness and envy, an ambitious, unscrupulous, gloomy, crafty man

nodes.description: Vaucheray, a saturnine, bitter, and unscrupulous older member of Lupin's gang in The Crystal Stopper who participates in the Villa Marie-Thérèse burglary and is condemned alongside Gilbert.

nodes.label: Prasville

citations.quote: among these new arrivals, Lupin was vastly astonished to recognize, by his figure and bearing, the famous Prasville, the erstwhile barrister, sportsman and explorer, now favourite at the Élysée, who, for some mysterious reason, had been pitchforked into the headquarters of police as secretary-general, with the reversion of the prefecture.
two years ago, Prasville and Daubrecq the deputy had had a personal encounter on the Place du Palais-Bourbon.

nodes.description: Prasville, the famous Prefect of Police in The Crystal Stopper who becomes entangled in the crystal stopper affair, having had a personal encounter with the deputy Daubrecq two years earlier on the Place du Palais-Bourbon.

nodes.label: Marquis d'Albufex

citations.quote: the second was the Marquis d'Albufex, a Bonapartist deputy, formerly chief political agent in France of Prince Napoleon.
the Marquis d'Albufex had been arrested for his complicity in the affair of the canal.
d'Albufex opened his veins last night, with a piece of broken glass, in his cell at the Santé.

nodes.description: The Marquis d'Albufex, a Bonapartist deputy and former political agent for Prince Napoleon in The Crystal Stopper who is arrested for complicity in the Two-Seas Canal Company bribery affair and subsequently opens his veins in his cell at the Santé.

nodes.label: Léonard

citations.quote: "It's Léonard, the valet!" cried Gilbert.
The man-servant was lying on his back, with his arms outstretched, a dagger stuck in his throat and a livid face.
play flunkey to Daubrecq the deputy...! Have you finished, Vaucheray?

nodes.description: Léonard, Daubrecq the deputy's manservant in The Crystal Stopper, found lying on his back with a dagger in his throat after being killed by Vaucheray and Gilbert during the burglary.

nodes.label: The Growler

citations.quote: "Growler?" he asked. "Masher?... Are you there?"
Growler and Masher, the two oarsmen, made straight for the opposite bank

nodes.description: The Growler, one of Arsène Lupin's two gang oarsmen who rows to the opposite bank during an operation, responding to Lupin's call and named alongside The Masher in The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: The Masher

citations.quote: "Growler?" he asked. "Masher?... Are you there?"
the Masher, the other rower, went to the gate on the Avenue de Ceinture

nodes.description: The Masher, one of Arsène Lupin's two gang oarsmen who goes to the gate on the Avenue de Ceinture during an operation, named alongside The Growler in The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Sébastiani

citations.quote: "Inquire about huntsman Sébastiani."

Sébastiani, a Corsican, was recommended to the Duc de Montmaur by the Marquis d'Albufex. He lives at two or three miles from the house, in a hunting-lodge built among the ruins of the feudal stronghold

nodes.description: Sébastiani, a Corsican huntsman recommended to the Duc de Montmaur by the Marquis d'Albufex and living a few miles from the chateau, investigated in connection with the Marquis in The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Jacques Mergy

citations.quote: "my poor little Jacques!"
He put down in the easy-chair a child of six or seven years of age, the tiniest little fellow in a gray jersey and a knitted woollen cap
my little Jacques, who had slipped into your bedroom, brought it to me.

nodes.description: Jacques Mergy, Clarisse Mergy's young son of six or seven in 'The Crystal Stopper', placed in an easy-chair by Lupin and unwittingly instrumental in delivering a key object to his mother.

nodes.label: Langeroux

citations.quote: "The man who came to see Daubrecq last night is Langeroux the deputy, leader of the independent left. A poor man, with a large family."

nodes.description: Langeroux, a deputy and leader of the independent left in The Crystal Stopper, a poor man with a large family who visited Daubrecq the night before and is numbered among The Twenty-Seven compromised by the canal company bribery affair.

nodes.label: Victorien Mergy

citations.quote: "Victorien Mergy the deputy?"
three years ago, Mergy the deputy had blown out his brains in the lobby of the Chamber, without leaving a word of explanation behind him

nodes.description: Victorien Mergy, a French deputy whose name appears on Daubrecq's list of the Twenty-seven implicated in the Two-Seas Canal bribery scandal, who shot himself in the lobby of the Chamber of Deputies three years before the events of The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Stanislas Vorenglade

citations.quote: Prasville is mixed up in the Canal affair! Not directly: that is to say, his name is not on the list of the Twenty-seven; but it is there under the name of one of his friends, an ex-deputy called Vorenglade, Stanislas Vorenglade, his man of straw
Vorenglade himself! Vorenglade, who, tired of living in poverty, wants to extort money from Prasville

nodes.description: Stanislas Vorenglade, a man in 'The Crystal Stopper' who, tired of living in poverty, attempts to extort money from Prasville by threatening to reveal that Prasville's name is connected via Vorenglade himself to the Canal affair.

nodes.label: Clémence

citations.quote: "Come, come, Clémence, did you open this stamp-box?"
I put him in touch with Clémence, the portress in the Square Lamartine, who was entirely devoted to me

nodes.description: Clémence, the portress at Daubrecq's address in the Square Lamartine in 'The Crystal Stopper', entirely devoted to Lupin and placed there by him to provide access and intelligence on Daubrecq's movements.

nodes.label: Achille

citations.quote: He was known there by the name of Michel Beaumont. He had a snug flat here and was looked after by a man-servant, Achille, who was utterly devoted to his interests

nodes.description: Achille, the manservant who looked after Arsène Lupin's flat in Paris under the name Michel Beaumont, as seen in The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Michel Beaumont persona

citations.quote: He was known there by the name of Michel Beaumont.
"Monsieur de Beaumont, Michel."
send a squad of men to the Rue Chateaubriand, at the corner of the Rue Balzac.... That's where our national hero lives, under the name of Michel Beaumont.

nodes.description: The Michel Beaumont persona, under which Arsène Lupin keeps a flat in the Rue Chateaubriand in The Crystal Stopper, looked after by his manservant Achille.

nodes.label: M. Nicole persona

citations.quote: "M. Nicole," said Clarisse, "a private teacher, who is acting as tutor to my little Jacques."
M. Nicole, 25, Place de Clichy. I am staying at a friend's flat
The visiting-card which he held in his hand bore these words: M. Nicole, Master of Arts, Private Tutor.

nodes.description: The M. Nicole persona, in which Arsène Lupin poses as a Master of Arts and private tutor living at 25 Place de Clichy in The Crystal Stopper, introduced to the household as young Jacques's tutor.

nodes.label: Dr. Vernes persona

citations.quote: "Can M. Daubrecq see Dr. Vernes?"
"No, monsieur le député, Dr. Vernes, of Saint-Germain."
The man who rang at Daubrecq the deputy's gate, at six o-clock that evening, was a stout, elderly gentleman, in a black frock-coat, a bowler hat, spectacles and whiskers.

nodes.description: The Dr. Vernes persona, a disguise used by Arsène Lupin in The Crystal Stopper, presenting himself as a stout elderly physician from Saint-Germain to gain access to Daubrecq the deputy.

nodes.label: Crystal stopper

citations.quote: It was a decanter-stopper, a little crystal stopper, like those used for the bottles in a liqueur-stand.
the knob, with its many facets, was gilded right down to the indent
he stooped and unscrewed the stopper himself. The inside of the knob was hollow; and in the hollow space was a piece of paper rolled into a tiny pellet.

nodes.description: A hollow crystal decanter stopper used by Daubrecq to conceal the list of the Twenty-seven, its gilded knob hiding a rolled piece of paper inside, central to the plot of The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Daubrecq's glass eye

citations.quote: "A glass eye!" "Why, of course!" cried M. Nicole, laughing gaily. "A common or garden decanter-stopper, which the rascal stuck into his eyesocket in the place of an eye which he had lost"
He pushed it. The eye was hollow. There was a tiny ball of paper inside.

nodes.description: Daubrecq's artificial glass eye in 'The Crystal Stopper', revealed to be hollow and containing a tiny ball of paper on which the Twenty-seven list is recorded, hidden in plain sight in his eye socket.

nodes.label: Hidden list

citations.quote: Daubrecq possesses a list of twenty-seven names.

There was a set of names written one below the other, twenty-seven of them, the twenty-seven names of the famous list: Langeroux, Dechaumont, Vorenglade, d'Albufex, Victorien Mergy and the rest. And, at the foot, the signature of the chairman of the Two-Seas Canal Company, the signature written in letters of blood.

nodes.description: The hidden list, the document at the centre of The Crystal Stopper containing the twenty-seven names of politicians bribed in the Two-Seas Canal Company scandal, which Daubrecq conceals within a crystal stopper to use as a blackmail instrument.

nodes.label: Maryland tobacco packet

citations.quote: He took from the chimney an unopened yellow packet and tore off the government band

He opened the packet and inserted his thumb and fore-finger, slowly, gingerly, like a conjurer performing a sleight-of-hand trick before a puzzled audience, and, beaming all over his face, extracted from the tobacco a glittering object which he held out before the spectators. Clarisse uttered a cry. It was the crystal stopper.
Maryland, Maryland tobacco, the only tobacco that Daubrecq smokes.

nodes.description: An unopened sealed packet of Maryland tobacco—the only brand Daubrecq smokes—used in 'The Crystal Stopper' to conceal the crystal stopper, inserted by loosening the government revenue band and re-sealing the packet.

nodes.label: Crystal stopper model (decoy)

citations.quote: There was nothing in that piece of crystal, nothing, do you understand? No paper, no hiding-place!
Because what you stole from Daubrecq was not the stopper made by his instructions, but the stopper which was sent to John Howard, the Stourbridge glassworker, to serve as a model.
There is a scratch at the bottom of the stem

nodes.description: A decoy crystal stopper in The Crystal Stopper — an exact replica of Daubrecq's original, identifiable by a scratch at the bottom of the stem but containing no hidden list.

nodes.label: Daubrecq's letter fragment about hollowing crystal

citations.quote: I picked up under his writing-table a letter which he had begun to write, crumpled up and thrown into the waste-paper-basket. It consisted of a few lines in bad English; and I was able to read this: 'Empty the crystal within, so as to leave a void which it is impossible to suspect.'

nodes.description: A crumpled draft letter found under Daubrecq's writing table in The Crystal Stopper, revealing his plan to hollow out a crystal stopper to conceal the list of the Twenty-seven.

nodes.label: Stourbridge glass-blower invoice

citations.quote: A month later, I discovered, among the ashes in the drawing-room fireplace, the torn half of an English invoice. I gathered that a Stourbridge glass-blower, of the name of John Howard, had supplied Daubrecq with a crystal bottle made after a model.

nodes.description: A torn invoice half found by Lupin in the ashes of Daubrecq's fireplace in 'The Crystal Stopper', its Stourbridge origin indicating that the crystal stopper was made in that English glass-blowing town.

nodes.label: Cross of Lorraine watermark

citations.quote: The list of the Twenty-seven, as we know for absolutely certain, was written on a sheet of letter-paper belonging to the chairman of the Canal Company, of which there are a few samples in this cash-box. Now all these samples have as a water-mark a little cross of Lorraine which is almost invisible, but which can just be seen in the thickness of the paper when you hold it up to the light.

nodes.description: A Cross of Lorraine watermark on the letter-paper on which the list of the Twenty-seven was written, identified by Lupin as a key link in The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Napoleon profile ivory fragment

citations.quote: Merely this... A little piece of ivory, which they picked up on the ground.

When you look at the knob in a certain way, you end by seeing that the outline represents the profile of the Little Corporal. What you have in your hand, monsieur le secrétaire-général, is a bit of the ivory knob at the top of a half-pay officer's life-preserver.

nodes.description: A small ivory fragment found near the Marquis d'Albufex in The Crystal Stopper, whose knob — viewed at the right angle — reveals the profile of Napoleon, linking d'Albufex to the missing crystal stopper.

nodes.label: Crystal stopper case

citations.quote: He had bad dreams. Gilbert and Vaucheray were kneeling on the flags of their cells, wildly stretching out their hands to him
The crystal stopper: everything turns on that.... Once I get hold of it....

nodes.description: The crystal stopper case, the central mystery of Leblanc's novel The Crystal Stopper in which Lupin seeks to recover the glass stopper that contains Daubrecq's blackmail list of twenty-seven corrupt politicians in order to save Gilbert from the guillotine.

nodes.label: Villa Marie-Thérèse burglary and murder of Léonard

citations.quote: "The Villa Marie-Thérèse is ours to do as we please with."
The man-servant was lying on his back, with his arms outstretched, a dagger stuck in his throat and a livid face.
the real identity of Vaucheray, one of the alleged murderers of Léonard the valet, has at last been ascertained.

nodes.description: The burglary of Villa Marie-Thérèse at Enghien and the murder of the manservant Léonard — stabbed with a dagger — carried out by Vaucheray as part of Arsène Lupin's gang's operations, central to The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Daubrecq blackmail scheme using the Twenty-seven list

citations.quote: "Come," said Lupin, "Daubrecq is nothing more nor less than a blackmailer"
A week after the theft, he went to the Chamber of Deputies, asked for my husband and bluntly demanded thirty thousand francs of him, to be paid within twenty-four hours.

he was using his power, gradually emerging from the shadow, lavishly squandering the money which he extorted from his victims, securing his election as a district-councillor and deputy, holding sway by dint of threats and terror

nodes.description: Daubrecq's systematic blackmail of twenty-seven French deputies and officials implicated in the Two-Seas Canal bribery scandal, using a list concealed in a hollow crystal stopper to extort money, the central plot of The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Daubrecq's murder of Prasville's opera-singer

citations.quote: on our wedding-day, Louis Prasville, who acted as my husband's best man in defiance of Daubrecq's opposition, went home to find the girl he loved, the opera-singer, dead, strangled
It was known that Daubrecq had been persecuting her with his attentions for some days

nodes.description: Daubrecq's murder of an opera-singer in The Crystal Stopper, in which Daubrecq killed the woman on the very day she married Louis Prasville — having persecuted her with his attentions for some days beforehand.

nodes.label: Two-Seas Canal Company bribery scheme

citations.quote: his name was on the list of the Twenty-seven.

the chairman of the company, before dying, affixed his signature in letters of blood. There is more than that. There are certain less positive proofs, which the people interested do not know of: the correspondence between the chairman and the secretary
the affair of the canal was published in the papers, with its whole series of swindles and abominations, that my husband knew that a number of his fellow-members had been bribed

nodes.description: A bribery scheme involving twenty-seven deputies and officials of the Two-Seas Canal Company, whose names Daubrecq uses for blackmail in The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: d'Albufex kidnapping and torture of Daubrecq

citations.quote: "Daubrecq has disappeared." "Eh? What's that? Daubrecq disappeared?" "Yes, carried off from his house, in broad daylight."
the captive chained down upon the truckle-bed
Sébastiani made the stick turn a complete circle. The thongs stretched and tightened. Daubrecq gave a groan.

nodes.description: The Marquis d'Albufex's kidnapping and torture of Daubrecq, who is chained to a truckle-bed in Château de Mortepierre's Lovers' Tower and subjected to rack, to extract the list of deputies in The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Lupin's sniper shots at the scaffold

citations.quote: a shot, a shot fired from the other side, from one of the houses opposite.
Blood spurted from Vaucheray's forehead and covered his face.
a man holding a rifle in his hand and surrounded with a cloud of smoke.

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's sniper shots fired from a house opposite the Boulevard Arago scaffold, mortally wounding Vaucheray to prevent disclosure on the day of execution in The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Clarisse's revenge against Daubrecq

citations.quote: Yes, revenge has been the motive of my conduct and the sole preoccupation of my life. To avenge my husband, to avenge my ruined son, to avenge myself for all the harm that he has done me: I had no other dream, no other object in life.

nodes.description: Clarisse Mergy's sole declared motive throughout 'The Crystal Stopper': revenge against Daubrecq for destroying her husband and corrupting her son Jacques, which she describes as the preoccupation of her entire life.

nodes.label: Lupin's determination to save Gilbert from the scaffold

citations.quote: "My object is simple enough. It is the release of my friends Gilbert and Vaucheray."
"Gilbert," he often used to say, "you are an honest man."
