"The governor's here!"... a voice rose above the surrounding clamour

nodes.description: Lupin's declared primary objective in 'The Crystal Stopper': to free his loyal accomplice Gilbert from the death sentence, driven by personal affection and his conviction that Gilbert was fundamentally an honest man.

nodes.label: Daubrecq's lust for power and revenge over Clarisse's rejection

citations.quote: Daubrecq ground his teeth. He stamped his feet. His bloodshot eyes...rolled in their sockets; and he kept on saying, 'I shall be revenged.... I shall be revenged.... Oh, you don't know what I am capable of!... I shall wait ten years, twenty years, if necessary....'
For more than twenty years have I awaited my hour

nodes.description: Daubrecq's twin motives in 'The Crystal Stopper' of political power over the Twenty-seven deputies and personal revenge against Clarisse for her rejection of him more than twenty years earlier.

nodes.label: Cut door-panel acrobat infiltration

citations.quote: Were cut out by his instructions, in anticipation of the contest on which he was embarking against you and against Daubrecq, at whose house he did the same thing. He had under his orders a sort of acrobat, an extraordinarily thin dwarf, who was able to wriggle through those apertures

nodes.description: A break-in technique used by Vaucheray at Daubrecq's instruction in 'The Crystal Stopper', involving panels cut out of a door to allow an acrobat to slip through in preparation for the contest against Daubrecq.

nodes.label: Cliff escalade at Mortepierre (Lovers' Tower rescue)

citations.quote: He put his foot on the ladder and made sure that it did not sway too much. Then he went up.
He clutched at the rough surface of the cliff and continued the climb, with bruised nails and bleeding fingers.
What another has done I can do.

nodes.description: Lupin's bare-hands cliff ascent to reach the Mortepierre Lovers' Tower in The Crystal Stopper, climbing first by ladder and then over rough rock with bruised nails and bleeding fingers to free a prisoner.

nodes.label: Chloroform capture of Daubrecq at Nice hotel

citations.quote: some one slipped behind Daubrecq, sprang up fiercely, flung one of his arms round Daubrecq's neck, threw him to the ground with incredible violence and applied a pad of cotton-wool to his face. A sudden smell of chloroform filled the room.
Give him a few drops of chloroform every three or four hours: it's his one weakness

nodes.description: Lupin's technique of overpowering Daubrecq with chloroform at a Nice hotel in The Crystal Stopper, administering small doses every few hours to exploit Daubrecq's known weakness to the drug.

nodes.label: Tobacco-packet hiding place for crystal stopper

citations.quote: Maryland, Maryland tobacco, the only tobacco that Daubrecq smokes.
all Daubrecq had to do, in order to introduce the crystal stopper, was to bear upon the band a little, loosen it, draw it back, unfold the yellow paper, remove the tobacco and fasten it up again.
The packet itself, the plug of Maryland made up and passed by the State and by the Inland Revenue Office, was a sacred, intangible thing, a thing above suspicion!

nodes.description: Daubrecq's concealment method in 'The Crystal Stopper' of hiding the crystal stopper inside a sealed packet of Maryland tobacco—his exclusive brand—by loosening the government revenue band and re-inserting it with the stopper inside.

nodes.label: Glass eye concealment of the Twenty-seven list

citations.quote: 'as the list is not to be discovered away from Daubrecq,' I said to myself, 'it cannot exist away from Daubrecq. And, as it is not to be discovered in the clothes he wears, it must be hidden deeper still, in himself, to speak plainly, in his flesh, under his skin...'

A common or garden decanter-stopper, which the rascal stuck into his eyesocket in the place of an eye which he had lost--a decanter-stopper, or, if you prefer, a crystal stopper, but the real one, this time, which he faked, which he hid behind the double bulwark of his spectacles and eye-glasses

nodes.description: Daubrecq's method of concealing the Twenty-seven list inside a hollow glass eye worn in place of a missing natural eye, as revealed in 'The Crystal Stopper' when Lupin deduces the list cannot exist away from Daubrecq's person.

nodes.label: Raid on Villa Marie-Thérèse at Enghien

citations.quote: The two boats fastened to the little pier that jutted out from the garden lay rocking in its shadow. Here and there lighted windows showed through the thick mist on the margins of the lake. The Enghien Casino opposite blazed with light

nodes.description: The night-time raid on Villa Marie-Thérèse at Enghien that opens 'The Crystal Stopper', conducted from two boats moored at the garden pier while lights flicker in the darkened house.

nodes.label: Arrest of Gilbert and Vaucheray

citations.quote: Gilbert was at once seized and firmly bound.
For the first time, it held accomplices of Lupin in its clutches--declared, undisputed accomplices--and those accomplices had committed a murder.

nodes.description: The arrest of Gilbert and Vaucheray in 'The Crystal Stopper', the first time the authorities held declared accomplices of Lupin in custody, seizing Gilbert immediately and binding him firmly.

nodes.label: Trial verdict: Gilbert and Vaucheray sentenced to death

citations.quote: After a long deliberation, the jury returned to court and the foreman read out the answers to the questions put from the bench. The answer was "Yes" to every count of the indictment, a verdict of guilty without extenuating circumstances.
the assize-president asked them: 'Have you anything more to say, Vaucheray?'

nodes.description: The assize-court verdict in 'The Crystal Stopper' in which the jury, after long deliberation, sentences both Gilbert and Vaucheray to death, the president asking each in turn whether they have anything more to say.

nodes.label: Lupin's cliff rescue and knife attack at Mortepierre

citations.quote: At the same moment, he felt a violent pain in the shoulder: "Blast it!" he said, sinking to the ground. Daubrecq had stabbed him with a knife below the nape of the neck
You are Master Arsène Lupin, are you not? Clarisse's protector, Gilbert's saviour....

nodes.description: Lupin's cliff-top action at Mortepierre in 'The Crystal Stopper', in which he saves a prisoner from the drop but is immediately stabbed in the shoulder by Daubrecq and collapses to the ground.

nodes.label: Daubrecq's suicide at Gare du Nord

citations.quote: He had not gone fifty steps when he heard the sound of a shot. He turned round. Daubrecq had blown his brains out. "De profundis!" murmured Lupin, taking off his hat.

nodes.description: Daubrecq's suicide at the Gare du Nord in 'The Crystal Stopper', shooting himself in the head within fifty steps of leaving Lupin, as he had no escape and no further power.

nodes.label: Gilbert's escape from Île de Ré

citations.quote: Two months later, Gilbert, whose sentence had been commuted to one of penal servitude for life, made his escape from the Île de Ré, on the day before that on which he was to have been transported to New Caledonia.

nodes.description: Two months after his death sentence was commuted to penal servitude for life, Gilbert escapes from the penal colony at Île de Ré in 'The Crystal Stopper', the fulfilment of Lupin's long campaign to save him.

nodes.label: Daubrecq stole the list from dying Germineaux

citations.quote: Alexis Daubrecq, who, for six months, disguised beyond recognition, had acted as Germineaux's secretary. It does not matter how he discovered that Germineaux was the possessor of the paper in question. The fact remains that he broke open the chest on the night before the death.

nodes.description: In The Crystal Stopper, Daubrecq spent six months disguised as dying Germineaux's secretary in order to steal the list of the Twenty-seven from him.

nodes.label: Gilbert's real identity as Antoine Mergy

citations.quote: "Antoine?" "Yes, Antoine is Gilbert's real name. The unhappy boy has at least succeeded in concealing his identity."
He is farming his own land, way down in Algeria, under his real name, his only name of Antoine Mergy.

nodes.description: Gilbert, held in the Santé in The Crystal Stopper, is in truth Antoine Mergy, a young man who concealed his real name and eventually returned to farm land in Algeria under it.

nodes.label: Daubrecq deliberately corrupted Gilbert in revenge against Clarisse

citations.quote: his evil genius, the man who, secretly, unknown to his parents, enticed him away from school, the man who led him astray, who corrupted him, who took him from us, who taught him to lie, to waste his substance and to steal.
Daubrecq had taken his revenge. On the day after my husband turned our unhappy child out of the house, Daubrecq sent us a most cynical letter in which he revealed the odious part which he had played

nodes.description: The disclosure in 'The Crystal Stopper' that Daubrecq secretly lured Gilbert away from school and led him into a criminal life as deliberate revenge against Clarisse, acting on the day after her husband expelled the boy.

nodes.label: The list found in the tobacco packet was a forgery

citations.quote: "Not a bit of it! It is you who have been taken in, my poor friend. Daubrecq has the real list, the list which he stole from the dying man's safe." "But this one...." "This one is a forgery."

nodes.description: The discovery that the list of deputies found in the tobacco packet is a decoy forgery and that Daubrecq still holds the real list he originally stole, revealed in The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: Prasville's name on the list via Vorenglade

citations.quote: Prasville is mixed up in the Canal affair! Not directly: that is to say, his name is not on the list of the Twenty-seven; but it is there under the name of one of his friends, an ex-deputy called Vorenglade, Stanislas Vorenglade, his man of straw

nodes.description: The revelation in 'The Crystal Stopper' that Prasville is implicated in the Canal affair not by direct entry on the Twenty-seven list but through Vorenglade, who uses this knowledge to extort him.

nodes.label: Villa Marie-Thérèse, Enghien

citations.quote: "The Villa Marie-Thérèse is ours to do as we please with."
the discovery of the body in the pantry restored the commissary to a sense of his responsibility.

nodes.description: Villa Marie-Thérèse at Enghien in The Crystal Stopper, Daubrecq's country house burglarised by Lupin's gang at the story's opening and the scene of a murder discovered in the pantry.

nodes.label: Square Lamartine, Paris (Daubrecq's house)

citations.quote: Daubrecq had moved to his winter quarters in Paris immediately after the burglary at the Villa Marie-Thérèse and was now living in his own house, on the left-hand side of the little Square Lamartine that opens out at the end of the Avenue Victor-Hugo.

nodes.description: Square Lamartine, Paris, Daubrecq's winter address in The Crystal Stopper, to which he moved immediately after Lupin's gang burglarised his Villa Marie-Thérèse at Enghien.

nodes.label: Château de Mortepierre (Lovers' Tower fortress)

citations.quote: On the confines of the forest, the little river Ligier, which has dug itself a deep valley at this spot, forms a loop which is overhung by the enormous cliff of Mortepierre.
the donjon was known to the people of the neighbourhood as the Lovers' Tower

nodes.description: Château de Mortepierre in The Crystal Stopper, a fortress on a loop of the river Ligier whose dungeon, known locally as the Lovers' Tower, serves as the Marquis d'Albufex's prison for the kidnapped Daubrecq.

nodes.label: Santé Prison, Paris

citations.quote: it was so clearly understood, both at the Santé Prison and at the Law Courts, that all communication between Lupin and the prisoners must be absolutely prevented

nodes.description: The Santé Prison, Paris, where Gilbert is held awaiting execution in The Crystal Stopper, with all communication between him and Lupin strictly cut off by the prison and court authorities.

nodes.label: Nice hotel (Daubrecq's final lair)

citations.quote: On one of the hills that girdle Nice with the finest scenery in the world, between the Vallon de Saint-Silvestre and the Vallon de La Mantéga, stands a huge hotel which overlooks the town and the wonderful Baie des Anges.
She asked for a bedroom facing south and selected No. 130, on the second floor

nodes.description: A hotel on the hills above Nice between the Vallon de Saint-Silvestre and the surrounding countryside in 'The Crystal Stopper', Daubrecq's final retreat, where Clarisse takes room No. 130 on the second floor to surveil him.

nodes.label: Rue Chateaubriand flat, Paris (Lupin's base as Michel Beaumont)

citations.quote: One of the residences which Lupin occupied at that period and which he used oftener than any of the others was in the Rue Chateaubriand, near the Arc de l'Étoile. He was known there by the name of Michel Beaumont.

nodes.description: A flat in the Rue Chateaubriand, Paris, one of Lupin's most frequently used residences in The Crystal Stopper, where he operates under the alias Michel Beaumont.

nodes.label: Boulevard Arago scaffold site

citations.quote: the execution will take place outside the prison-walls, in the square formed by the Boulevard Arago and the Rue de la Santé.
a shot, a shot fired from the other side, from one of the houses opposite.

nodes.description: The Boulevard Arago scaffold site in Paris in The Crystal Stopper, the public square outside the prison walls where Gilbert's execution is scheduled and from which Lupin fires sniper shots to disrupt it.

nodes.label: unnamed narrator (Lupin's confidant)

citations.quote: Notwithstanding my friendly relations with Lupin and the many flattering proofs of his confidence which he has given me, there is one thing which I have never been quite able to fathom, and that is the organization of his gang.
"Taken all round," said Lupin to me, one day, after telling me the different episodes of the story

nodes.description: The unnamed narrator of 'The Crystal Stopper', Lupin's confidant, who records the story from Lupin's own account long after the events and attests to the trust Lupin placed in him.

nodes.label: Lupin's gang

citations.quote: The existence of the gang is an undoubted fact. Certain adventures can be explained only by countless acts of devotion, invincible efforts of energy and powerful cases of complicity, representing so many forces which all obey one mighty will.

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's criminal gang, whose members include Gilbert, The Growler, The Masher, and Vaucheray, described in The Crystal Stopper as held together by countless acts of devotion.

nodes.label: The Twenty-seven (Canal Company bribery network)

citations.quote: A word will be enough: my husband's name was on the list of the Twenty-seven.
Daubrecq possesses a list of twenty-seven names. To give up any one of those names he is obliged, if he would have his accusation believed, to publish the list itself

nodes.description: The Twenty-seven, the network of twenty-seven French deputies and officials who accepted bribes in the Two-Seas Canal Company scandal and whose names Daubrecq holds on a hidden list as the instrument of his blackmail in The Crystal Stopper.

nodes.label: The Hollow Needle

citations.quote: THE HOLLOW NEEDLE / FURTHER ADVENTURES OF ARSÈNE LUPIN / by Maurice Leblanc

nodes.description: The Hollow Needle, an Arsène Lupin novel by Maurice Leblanc in which Lupin's secret royal treasure hidden inside the hollow sea-stack at Étretat is uncovered by young detective Isidore Beautrelet.

nodes.label: Isidore Beautrelet

citations.quote: My name? Isidore Beautrelet.
sixth-form pupil at the Lycée Janson-de-Sailly

nodes.description: Isidore Beautrelet, a sixth-form pupil at the Lycée Janson-de-Sailly who applies a hypothetico-deductive method to unmask Arsène Lupin and ultimately discover the secret of the Hollow Needle at Étretat.

nodes.label: Raymonde de Saint-Véran

citations.quote: his niece Raymonde de Saint-Véran, whom he had taken to live with him two years before
She put the gun to her shoulder, calmly took aim and fired.

nodes.description: Raymonde de Saint-Véran, the niece of the Comte de Gesvres who had lived at the Château d'Ambrumésy for two years, who fires a shot at the intruders the night of the murder and later becomes the object of Arsène Lupin's obsessive love in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Comte de Gesvres

citations.quote: Here the Comte de Gesvres lived with his daughter Suzanne
the Comte de Gesvres, who had now owned it for some twenty years

nodes.description: Comte de Gesvres, the owner of Château d'Ambrumésy for some twenty years who lives there with his daughter Suzanne, at the scene of the burglary and murder in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Suzanne de Gesvres

citations.quote: his daughter Suzanne, a delicate, fair-haired, pretty creature
the devotion of Mlle. Suzanne de Gesvres, who spent night after night by the wounded lad's bedside

nodes.description: Suzanne de Gesvres, the delicate, fair-haired daughter of the Count of Gesvres, who nurses the wounded Isidore Beautrelet for nights on end in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Jean Daval

citations.quote: Raymonde flung herself down before the other body and recognized Jean Daval, the count's private secretary
Jean Daval was killed by—

nodes.description: Jean Daval, the private secretary of the Comte de Gesvres at the Château d'Ambrumésy, whose dead body is discovered by Raymonde de Saint-Véran during the burglary that opens The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: M. Filleul

citations.quote: M. Filleul remarked: 'If the motive of the crime was theft, this drawing room, at any rate, was not the object of it.'
M. Filleul was a magistrate of the ironic school

nodes.description: M. Filleul, the ironic Examining Magistrate who investigates the crime scene in The Hollow Needle, noting that the drawing room was not the object of the theft.

nodes.label: Brédoux

citations.quote: He had understood: the accomplice was Brédoux, the clerk of the examining magistrate himself.
Brédoux was a queer sort of misshapen creature, who balanced on a pair of very long spindle-legs a huge trunk

nodes.description: Brédoux, a misshapen clerk to the examining magistrate who secretly serves as an accomplice of Arsène Lupin in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Ephraim B. Harlington

citations.quote: Yours faithfully, EPHRAIM B. HARLINGTON.
This name conceals the identity of an excellent fellow, who is secretary to Cooley, the American millionaire

nodes.description: Ephraim B. Harlington, the alias used by the secretary of the American millionaire Cooley, who corresponds with characters in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Louis Valméras

citations.quote: Louis Valméras, 34, Rue du Mont-Thabor
Valméras was Lupin!

nodes.description: Louis Valméras, residing at 34 Rue du Mont-Thabor, who is revealed by Isidore Beautrelet to be none other than Arsène Lupin operating under a false identity in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: M. Massiban

citations.quote: the letter by M. Massiban, of the Academy of Inscriptions and Belles-Lettres
the real Massiban, more real than the real one, with my own ideas, on the lacustrine inscriptions

nodes.description: M. Massiban, a member of the Academy of Inscriptions and Belles-Lettres whose identity and historic book are appropriated by Arsène Lupin as a persona in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Dr. Delattre

citations.quote: Dr. Delattre, the well-known surgeon, was present, with his wife and daughter, at the performance of Hernani at the Comédie Française
I was taken to see a patient whose condition rendered an immediate operation necessary

nodes.description: Dr. Delattre, a well-known surgeon who is abducted and compelled to perform an emergency operation — used by Arsène Lupin for his own purposes — in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Étienne de Vaudreix persona

citations.quote: Arsène Lupin has been living in Paris for twelve months under the name of Étienne de Vaudreix
Étienne de Vaudreix, alias Arsène Lupin, alias myself

nodes.description: The Étienne de Vaudreix persona, under which Arsène Lupin lived in Paris for twelve months in The Hollow Needle before being unmasked.

nodes.label: Baron Anfredi persona

citations.quote: an Italian, to whom my client let it for the summer season: Baron Anfredi
I think, I am sure that your tenant is none other than Arsène Lupin

nodes.description: The Baron Anfredi persona, an Italian identity adopted by Arsène Lupin to rent a property for the summer season in The Hollow Needle, suspected by Isidore Beautrelet to be Lupin himself.

nodes.label: Louis Valméras persona

citations.quote: The man who stood before him, the man whom the brutal force of events compelled him to look upon as Arsène Lupin, was—Valméras!
if I resumed my identity as the Louis Valméras that I had been from a child

nodes.description: The Louis Valméras persona, a childhood identity resumed by Arsène Lupin in The Hollow Needle, so convincing that even when confronted, Beautrelet finds himself forced to accept that the man before him is Valméras.

nodes.label: Massiban impostor persona

citations.quote: Was my make-up as old Massiban so good as all that?
I, up against the window, was tearing out the pages of the historic book

nodes.description: The Massiban impostor persona used by Arsène Lupin in The Hollow Needle to impersonate the Academy scholar and gain possession of the historic book containing the secret of the Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Hollow Needle secret

citations.quote: the problem of the Hollow Needle it was called, by the countless solvers of riddles
The Étretat Needle was hollow!

nodes.description: The Hollow Needle secret, the central mystery of the Lupin novel of the same name, revealing that the sea-stack at Étretat is hollow and has served for centuries as a French royal treasure vault.

nodes.label: Ambrumésy burglary and murder of Jean Daval

citations.quote: Arsène Lupin, surprised in the middle of one of his most daring burglaries, runs away by the path leading to the ruins and drops down shot
the Ambrumésy murder

nodes.description: The Ambrumésy burglary and murder of Jean Daval, the opening crime in The Hollow Needle in which Arsène Lupin commits a daring burglary at the Château d'Ambrumésy and Jean Daval is murdered, drawing Isidore Beautrelet into the investigation.

nodes.label: Rubens paintings substitution scheme

citations.quote: the four Rubenses on that wall are false
a painter called Charpenais. Would you be so good as to examine the pictures and to tell us if you recognize them as genuine?

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's substitution of four counterfeit Rubens canvases for the genuine paintings at Château d'Ambrumésy, executed by a painter named Charpenais, in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Gothic chapel sculptures theft

citations.quote: The chapel has been removed! A whole Gothic chapel collected stone by stone!
a whole population of statues captured and replaced by these chaps in stucco

nodes.description: Arsène Lupin's audacious theft of an entire Gothic chapel at Château d'Ambrumésy, removed stone by stone with its population of statues replaced by stucco replicas, in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Abduction of Raymonde de Saint-Véran

citations.quote: the Château d'Ambrumésy was broken into last night by criminals, who bound and gagged Mlle. de Gesvres and carried off Mlle. de Saint-Véran
desperate, maddened by grief, he takes a terrible resolve. He leaves his lair, prepares his stroke and, on Saturday the sixth of June, assisted by his accomplices, he carries off the girl.

nodes.description: The abduction of Raymonde de Saint-Véran from the Château d'Ambrumésy by Arsène Lupin, driven by desperate grief after her disappearance and his obsessive love for her, described in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Kidnapping of Ganimard and Holmlock Shears

citations.quote: the same newspapers… also announced two further pieces of news: the disappearance of Ganimard, and the kidnapping of Holmlock Shears, in broad daylight, in the heart of London
He kidnaps Ganimard. He kidnaps Holmlock Shears.

nodes.description: The simultaneous kidnapping of Inspector Ganimard and the English detective Holmlock Shears (Herlock Sholmes) by Arsène Lupin, reported in newspaper announcements alongside the Château d'Ambrumésy events in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Lupin wounded and hidden in abbey crypt

citations.quote: he is there and nowhere else that you will find Arsène Lupin!
The corpse lay half-naked, lean, frightful… a motionless form stretched upon the ground.

nodes.description: An episode in 'The Hollow Needle' in which Lupin, shot and half-naked, lies as a motionless form on the floor of an abbey crypt where Beautrelet discovers him hidden.

nodes.label: Beautrelet discovers the Hollow Needle of Étretat

citations.quote: a thread of smoke came filtering through a crevice… a thread of smoke rose in slow spirals in the calm air of the twilight
The Étretat Needle was hollow!

nodes.description: The moment student detective Isidore Beautrelet discovers that the Étretat Needle is hollow, finding a thread of smoke rising from a crevice in the clifftop in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Ganimard's assault on the Hollow Needle

citations.quote: At ten o'clock exactly, they reached the skirt of wall. It was the decisive moment.
Lupin! Surrender! roared Ganimard, with his eyes glittering and his revolver showing through the broken panel of the door.

nodes.description: Inspector Ganimard's armed assault on the Hollow Needle of Étretat in The Hollow Needle, during which he confronts Lupin at gunpoint inside the secret cavern.

nodes.label: Château d'Ambrumésy

citations.quote: once the abbey residence of the priors of Ambrumésy, mutilated under the Revolution, both restored by the Comte de Gesvres
the ruins of Ambrumésy, the famous mediæval monastery

nodes.description: The Château d'Ambrumésy, a famous medieval Norman monastery restored as an aristocratic estate belonging to the Comte de Gesvres, where the burglary, the murder of Jean Daval, and the abduction of Raymonde de Saint-Véran take place at the opening of The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Aiguille d'Étretat (Hollow Needle)

citations.quote: Opposite him, almost level with the cliff, in the open sea rose an enormous rock, over eighty yards high, a colossal obelisk
The Étretat Needle was hollow!

nodes.description: The Aiguille d'Étretat (Hollow Needle), the colossal sea-stack over eighty yards high off the Normandy coast that is revealed in Leblanc's The Hollow Needle to be hollow and to contain a royal treasure vault.

nodes.label: Chambre des Demoiselles

citations.quote: That cave, over there—to the right of the fort. Has it a name?
all the Étretat folk like to call it the Demoiselles. Why, of course—it's the Chambre des Demoiselles.

nodes.description: The Chambre des Demoiselles, a coastal cave at Étretat known to local folk, which conceals the carved D and F letters pointing to the location of the Hollow Needle in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Fort de Fréfossé

citations.quote: Over the pointed door, which was closed with an old rusty lock, he read the words: FORT DE FRÉFOSSÉ
D for Demoiselles, F for Fréfossé: the connection was too remarkable to be a mere accidental fact

nodes.description: Fort de Fréfossé, a ruined clifftop fortification at Étretat in The Hollow Needle, whose initial 'F' paired with 'D for Demoiselles' forms part of Beautrelet's geographic triangulation pointing toward the Needle.

nodes.label: Château de l'Aiguille (on the Creuse)

citations.quote: That's the Château de l'Aiguille, sir.
Louis XIV., foreseeing that the secret might be noised abroad, had built and named that castle so as to offer the quidnuncs a natural explanation of the ancient mystery

nodes.description: Château de l'Aiguille on the Creuse in 'The Hollow Needle', built and named by Louis XIV as a deliberate decoy to mislead seekers of the real Hollow Needle, the ancient royal treasure cache.

nodes.label: Étretat

citations.quote: En aval d'Étretat. La Chambre des Demoiselles. Sous le Fort de Fréfossé. L'Aiguille creuse.
He then went down to Étretat, selected the cheapest hotel

nodes.description: Étretat, the Norman coastal town near the Aiguille Creuse sea-stack, which Isidore Beautrelet investigates to find Arsène Lupin's secret royal treasure refuge in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Lycée Janson-de-Sailly

citations.quote: Sixth-form pupil at the Lycée Janson-de-Sailly
at eight o'clock, returned to the Lycée Janson together with his schoolfellows

nodes.description: The Lycée Janson-de-Sailly in Paris, the sixth-form school attended by Isidore Beautrelet in 'The Hollow Needle', where classmates nickname him the rival of Holmlock Shears.

nodes.label: Hollow Needle secret (royal treasure refuge)

citations.quote: It is a sanctuary and also a stupendous hiding-place. All the treasures of the kings, increasing from century to century
Arsène Lupin gives and bequeaths to France all the treasures contained in the Hollow Needle

nodes.description: The secret of the Aiguille d'Étretat—a hollow sea-stack off the Normandy coast serving as a royal refuge and treasure-house accumulated by French kings over centuries—bequeathed by Arsène Lupin to France at the end of The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: The cryptographic document (dots and figures)

citations.quote: a piece of paper, folded in four… It was covered with figures, dots and signs
The problem of the Hollow Needle it was called, by the countless solvers of riddles who, with their eyes bent upon the figures and dots, strove to read a meaning into them

nodes.description: A folded paper covered in figures, dots, and signs found at Étretat, which encodes the secret of the Hollow Needle and is the key document pursued by Beautrelet and Lupin in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Chauffeur's leather cap

citations.quote: they picked up a chauffeur's cap, in very soft buff leather
The real one, which constituted our only piece of evidence, has gone off on the head of the sham flyman!

nodes.description: A soft buff leather chauffeur's cap found near the Ambrumésy estate in 'The Hollow Needle', the only real piece of physical evidence from the crime scene, lost when it is inadvertently carried away on the head of an impersonator.

nodes.label: Four false Rubens paintings (copies by Charpenais)

citations.quote: the four Rubenses on that wall are false
a painter called Charpenais… The copies which he made, canvases and frames, have taken the place of the four original pictures

nodes.description: Four paintings by the copyist Charpenais hung in place of stolen originals at Ambrumésy in 'The Hollow Needle', their canvases and frames faithfully duplicating the Rubens originals, detected by Beautrelet as fakes.

nodes.label: Marie Antoinette's book of hours

citations.quote: Marie Antoinette's book of hours?… it has been, for the last five years, in a glass case in the Musée Carnavalet
He drew out a sheet folded in two… Under the queen's signature there were—two words, in black ink: ARSÈNE LUPIN.

nodes.description: Marie Antoinette's book of hours, displayed at the Musée Carnavalet, which contains a hidden sheet bearing the queen's signature and the words 'Arsène Lupin', pointing Beautrelet toward the Hollow Needle secret.

nodes.label: The Treatise of the Needle (printed pamphlet, 1679)

citations.quote: The Mystery of the Hollow Needle. The Whole Truth now first exhibited. One hundred copies printed by myself for the instruction of the Court.
It was the very book which Louis XIV. condemned.

nodes.description: A 1679 privately printed pamphlet titled The Mystery of the Hollow Needle in The Hollow Needle, one of a hundred copies condemned by Louis XIV, which reveals the secret of the Étretat Needle to Beautrelet.

nodes.label: D and F letters carved in cave floor

citations.quote: Two huge letters, each perhaps a foot long, appeared cut in relief in the granite of the floor. Those two letters… were a D and an F.
D for Demoiselles, F for Fréfossé: the connection was too remarkable to be a mere accidental fact

nodes.description: The carved letters D and F — standing for Demoiselles and Fréfossé — found in the granite floor of the Chambre des Demoiselles cave, confirming Beautrelet's geographic triangulation of the Hollow Needle's location.

nodes.label: Lupin controls the Hollow Needle as stronghold

citations.quote: From Cæsar to Lupin—and there it ends.
Without the Hollow Needle, Lupin is incomprehensible, a myth, a character in a novel, having no connection with reality.

nodes.description: In The Hollow Needle, Lupin has made the Hollow Needle of Étretat his personal stronghold, the latest in a line of possessors stretching from Julius Caesar through the Kings of France to himself.

nodes.label: Secret of the Needle transmitted through French kings

citations.quote: a secret which was known to the Kings of England, which was lost by them when the crown passed from the poor fool, Henry VI., to the Duke of York, which was revealed to Charles VII., King of France, by Joan of Arc
JULIUS CÆSAR / CHARLEMAGNE / ROLLO / WILLIAM THE CONQUEROR / RICHARD CŒUR-DE-LEON / LOUIS XI. / FRANCIS I. / HENRY IV. / LOUIS XIV. / ARSÈNE LUPIN

nodes.description: The secret of the Hollow Needle was transmitted in succession through the Kings of England and then of France — from Julius Caesar through Charlemagne, William the Conqueror, Richard Coeur-de-Lion, Louis XI, Francis I, Henry IV, and Napoleon — until it reached Lupin.

nodes.label: Beautrelet's hypothetico-deductive method

citations.quote: I think first, I try, above all, to get the general hang of the case… Then I imagine a reasonable and logical hypothesis, which fits in with the general idea. And then, and not before, I examine the facts to see if they agree with my hypothesis.
Facts very seldom fail to carry their own explanation!

nodes.description: Beautrelet's hypothetico-deductive method in The Hollow Needle: first grasp the general hang of a case, then form a reasonable hypothesis, then test it without mercy against every available fact.

nodes.label: Vowel-substitution cipher decryption of the document

citations.quote: not one of those figures is higher than the figure 5. There is, therefore, a great chance that each of these figures represents one of the five vowels, taken in alphabetical order
En aval d'Étretat. La Chambre des Demoiselles. Sous le Fort de Fréfossé. L'Aiguille creuse.

nodes.description: Beautrelet's vowel-substitution decryption of the Étretat cryptogram — noting that no figure exceeds 5 — to produce the French text directing searchers to the Chambre des Demoiselles and the Hollow Needle in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Beautrelet's Cauchois geographic triangulation

citations.quote: Rouen, Dieppe, the Havre: the three angles of the triangle, the three large towns that occupy the three points. In the centre, the Caux country.
Each one of them, without exception, had happened within the boundaries of the old kingdom of Neustria

nodes.description: Beautrelet's triangulation placing the Hollow Needle in the old kingdom of Neustria using Rouen, Dieppe, and Le Havre as the three points of an enclosing triangle, narrowing the search to the Étretat coast in The Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: Lupin's love for Raymonde de Saint-Véran

citations.quote: Yes, Mlle. de Saint-Véran is alive. Yes, I love her. Yes, I have the mortification not to be loved by her.
Lupin becomes enamored of his nurse. Gratitude turns to love, admiration to passion.

nodes.description: Lupin's love for Raymonde de Saint-Véran in 'The Hollow Needle', which he openly admits while acknowledging the mortification of not being loved in return, having fallen for her during his convalescence under her nursing care.

nodes.label: Jean Daval's greed / love affair motive

citations.quote: It was for this woman, whom he got to know two years ago, and to supply her constant need of money that Daval turned thief.
a certain Mme. Verdier. It was for this woman… that Daval turned thief

nodes.description: Jean Daval's motive for turning thief in 'The Hollow Needle': financial greed and a love affair with Madame Verdier, whose constant need for money drove him to steal from the Ambrumésy estate.

nodes.label: Beautrelet as amateur detective / rival of Holmlock Shears

citations.quote: his schoolfellows, who called him the rival of Holmlock Shears
he, alone, had done everything. To him alone the merit of the victory was due.

nodes.description: Isidore Beautrelet's role in 'The Hollow Needle' as a schoolboy amateur detective whose classmates call him the rival of Holmlock Shears and who single-handedly tracks Arsène Lupin to the Hollow Needle.

nodes.label: John Thorndyke's Cases

citations.quote: JOHN THORNDYKE'S CASES / RELATED BY CHRISTOPHER JERVIS, M.D. / AND EDITED BY R. AUSTIN FREEMAN
The stories in this collection, inasmuch as they constitute a somewhat new departure in this class of literature, require a few words of introduction.

nodes.description: John Thorndyke's Cases, a short-story collection by R. Austin Freeman narrated by Dr. Christopher Jervis, in which Dr. Thorndyke solves crimes through forensic science and microscopic analysis.

nodes.label: R. Austin Freeman

citations.quote: AND EDITED BY R. AUSTIN FREEMAN / AUTHOR OF "THE GOLDEN POOL," ETC.
I have been scrupulous in confining myself to authentic facts and practicable methods. R. A. F. Gravesend, September 21, 1909.
By R. AUSTIN FREEMAN
THE VANISHING MAN / A Detective Romance / BY R. AUSTIN FREEMAN

nodes.description: R. Austin Freeman, the British author who created Dr. John Thorndyke and pioneered the forensic detective story, authoring John Thorndyke's Cases, The Red Thumb Mark, The Vanishing Man, and The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke.

nodes.label: Dr. Thorndyke saga

citations.quote: my friend John Thorndyke, the well-known medico-legal expert
The peculiar construction of the first four stories in the present collection will probably strike both reader and critic
John Thorndyke was not only an enthusiast, a man of profound learning and great reputation, but he was an exceptional teacher

nodes.description: The Dr. Thorndyke saga, a series of medico-legal mystery fiction featuring Dr. John Thorndyke, including John Thorndyke's Cases, The Red Thumb Mark, and The Vanishing Man, by R. Austin Freeman.

nodes.label: Dr. Christopher Jervis
nodes.source_location: Title page / The Man with the Nailed Shoes

citations.quote: RELATED BY CHRISTOPHER JERVIS, M.D.

citations.section: Title page / The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: "You certainly don't seem to be overworked, Jervis," he remarked

citations.section: Title page / The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: I had been assured by my medical agent, Mr. Turcival, that I should find the practice of which I was now taking charge 'an exceedingly soft billet'

citations.section: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
citations.quote: (_Related by Christopher Jervis, M.D._)

citations.section: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
citations.quote: An excellent suggestion, Jervis, answered Thorndyke.

nodes.description: Dr. Christopher Jervis, a medical doctor and Dr. Thorndyke's companion and narrative voice in John Thorndyke's Cases and other Freeman works, who assists Thorndyke in his forensic investigations.

nodes.label: Polton
nodes.source_location: The Man with the Nailed Shoes

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: the most surprising visitor was Thorndyke's laboratory assistant, Polton, who appeared one evening with a large trunk and a sailor's hammock

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: "Will you please prepare a Marsh's apparatus, Polton," said Thorndyke. "I have a couple ready, sir," replied Polton.

citations.section: THE ECHO OF A MUTINY, Part II
citations.quote: Thorndyke's laboratory assistant, Polton, was a fine example of the latter type, deft, resourceful, ingenious and untiring.

citations.section: THE ECHO OF A MUTINY, Part II
citations.quote: Though by trade a watchmaker, Polton was, by choice, an optician.
Thorndyke surveyed the tea-tray thoughtfully and then looked at his retainer. 'I see you have put three tea-cups, Polton,' he said.
The little man smiled a quaint, crinkly smile of gratification as he explained: 'I happened to look out of the laboratory window as you turned the corner, sir.'

evidence_refs: corpus/dr-thorndyke/the-adventures-of-dr-thorndyke/text.txt#THE ECHO OF A MUTINY, Part II
nodes.description: Polton, Dr. Thorndyke's deft and resourceful laboratory assistant who appears across John Thorndyke's Cases and other Freeman works, maintaining and operating the scientific apparatus such as Marsh's arsenic-detection device.

nodes.label: Jervis narrator role

citations.quote: RELATED BY CHRISTOPHER JERVIS, M.D.
these events befell in the days before I had joined him as his assistant, and his special knowledge and powers of inference were not then fully appreciated by me.

citations.section: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
citations.quote: (_Related by Christopher Jervis, M.D._)

nodes.description: Dr. Christopher Jervis's narrator role, in which he relates the cases of Dr. John Thorndyke in John Thorndyke's Cases and The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke, serving as Thorndyke's assistant and chronicler.

nodes.label: Footprint analysis and reading
nodes.source_location: The Man with the Nailed Shoes

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: The subject of footprints has always interested me deeply for two reasons. First, the evidence furnished by footprints is constantly being brought forward, and is often of cardinal importance; and, secondly, the whole subject is capable of really systematic and scientific treatment. In the main the data are anatomical, but age, sex, occupation, health, and disease all give their various indications.

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: Note their enormous size. They should be the footprints of a giant. But the length of the stride shows that they were made by a rather short man.

nodes.description: A forensic method used by Dr. Thorndyke in 'The Man with the Nailed Shoes' to determine identity and gait from footprint evidence, noting that enormous-looking prints were made by a person of normal stride.

nodes.label: Plaster-of-Paris footprint casting
nodes.source_location: The Man with the Nailed Shoes

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: that same night I cycled down to the shore, through the Gap, with a supply of plaster of Paris, and proceeded to take plaster moulds of the more important of the footprints. As I thought that liquid plaster might confuse or even obliterate the prints in sand, I filled up the respective footprints with dry plaster, pressed it down lightly, and then cautiously poured water on to it. The moulds, which are excellent impressions, of course show the appearance of the boots which made the footprints, and from these moulds I have prepared casts which reproduce the footprints themselves.

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: A mould is a direct, and therefore reversed, impression. A cast is the impression of a mould, and therefore a facsimile of the object.

nodes.description: Plaster-of-Paris footprint casting technique used by Dr. Thorndyke in The Man with the Nailed Shoes to take direct impressions from the shore and produce cast facsimiles as evidence.

nodes.label: Transparent photograph superposition comparison
nodes.source_location: The Man with the Nailed Shoes

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: if we put the photograph of the mould of the prisoner's right shoe over that of the murderer's right shoe, and hold the two superposed photographs up to the light, we cannot make the two pictures coincide.

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: the most conclusive fact of all--from which there is no possible escape--is that the number of nails in the two shoes is not the same. In the sole of the prisoner's right shoe there are forty nails; in that of the murderer there are forty-one.

nodes.description: Dr. Thorndyke's forensic technique in 'The Man with the Nailed Shoes' of superimposing photographic transparencies of shoe moulds to show that the number and pattern of nails in two shoes are identical, proving the same person made both sets of prints.

nodes.label: Thorough post-mortem examination
nodes.source_location: The Man with the Nailed Shoes

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: Dr. Thorndyke insisted on weighing the body, and examining every organ--lungs, liver, stomach, and brain--yes, actually the brain!--as if there had been no clue whatever to the cause of death.

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: It is not our function to confirm an hypothesis suggested by outside circumstances, but rather, on the contrary, to make certain that no other explanation is possible. And that is my invariable practice. No matter how glaringly obvious the appearances may be, I refuse to take anything for granted.

nodes.description: A thorough post-mortem examination conducted by Dr. Thorndyke in The Man with the Nailed Shoes, weighing the body and examining all organs to avoid confirming a predetermined hypothesis.

nodes.label: Microscopic analysis of hair and dust
nodes.source_location: The Stranger's Latchkey / The Anthropologist at Large / A Message from the Deep Sea

citations.section: The Stranger's Latchkey / The Anthropologist at Large / A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: he produced a small, portable microscope and some glass slides and cover-slips, and having opened the paper and tipped the ball of fluff from the key-barrel on to a slide, set to work with a pair of mounted needles to tease it out into its component parts.

citations.section: The Stranger's Latchkey / The Anthropologist at Large / A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: The hairs proved on measurement to have the surprisingly large diameter of 1/135 of an inch--fully double that of ordinary hairs, although they were unquestionably human.

citations.section: The Stranger's Latchkey / The Anthropologist at Large / A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: You will have, Jervis, to study the minute properties of dust and dirt. Their evidential value is immense.

evidence_refs: corpus/dr-thorndyke/john-thorndyke-s-cases/text.txt#The Stranger's Latchkey / The Anthropologist at Large / A Message from the Deep Sea
nodes.description: Microscopic analysis of hair and dust used by Dr. Thorndyke to identify unusually thick hairs and trace evidential properties of dust, as demonstrated in the Freddy Haldean abduction case.

nodes.label: Dust extraction and powder analysis

citations.quote: Now, Polton, we have here a specimen for examination, and time is precious. First of all, we want your patent dust-extractor.

The little man bustled to a cupboard and brought forth a singular appliance, of his own manufacture, somewhat like a miniature vacuum cleaner. It had been made from a bicycle foot-pump, by reversing the piston-valve.

nodes.description: A forensic technique employed by Dr. Thorndyke's assistant Polton in 'The Anthropologist at Large' using a patent dust-extractor of his own manufacture to collect and analyse powder specimens for evidence.

nodes.label: Skull shape anthropometry (cephalic index)

citations.quote: a pair of large callipers, which he applied to the inside of the hat; "six inches and nine-tenths long by six and six-tenths broad, which gives us"--he made a rapid calculation--"the extraordinarily high cephalic index of 95.6."
in the black races, long skull, long orbits, flat hair; in the white races, oval skull, oval orbits, oval hair; and in the yellow races, round skull, round orbits, round hair.

nodes.description: Skull shape anthropometry using callipers to measure cephalic index, employed by Dr. Thorndyke in The Anthropologist at Large to identify the racial origin of a hat's owner in the Isaac Löwe Japanese art robbery case.

nodes.label: Transverse cross-section of hair under microscope
nodes.source_location: The Anthropologist at Large / A Message from the Deep Sea

citations.section: The Anthropologist at Large / A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: Here is a transverse section of his hair, which is quite circular--unlike yours or mine, which would be oval.

citations.section: The Anthropologist at Large / A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: The hairs of which that little tress was composed had not been pulled out at all. They had fallen out spontaneously. They were, in fact, shed hairs--probably combings. Let me explain the difference. When a hair is shed naturally, it drops out of the little tube in the skin called the root sheath... When a hair is forcibly pulled out, its root drags out the root sheath with it.

evidence_refs: corpus/dr-thorndyke/john-thorndyke-s-cases/text.txt#The Anthropologist at Large / A Message from the Deep Sea
nodes.description: Thorndyke's transverse microscopic cross-section of hair fibres to distinguish racial types by the circular or oval shape of the section, applied in the Isaac Löwe Japanese art robbery and the Minna Adler murder cases.

nodes.label: Fingerprint powder (insufflator) development

citations.section: The Blue Sequin / The Aluminium Dagger / A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: he produced from his pocket a small insufflator or powder-blower, with which he blew a stream of impalpable smoke-like powder on to the edges of the middle window

citations.section: The Blue Sequin / The Aluminium Dagger / A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: he took from his bag a long, registering chemical thermometer and an insufflator or powder-blower... with the latter blew a stream of fine yellow powder on to the black leather handle of the dagger.

citations.section: The Blue Sequin / The Aluminium Dagger / A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: I took them home and carefully dusted each on both sides with a certain surgical dusting-powder. The powder adhered to the places where his fingers and thumbs had pressed against the photographs, showing the fingerprints very distinctly.

evidence_refs: corpus/dr-thorndyke/john-thorndyke-s-cases/text.txt#The Blue Sequin / The Aluminium Dagger / A Message from the Deep Sea
nodes.description: Fingerprint development using a powder insufflator, employed by Dr. Thorndyke in multiple cases including the Edith Grant death investigation, to reveal latent prints on surfaces.

nodes.label: Marsh's apparatus arsenic test

citations.quote: "Will you please prepare a Marsh's apparatus, Polton," said Thorndyke. "I have a couple ready, sir," replied Polton. "Then pour the acid into one and bring it to me, with a tile."
he dropped a few drops of the hypochlorite solution on to the tile, and immediately the black stain faded away and vanished.

nodes.description: Marsh's apparatus arsenic test, a forensic chemistry technique used by Dr. Thorndyke in The Moabite Cipher to detect arsenic via a black stain that fades when treated with hypochlorite solution.

nodes.label: Water-revealed secret writing (artificial palimpsest)

citations.quote: He filled a tumbler with water, and, rolling up the document, dropped it in. Immediately there began to appear on it a new set of characters of a curious grey colour.

"When this dries," said Thorndyke, "the writing will completely disappear, but it will reappear whenever the paper is again wetted." The Professor nodded. "Very ingenious," said he--"a sort of artificial palimpsest, in fact."

nodes.description: A technique used by Dr. Thorndyke in The Moabite Cipher, revealing hidden writing on a document by immersing it in water, causing the secret message to appear and then vanish again on drying.

nodes.label: Microscopic identification of deep-sea Foraminifera sand
nodes.source_location: A Message from the Deep Sea

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: It is a message to us from the deep sea, Jervis; from the floor of the Eastern Mediterranean.

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: Sand of this kind is contained in considerable quantities in Turkey sponges. The warehouses in which the sponges are unpacked are often strewn with it ankle deep; the men who unpack the cases become dusted over with it.

nodes.description: Thorndyke's identification of deep-sea foraminifera sand from the Eastern Mediterranean floor, traced to Turkey-sponge warehouses, as a clue localising a suspect in the Minna Adler murder in A Message from the Deep Sea.

nodes.label: Fingerprint casting from candle-wax impressions
nodes.source_location: A Message from the Deep Sea

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: the warm, soft wax had taken beautifully clear impressions of a right thumb and forefinger. I took three moulds of the candle-end in moulding wax, and from these moulds have made this cement cast, which shows both the fingerprints and the marks of the candlestick.

nodes.description: Fingerprint casting from candle-wax impressions used by Dr. Thorndyke in A Message from the Deep Sea, taking three moulds from clear right thumb and forefinger impressions left in warm wax.

nodes.label: Ballistic analysis of dagger fired from Chassepôt rifle

citations.quote: He lifted the dagger by its point, slipped the hilt into the muzzle. When he let go, the dagger slid quietly down the barrel, until its hilt appeared in the open breech.

on the original dagger there are linear scratches which exactly correspond with the grooves of the rifling. Then there is the fact that the dagger was certainly spinning from left to right--in the direction of the rifling, that is--when it entered the body.

nodes.description: Thorndyke's demonstration that an aluminium dagger was fired from a Chassepôt rifle by matching the linear rifling scratches on its hilt to the grooves of the barrel, solving the murder of Alfred Hartridge in The Aluminium Dagger.

Leonard Wolfe was arrested at Eltham, in the very act of burying in his garden a large and powerful compressed-air rifle.

nodes.description: Leonard Wolfe (alias Thomas Barlow), the sole beneficiary of Alfred Hartridge's £20,000 estate in The Aluminium Dagger, who was arrested at Eltham while burying in his garden the large compressed-air rifle used in the remote-control murder.

citations.quote: At the end of the passage was a half-open door, considerably battered and bruised. Above the door, painted in white lettering, was the inscription, 'Mr. Hartridge'; and through the doorway protruded the rather foxy countenance of Inspector Badger.
'Hallo, sir!' said he. 'You can't come here.' ... 'I know exactly what your business is, Inspector Badger.'
Inspector Badger burst into the room in a highly excited state. 'What is all this, Doctor Thorndyke?'

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: the hair in the dead woman's hand was not the hair of the murderer. It was placed in the hand of the corpse for an obvious purpose; and the fact that the murderer had brought it with him shows that the crime was premeditated.

nodes.description: Paul Petrofsky's premeditated murder of Minna Adler, in which he planted a shed hair tress in the dead woman's hand to mislead investigators, as resolved by Dr. Thorndyke in A Message from the Deep Sea.

nodes.label: Nailed shoes (diamond-and-cross pattern)
nodes.source_location: The Man with the Nailed Shoes

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: They were made by shoes armed with smallish hob-nails, which were arranged in a diamond-shaped pattern on the soles and in a cross on the heels.

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: The prisoner's shoes were not the shoes that made those footprints... In the sole of the prisoner's right shoe there are forty nails; in that of the murderer there are forty-one.

nodes.description: A pair of shoes with hob-nails arranged in a diamond-and-cross pattern in 'The Man with the Nailed Shoes', the key forensic object linking a murderer to footprints at the crime scene through Thorndyke's photographic superposition technique.

nodes.label: Aluminium-hilted dagger (TRADITORE inscription)

citations.quote: A very singular weapon, this... Singular both in shape and material. I have never seen an aluminium hilt before, and bookbinder's morocco is a little unusual.
the inspector pointed, as he spoke, to the metal guard of the dagger, on which was engraved, in clumsy lettering, the single word, 'TRADITORE.'

nodes.description: A singular weapon in 'The Aluminium Dagger' with an aluminium hilt—a material never previously seen by the investigators—engraved with the word TRADITORE, linked to the remote-rifle murder of Alfred Hartridge by Leonard Wolfe.

nodes.label: Moabite cipher document

citations.quote: It consisted of a single half-sheet of thin notepaper, both sides of which were covered with strange, crabbed characters, written with a brownish-black ink in continuous lines, without any spaces to indicate the divisions into words
The character is the Moabite or Phoenician--primitive Semitic, in fact--and reads from right to left.

nodes.description: A half-sheet of notepaper covered on both sides with Moabite or Phoenician characters reading from right to left in 'The Moabite Cipher', used to encode instructions for a Piccadilly jewel theft, deciphered by Dr. Thorndyke.

nodes.label: Mandarin's Pearl (ebony pendant with pear-shaped pearl)

citations.quote: It consisted of an oblong block of ebony in which was set a single pear-shaped pearl more than three-quarters of an inch long. The sides of the ebony block were lacquered--probably to conceal a joint--and bore a number of Chinese characters
the jeweller pronounced the pearl to be undoubtedly genuine, and worth anything up to a thousand pounds.

nodes.description: An ebony pendant set with a single pear-shaped pearl over three-quarters of an inch long in 'The Mandarin's Pearl', pronounced genuine and worth up to a thousand pounds, which is the object of Alfred Calverley and Raggerton's conspiracy.

nodes.label: Blue sequin from victim's hat

citations.quote: He drew from his pocket a folded paper, which he opened, displaying a tiny blue disc. "It is one of the sequins with which her hat was trimmed, and I have in this envelope several more which I took from the hat itself. This single sequin I picked up on the rear end of the off side footboard."
Embedded in it was a blue sequin. [in the steer's horn blood-stain]

nodes.description: A tiny blue sequin from Edith Grant's hat recovered from the bloodstained tip of a steer's horn in 'The Blue Sequin', constituting the key physical evidence that Dr. Thorndyke uses to reconstruct the cause of her death.

nodes.label: Steer's left horn bearing brain tissue, blood, and sequin

citations.quote: One of the steers--this one--had thrust its long horn out through the bars. The point of that horn struck the deceased's head, driving her face violently against the corner of the window.
He turned the horn over, and pointed to a small patch of dried blood. Embedded in it was a blue sequin.

nodes.description: The left horn of a steer in 'The Blue Sequin' that accidentally struck and killed Edith Grant, bearing dried blood, brain tissue, and an embedded blue sequin from her hat—the physical evidence reconstructed by Dr. Thorndyke.

nodes.label: Deep-sea Foraminifera sand on pillow
nodes.source_location: A Message from the Deep Sea

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: It is a message to us from the deep sea, Jervis; from the floor of the Eastern Mediterranean.

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: Sand of this kind is contained in considerable quantities in Turkey sponges... as one of these happened to belong to a species which is found only in the Levant, I was able to fix the locality.

nodes.description: Deep-sea Foraminifera sand from the Eastern Mediterranean found on a victim's pillow in A Message from the Deep Sea, traced to Turkey sponges to identify the murderer's trade.

nodes.label: Shed hair tress planted in dead woman's hand
nodes.source_location: A Message from the Deep Sea

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: The hairs of which that little tress was composed had not been pulled out at all. They had fallen out spontaneously. They were, in fact, shed hairs--probably combings.

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: the lock of hair which lay in the dead woman's hand had roots at both ends, and so could not have been torn from the head of the murderer.

nodes.description: A tress of shed hair with roots at both ends placed in Minna Adler's dead hand in A Message from the Deep Sea, proven by Thorndyke to be planted combings rather than hair torn from the murderer.

nodes.label: Metal washer bearing rifling marks (dagger rifle)

citations.quote: Here is such a washer, which Polton has made for us... a soft metal washer, which fitted on to this hexagon, and which would be pressed into the grooves of the rifling, and so spin the dagger
The two discs were identical in size, and the margin of each was indented with identical markings, corresponding to the rifling of the barrel.

nodes.description: A soft-metal washer bearing the rifling marks of the aluminium dagger's barrel, crafted by Polton and used by Thorndyke to prove the dagger was also a firearm in The Aluminium Dagger.

nodes.label: Morphia poison in Hearn's stomach
nodes.source_location: The Man with the Nailed Shoes

citations.quote: I came to the conclusion that death was occasioned by an overdose of morphia.

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: the professor is here, your Worship, and is prepared to swear to having obtained over one grain of morphia from the contents of the stomach

nodes.description: Morphia found in Charles Hearn's stomach at post-mortem, confirmed by Thorndyke in The Man with the Nailed Shoes as the cause of death, with the poison traced to a local pharmacist.

nodes.label: Little Sundersley (East Coast England)
nodes.source_location: The Man with the Nailed Shoes

nodes.description: Little Sundersley, a particularly remote village on the East Coast of England in The Man with the Nailed Shoes in John Thorndyke's Cases, the isolated setting of Thorndyke's morphia poisoning investigation.

nodes.label: Woldhurst (branch line, Kent)

citations.quote: A shocking crime was discovered yesterday morning at the little town of Woldhurst, which lies on the branch line from Halbury Junction.
It would be about here that the murder was committed... at least, between here and Woldhurst.

nodes.description: Woldhurst, a small Kent town on a branch railway line in The Blue Sequin in John Thorndyke's Cases, where Edith Grant's death is initially treated as murder by Stopford before Thorndyke proves it was a cattle-truck accident.

citations.section: The Moabite Cipher / The Aluminium Dagger
citations.quote: stole noiselessly in their rubber-soled boots, down the stone stairs and along the pavement

nodes.description: The Inner Temple, London, location of Thorndyke's chambers, whose soft-voiced bell opens several of his cases including The Aluminium Dagger in John Thorndyke's Cases.

nodes.label: Whitechapel / Aldgate (scene of Minna Adler murder)
nodes.source_location: A Message from the Deep Sea

citations.quote: The Whitechapel Road, though redeemed by scattered relics of a more picturesque past

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea

nodes.description: The Whitechapel Road area of East London in A Message from the Deep Sea in John Thorndyke's Cases, the neighbourhood where Minna Adler is murdered near the London Hospital.

nodes.label: Silencing potential witness / informant (Jezzard gang vs Hearn)
nodes.source_location: The Man with the Nailed Shoes

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: 'Now I am ready to clean off my own slate and set you free at the same time; but I must have some means of livelihood in view.' 'You mean that you will turn King's evidence?'

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: a suppressed sneeze from the other side of the hedge... Immediately hurried footsteps were heard in the lane outside the hedge.

nodes.description: The motive of the Jezzard gang in 'The Man with the Nailed Shoes': silencing Hearn as a potential informant by staging his murder as an accident, in exchange for Hearn's promise of a share of future criminal proceeds.

nodes.label: Inheritance of Hartridge estate (Wolfe)

citations.quote: There is only one person who benefits by the death of Alfred Hartridge... His name is Leonard Wolfe... he inherits the entire estate--about twenty thousand pounds.

Leonard Wolfe and the deceased, Alfred Hartridge, entered into an agreement... Wolfe was to marry Hester Greene, and in consideration of this service Alfred Hartridge was to assign to Wolfe the whole of his property.

nodes.description: Leonard Wolfe's motive in 'The Aluminium Dagger': under an agreement with Alfred Hartridge, Wolfe was to marry Hester Greene and inherit the Hartridge estate, making Hartridge's death the only way to secure the inheritance.

nodes.label: Jealousy / love rival (Petrofsky vs Minna Adler)
nodes.source_location: A Message from the Deep Sea

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: Moses broke off with Miriam, and engaged himself to Minna. Then Miriam was furious

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: it was just after that that the trouble came... Miriam is a very passionate girl, and she was furiously jealous of Minna

nodes.description: The motive behind Paul Petrofsky's murder of Minna Adler in 'A Message from the Deep Sea': Miriam's furious jealousy after Moses broke off his engagement to her in favour of Minna, a passion so intense it led to violence.

nodes.label: Hearn's chest wound inflicted post-mortem (morphia death preceded stabbing)
nodes.source_location: The Man with the Nailed Shoes

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: when that wound was inflicted the deceased had already been dead from a quarter to half an hour.

citations.section: The Man with the Nailed Shoes
citations.quote: a wound on the living body becomes filled with blood... But the wound on the deceased contained only a little blood-clot. There was hardly any blood on the clothing.

nodes.description: The forensic finding that the chest wound inflicted on Hearn was post-mortem — the blood-clot and absence of vital reaction proving he had already been dead from morphia for a quarter to half an hour before being stabbed, in The Man with the Nailed Shoes.

nodes.label: Edith Grant killed by steer horn projecting from cattle truck, not by Harold Stopford

citations.quote: This hypothesis is inherently probable, it fits all the facts, and those facts admit of no other explanation.
I can now prove when and where and how Miss Grant met her death.

nodes.description: In The Blue Sequin, Thorndyke demonstrates that Edith Grant was accidentally killed by a steer horn projecting from a passing cattle truck, the only hypothesis consistent with all the physical evidence, exonerating Harold Stopford of murder.

nodes.label: Planted shed hair in Minna Adler's hand (combings, not torn from murderer)
nodes.source_location: A Message from the Deep Sea

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: the hand was perfectly rigid, but it did not grasp the hair at all. The little tress lay in the palm quite loosely and the hand was only partially closed. Obviously the hair had been placed in it after death.

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: the lock of hair which lay in the dead woman's hand had roots at both ends, and so could not have been torn from the head of the murderer.

nodes.description: Thorndyke proves in A Message from the Deep Sea that the tress of hair in Minna Adler's hand was planted after death — it lay loosely in a rigid palm and had roots at both ends, indicating fallen combings rather than hair torn from a killer.

nodes.label: The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke (The Singing Bone)

nodes.label: The Case of Oscar Brodski

nodes.description: The Case of Oscar Brodski, the opening story of The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke and Freeman's experiment in the inverted detective narrative, in which Thorndyke reconstructs the murder of the diamond merchant Oscar Brodski by Silas Hickler.

nodes.label: A Case of Premeditation

citations.quote: II   A CASE OF PREMEDITATION

nodes.description: A Case of Premeditation, a Dr. Thorndyke story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which Thorndyke investigates the carefully planned knife-murder of the blackmailer ex-warder Pratt by Rufus Pembury, who used General O'Gorman's bloodhounds as a deliberate blind.

nodes.label: The Echo of a Mutiny

citations.quote: III   THE ECHO OF A MUTINY

nodes.description: The Echo of a Mutiny, a Dr. Thorndyke story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which Thorndyke uses microscopic identification of mole hair, acorn barnacle, and serpula deposits to prove that Tom Jeffreys murdered the lighthouse-keeper Amos Todd at the Girdler Sand.

nodes.label: A Wastrel's Romance

citations.quote: IV   A WASTREL'S ROMANCE

nodes.description: A Wastrel's Romance, a Dr. Thorndyke story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which Thorndyke uses microscopic analysis of coat dust to identify the location of a chloroform attack on Mrs. Chater at Willowdale.

nodes.label: The Old Lag

citations.quote: V   THE OLD LAG

nodes.description: The Old Lag, a Dr. Thorndyke story in The Adventures of Dr. Thorndyke in which Thorndyke proves Frank Belfield's innocence in the murder of the fence Caldwell by exposing Joseph Woodthorpe's scheme using forged fingerprint stamps and camouflaged evidence.

citations.quote: those prints were not made accidentally, but--as you ingeniously suggested just now--were put there for a purpose.

nodes.description: Joseph Woodthorpe's murder of Caldwell in Camberwell and his elaborate scheme of forging Frank Belfield's fingerprints from photographs of official prison finger-print forms to frame him for the crime, exposed by Dr. Thorndyke in The Old Lag.

nodes.label: Blood-flow direction inference to determine position of victim
nodes.source_location: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II

citations.section: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
citations.quote: if the blood ran down the face towards the chin, the face must have been upright at the time; and if the blood trickled from the front to the back of the head, the head must have been horizontal and face upwards. But the man when he was seen by the engine driver, was lying face downwards.

citations.section: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
citations.quote: The only possible inference is that when the wound was inflicted, the man was in the upright position--standing or sitting

evidence_refs: corpus/dr-thorndyke/the-adventures-of-dr-thorndyke/text.txt#THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
nodes.description: Thorndyke's inference that Oscar Brodski was in the upright position when killed — from the blood having run down toward the chin — proving the murder occurred while he sat or stood, not lying on a railway track, in The Case of Oscar Brodski.

nodes.label: Microscopic analysis of textile fibres to link crime scene to murder location
nodes.source_location: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II

citations.section: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
citations.quote: The object was a little tuft of some textile fabric. Under the microscope I found it to consist of several different fibres, differently dyed. The bulk of it consisted of wool fibres dyed crimson, but there were also cotton fibres dyed blue and a few which looked like jute, dyed yellow.

citations.section: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
citations.quote: Here are our old acquaintances, the red wool fibres, the blue cotton and the yellow jute.

evidence_refs: corpus/dr-thorndyke/the-adventures-of-dr-thorndyke/text.txt#THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
nodes.description: Microscopic analysis of textile fibres — including red wool, blue cotton, and yellow jute — used by Dr. Thorndyke in The Case of Oscar Brodski to link a crime scene to the murder location.

nodes.label: Reconstruction of broken spectacle lenses to detect planted glass
nodes.source_location: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II

citations.section: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
citations.quote: There's too much glass. We have almost completely built up the broken lenses, and the fragments that are left over are considerably more than are required to fill up the gaps.

citations.section: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
citations.quote: there are only one or two fragments here that belong to the spectacles... The remainder consists of a soft, uneven, moulded glass, easily distinguished from the clear, hard optical glass.

evidence_refs: corpus/dr-thorndyke/the-adventures-of-dr-thorndyke/text.txt#THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
nodes.description: Thorndyke's reconstruction of Oscar Brodski's broken spectacle lenses to show that the surplus glass fragments are from a soft, separately planted source — proving the murder scene had been staged in The Case of Oscar Brodski.

nodes.label: Examination of boot soles to determine whether victim walked to crime scene
nodes.source_location: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II

citations.section: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
citations.quote: I examined them most minutely and could find no trace of sand, gravel or earth, in spite of the fact that he must have crossed fields and rough land to reach the place where he was found. What I did find was fine tobacco ash, a charred mark as if a cigar or cigarette had been trodden on, several crumbs of biscuit, and, on a projecting brad, some coloured fibres, apparently from a carpet.

citations.section: THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
citations.quote: The manifest suggestion is that the man was killed in a house with a carpeted floor, and carried from thence to the railway.

evidence_refs: corpus/dr-thorndyke/the-adventures-of-dr-thorndyke/text.txt#THE CASE OF OSCAR BRODSKI, Part II
nodes.description: Thorndyke's examination of Oscar Brodski's boot soles showing no sand or earth, proving he was killed indoors on a carpeted floor and carried to the railway rather than having walked there himself, in The Case of Oscar Brodski.

nodes.label: Thorndyke's critique of bloodhound evidence and musk-scent trail analysis
nodes.source_location: A CASE OF PREMEDITATION, Part II

citations.section: A CASE OF PREMEDITATION, Part II
citations.quote: the entire system of using bloodhounds for criminal detection is based on a fallacy... They may discover such identity, but they cannot communicate their knowledge.

citations.section: A CASE OF PREMEDITATION, Part II
citations.quote: the knife smelled quite distinctly... the distinct trail leading straight to Ellis, who is, nevertheless, found to be without a scratch or a spot of blood

nodes.description: Thorndyke's argument that bloodhound tracking is logically flawed because it proves only olfactory similarity between people, not identity, demonstrated when a planted musk scent leads the dog to an innocent man in A Case of Premeditation.
