nodes.label: The Screen scene of School for Scandal gave Mrs. Mandeville her alibi window

citations.quote: 'Because the play was The School for Scandal, and that particular act of The School for Scandal. I should like to remind you, as I said just now, that she always arranged the furniture how she liked... when you say that witnesses could attest to having seen all the performers on the stage, I should like to remind you that in the principal scene of The School for Scandal one of the principal performers remains for a considerable time on the stage, but is not seen.'

nodes.description: In The Actor and the Alibi, Father Brown realises that the School for Scandal's screen scene was the specific act that created Mrs Mandeville's alibi window, its well-known stage business fixing the critical timing.

nodes.label: Young Musgrave exposed by his ability to jump the moat (an old man cannot)
nodes.source_location: The Worst Crime in the World

citations.section: The Worst Crime in the World
citations.quote: An old man of eighty can walk. An old man can even walk a good deal, pottering about in country lanes. But an old man can't jump... if the baronet came back while we were waiting, he must have come in as we came in--by jumping the moat.

citations.section: The Worst Crime in the World
citations.quote: When I saw that fancy picture of the black figure with the grey hair taking a flying leap across the moat I knew instantly that it was a young man dressed up as an old man.

nodes.description: In The Worst Crime in the World, Father Brown exposes Captain Musgrave by pointing out that no man of eighty could have leaped the moat — only the young son in his father's disguise could.

nodes.label: The Marquis of Marne is Maurice Mair (the supposed victim), not James

citations.quote: 'Oh, my God ... it isn't Jim at all ... it's Maurice!'
'I will tell it as I found it... Maurice fell before the bullet flew, and lay perfectly still, waiting. And his wicked friend and teacher stood also in the background, waiting.'

nodes.description: Father Brown reveals in The Chief Mourner of Marne that the reclusive Marquis is Maurice Mair — the supposed duel victim — who faked his death, murdered his brother James, and has lived under James's identity ever since.

nodes.label: Two different false beards on Michael Moonshine

citations.quote: 'Then I had a belated gleam of sense when I saw the beard and goggles and remembered the thief had come in another beard with other goggles. Now, of course, it was just possible that he had duplicates; but it was at least a coincidence that he used neither the old glasses nor the old beard, both in good repair.'

nodes.description: Two different false beards worn by Michael Moonshine in The Man with Two Beards, the discrepancy revealing that the thief at the scene and the suspect seen earlier were the same man wearing two separate disguises.

nodes.label: Hunter's hand painted brown (he refused palmist to hide it)

citations.quote: 'Because he didn't want to take his glove off,' said Father Brown. 'If he had, we should all have seen that his hand was painted pale brown already.'

nodes.description: A hunter's hand painted brown in The Red Moon of Meru, hidden beneath a glove because the painted hand — not a naturally dark-skinned one — would have been visible to bystanders watching the ruby's theft.

nodes.label: Vaudrey's white hat left in barber's shop

citations.quote: Father Brown's eye roamed round the room... till his gaze found something in a dusty corner just behind the door. It was a hat hanging on a hat-peg. It was a white hat, and one very well known to all that village... a certain sort of man often entirely forgets, when he has most carefully washed floors or destroyed stained rags.

nodes.description: Vaudrey's distinctive white hat left in a barber's shop, spotted by Father Brown in The Vanishing of Vaudrey as the overlooked clue proving Vaudrey had been in the village before his disappearance.

nodes.label: Romaine's motionless stance at the duel (waiting for Maurice to fall)

citations.quote: 'Hugo Romaine on that old occasion, also, was waiting for something.' 'But it was all over,' said the general. 'What could he have been waiting for?' 'He was waiting for the duel,' said Father Brown.

nodes.description: Hugo Romaine's motionless stillness at the Marne duel in The Chief Mourner of Marne, identified by Father Brown as the key clue that Romaine was waiting for Maurice to fall — not firing at him.

nodes.label: Grandison Chace as framing interlocutor
nodes.source_location: The Secret of Father Brown (framing story)

citations.section: The Secret of Father Brown (framing story)
citations.quote: 'Can you give me any examples, I wonder.... I mean, did you deal with this last batch in that introspective style?'

citations.section: The Secret of Father Brown (framing story)
citations.quote: Chace continued to look at him with a reflective frown; he was too intelligent not to understand the idea.

nodes.description: Grandison Chace's role in 'The Secret of Father Brown' as the American framing narrator who draws Father Brown into explaining his method of identifying with criminals, asking for concrete examples from recent cases.

nodes.label: The Wisdom of Father Brown

citations.quote: THE WISDOM OF FATHER BROWN By G. K. Chesterton

nodes.description: The Wisdom of Father Brown, a G. K. Chesterton short-story collection in which Father Brown solves a series of baffling cases, often assisted by his reformed criminal friend Flambeau.

nodes.description: The Absence of Mr Glass, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown exposes the elaborate misreading of evidence by the eminent criminologist Dr. Orion Hood, who has incorrectly theorized what actually turns out to be a magic act rehearsal.

citations.quote: TWO. -- The Paradise of Thieves
THE great Muscari, most original of the young Tuscan poets, walked swiftly into his favourite restaurant, which overlooked the Mediterranean

nodes.description: The Paradise of Thieves, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown set in Tuscany, in which Father Brown exposes Samuel Harrogate as a bank embezzler who staged his own brigand kidnapping using hired men.

THERE is somewhere in Brompton or Kensington an interminable avenue of tall houses

nodes.description: The Head of Caesar, a Father Brown story in The Wisdom of Father Brown set in a Brompton or Kensington avenue in which Father Brown and Flambeau investigate a matter involving Arthur and Christabel Carstairs.

citations.quote: SEVEN -- The Purple Wig
MR EDWARD NUTT, the industrious editor of the Daily Reformer, sat at his desk

nodes.source_location: EIGHT -- The Perishing of the Pendragons

citations.section: EIGHT -- The Perishing of the Pendragons
citations.quote: 'Both eyes bright, she's all right; one eye blinks, down she sinks.'

citations.section: EIGHT -- The Perishing of the Pendragons
citations.quote: He saw that the frustrated fire was extinguished

nodes.description: The Perishing of the Pendragons, a story in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown and Flambeau investigate arson attacks on the Pendragon tower on the Cornwall coast, revealing an inheritance plot.

nodes.label: Dr Orion Hood

citations.quote: I am Dr Hood, and my work is almost entirely literary and educational.
Dr Orion Hood, the eminent criminologist and specialist in certain moral disorders

nodes.description: Dr. Orion Hood, an eminent criminologist and specialist in moral disorders whose elaborate but entirely wrong interpretation of the evidence in The Absence of Mr Glass is exposed by Father Brown.

nodes.label: James Todhunter (Zaladin)

citations.quote: This young James Todhunter is a very decent man so far as I know
ZALADIN, the World's Greatest Conjurer, Contortionist, Ventriloquist and Human Kangaroo

nodes.description: James Todhunter, a respectable young man who also performs as Zaladin, World's Greatest Conjurer and Contortionist, whose puzzling room situation is solved by Father Brown in The Absence of Mr Glass.

nodes.label: Ezza Montano (King of Thieves)

citations.quote: 'I call myself Montano,' cried the strange courier in a voice equally loud and full. 'I am the King of Thieves, and I welcome you all to my summer palace.'
I am an actor; and if I ever had a private character, I have forgotten it.

nodes.description: Ezza Montano, a professional actor who calls himself the King of Thieves and plays the role of a brigand courier to assist Samuel Harrogate's staged kidnapping scheme in The Paradise of Thieves.

nodes.label: Samuel Harrogate

citations.quote: 'Samuel Harrogate, I arrest you in the name of the law for embezzlement of the funds of the Hull and Huddersfield Bank.'
He fled with the company's money to Italy, and actually got himself captured by sham brigands in his own pay

nodes.description: Samuel Harrogate, an English bank embezzler who fled to Italy with the funds of the Hull and Huddersfield Bank and arranged a sham brigand kidnapping of himself using his own hired men, arrested by Father Brown in The Paradise of Thieves.

nodes.label: Colonel Dubosc

citations.quote: 'I am Jules Dubosc, Colonel of Artillery, Belfort.'
He saw Colonel Dubosc walking about in a brilliantly-lighted and luxurious bedroom

nodes.description: Colonel Jules Dubosc, a French artillery colonel of Belfort who impersonates Dr. Paul Hirsch to steal secret documents in The Duel of Dr Hirsch, moving about a brilliantly lit bedroom while his double is watched.

nodes.label: Dr Paul Hirsch

citations.quote: Dr Hirsch, scientist, publicist and moralist
He had lately even discovered a noiseless explosive, the secret of which the Government was carefully guarding.

nodes.description: Dr. Paul Hirsch, a French scientist, publicist, and moralist who has recently discovered a noiseless explosive, whose reputation is endangered by a forged spy document in The Duel of Dr Hirsch.

nodes.label: Aurora Rome

citations.quote: Miss Aurora Rome
Aurora Rome lay lustrous in her robes of green and gold, with her dead face turned upwards

nodes.description: Aurora Rome, an actress found murdered in her dressing room with her dead face turned upward in her green and gold robes, the victim at the centre of The Man in the Passage.

nodes.label: Greywood Usher

citations.quote: an ex-detective named Greywood Usher, a cadaverous, careful-spoken Yankee philosopher
I got hold of him with the crooked end of a walking-stick.

nodes.description: Greywood Usher, a cadaverous ex-detective and Yankee philosopher who relies on a psychometric pulsometer machine to identify suspects, challenged by Father Brown in The Mistake of the Machine.

nodes.label: Christabel Carstairs

citations.quote: 'my name is Christabel Carstairs; and my father was that Colonel Carstairs you've probably heard of, who made the famous Carstairs Collection of Roman coins.'

nodes.description: Christabel Carstairs, daughter of the late Colonel Carstairs and one of the siblings blackmailed by her brother Arthur in The Head of Caesar.

nodes.label: Arthur Carstairs

citations.quote: 'This man is Mr Arthur Carstairs,' said the priest positively, 'and he is a coin-collector of a somewhat singular kind.'
He not only sold the Collection bit by bit, but sank bit by bit to the basest ways of making money--even to blackmailing his own family in a disguise.

nodes.description: Arthur Carstairs, a coin collector of singular and criminal kind in The Head of Caesar who sold the Carstairs family collection piece by piece and then blackmailed his own brother and sister while disguised.

nodes.label: Admiral Pendragon

citations.quote: Admiral Pendragon sat down again as abruptly as he had risen
'You call me an atheist. I am an atheist.' Here he swung round and fixed Father Brown with a face of frightful concentration.

nodes.description: Admiral Pendragon, a self-declared atheist who is the subject of a sinister arson scheme at Pendragon Tower designed to fake a family curse, in The Perishing of the Pendragons.

nodes.label: Colonel Cray

citations.quote: 'I was engaged to Audrey out there; and we all travelled back together.'
'If you had only seen the Monkey's Feet,' he said, smiling steadily, 'we should have been very gentle'

nodes.description: Colonel Cray, a soldier who had been engaged to Audrey abroad and travelled back with her, appearing in The Salad of Colonel Cray from The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: The Absence of Mr Glass

citations.quote: 'that's the first idea to get fixed; the absence of Mr Glass. He is so extremely absent.'
'Mr Glass is absent from everywhere; he is absent from the Nature of Things'

nodes.description: The Absence of Mr Glass, a Father Brown case in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown deduces from a silk top hat with no hair traces that the threatening 'Mr Glass' does not exist and that the ventriloquist Todhunter is his own prisoner.

nodes.label: Harrogate embezzlement and staged kidnapping

citations.quote: He fled with the company's money to Italy, and actually got himself captured by sham brigands in his own pay, so as to explain both the disappearance of the money and the disappearance of himself.

nodes.description: A scheme in which Samuel Harrogate embezzled the funds of the Hull and Huddersfield Bank then staged a sham brigand kidnapping in an Italian Apennine hollow to divert suspicion, exposed by Father Brown in The Paradise of Thieves.

nodes.label: Hirsch forged spy document affair

citations.quote: 'a paper was found on him--a paper I hold in my hand. It is in his hand. It is signed with his initials.'
'It is a clumsy forgery by somebody who knew nothing of the real hiding-place.'

nodes.description: The Hirsch forged spy document affair, a Father Brown case in The Wisdom of Father Brown in which Father Brown deduces that a forged spy note implicating Dr Paul Hirsch was planted by a man who is simultaneously Hirsch's accusatory double, Dubosc.

nodes.label: Murder of Aurora Rome

citations.quote: Aurora Rome lay lustrous in her robes of green and gold, with her dead face turned upwards.
the spear poor old Parkinson killed his wife with, just when she'd sent for me to settle their family troubles

nodes.description: The murder of Aurora Rome, a Father Brown case in The Wisdom of Father Brown set at the Apollo Theatre in which the actress Aurora Rome is found dead, with Father Brown deducing the killer's identity through a sliding looking-glass panel that broke an alibi.

nodes.label: Carstairs Collection theft and blackmail

citations.quote: He blackmailed his brother from Australia for his little forgotten crime
he blackmailed his sister for the theft he alone could have noticed

nodes.description: The Carstairs Collection theft and blackmail case, in The Head of Caesar, in which Father Brown exposes Arthur Carstairs for secretly selling off the family coin collection piece by piece and then blackmailing his siblings.

nodes.label: Hull and Huddersfield Bank embezzlement with staged kidnapping

citations.quote: 'Samuel Harrogate, I arrest you in the name of the law for embezzlement of the funds of the Hull and Huddersfield Bank.'

nodes.description: Samuel Harrogate's embezzlement of the Hull and Huddersfield Bank followed by a staged kidnapping by fake brigands in Italy to divert suspicion, uncovered by Father Brown in The Paradise of Thieves.

nodes.label: Arthur Carstairs blackmails siblings in disguise

citations.quote: He not only sold the Collection bit by bit, but sank bit by bit to the basest ways of making money--even to blackmailing his own family in a disguise.

nodes.description: Arthur Carstairs's blackmail of his own brother and sister while in disguise, after secretly selling off the Carstairs family coin collection piece by piece, as exposed by Father Brown in The Head of Caesar.

nodes.label: Pendragon tower arson to fake curse

citations.quote: whenever this tower, with its pitch and resin-wood, really caught fire, the spark on the horizon always looked like the twin light to the coast light-house.
'That is how the father and brother died. The wicked uncle of the legends very nearly got his estate after all.'

nodes.description: A scheme of arson against the pitch-and-resin Pendragon tower on the Cornwall coast to manufacture a hereditary curse and eliminate the Pendragon heirs for the sake of the estate's inheritance, uncovered by Father Brown in The Perishing of the Pendragons.

nodes.label: Inheritance and estate greed

citations.quote: 'The wicked uncle of the legends very nearly got his estate after all.'

nodes.description: The motive in 'The Perishing of the Pendragons': a wicked uncle's drive to secure the Pendragon estate, pursued through arson designed to fake the family curse and eliminate the rightful heir.

nodes.label: Concealment of financial ruin and embezzlement

citations.quote: He fled with the company's money to Italy, and actually got himself captured by sham brigands in his own pay, so as to explain both the disappearance of the money and the disappearance of himself.

nodes.description: The motive in 'The Paradise of Thieves': an embezzler's scheme to conceal his flight with company funds by arranging his own fake capture by brigands he had hired himself.

nodes.label: Wounded pride and resentment of unjust will

citations.quote: It was a cruel will his wicked father made, and you see he did resent it a little. He hated the Roman money he had, and grew fonder of the real money denied him.

nodes.description: Arthur Carstairs's motive in 'The Head of Caesar': wounded pride and resentment at his father's cruel will, which led him to hate the Roman money he had been left and to scheme against his siblings through blackmail.

nodes.label: Moral inference from character

citations.quote: 'I go by a man's eyes and voice, don't you know, and whether his family seems happy, and by what subjects he chooses--and avoids.'
'Real madmen always encourage their own morbidity. They never strive against it.'

nodes.description: Father Brown's investigative approach in 'The Mistake of the Machine' of inferring guilt or innocence by reading a person's eyes, voice, domestic happiness, and habitual subjects of conversation rather than relying on mechanical tests.

nodes.label: Inversion of misleading evidence

citations.quote: 'The man who wrote that note knew all about the facts. He could never have got 'em so wrong without knowing about 'em. You have to know an awful lot to be wrong on every subject--like the devil.'

nodes.description: Father Brown's reasoning that a forger who knows all the true facts would make the forged document wrong in precisely the right ways, used to expose the Hirsch spy-document affair in The Duel of Dr Hirsch.

nodes.label: Mirror reflection as alibi-breaking deduction

citations.quote: 'when I looked down that passage, the man you saw was myself--in a mirror'
'I noticed that some of them could probably be slid out into the passage.'

nodes.description: A deduction technique used by Father Brown in The Man in the Passage, where he realises that the 'man' witnessed in a corridor was his own reflection in a mirror, breaking a false alibi in the murder of Aurora Rome.

nodes.label: Critique of psychometric machine (lie-detector)

citations.quote: 'No machine can lie; nor can it tell the truth.'
'the reliable machine always has to be worked by an unreliable machine'--'I mean Man'

nodes.description: Father Brown's critique in The Mistake of the Machine of the lie-detector (psychometric machine), arguing that no machine can lie or tell the truth, and that any machine must be operated by the unreliable agency of man.

nodes.label: Contextual observation of small details

citations.quote: 'The wax always spots like that just a little in this weather'
Mere figure and gait, however distant, are more likely to remind us of somebody than a well-made-up face quite close.

nodes.description: Father Brown's reasoning from small contextual details — the way wax spots in certain weather, and the recognition of a distant figure by gait rather than face — applied to unravel the Carstairs Collection theft in The Head of Caesar.

nodes.label: Silk top hat with no hair traces

citations.quote: 'with the lens I should see the tiny hairs in a hat recently worn. It has none, which leads me to guess that Mr Glass is bald.'
It is without traces of hair, not because it is worn by the prematurely bald Mr Glass, but because it has never been worn by anybody.

nodes.description: A silk top hat bearing no hair traces whatsoever in The Absence of Mr Glass, revealed by Father Brown to prove it was never worn rather than belonging to a bald man.

nodes.label: Sliding looking-glass panel in theatre passage

citations.quote: 'It was a looking-glass on the other side. When I was in the dressing-room I noticed that some of them could probably be slid out into the passage.'

nodes.description: A sliding looking-glass panel in the passage beside the Apollo Theatre in The Man in the Passage, which reflected witnesses' own images and resolved the contradictory accounts of Aurora Rome's murder.

nodes.label: Forged spy note with systematically wrong details

citations.quote: 'It says the paper is in the cupboard on the right of the Secretary's desk. As a fact the cupboard with the secret drawer is some way to the left of the desk.'
'It says the grey envelope contains a long document written in red ink. It isn't written in red ink, but in ordinary black ink.'

nodes.description: A forged spy document in The Duel of Dr Hirsch whose details are systematically wrong — wrong cupboard, wrong ink colour — proving it was fabricated to frame Dr Hirsch.

nodes.label: Bronze Head of Caesar coin

citations.quote: 'This was all that was left of the Carstairs Collection.'
the coin clenched tight in my fist... I gave him the head of Caesar

nodes.description: A bronze Head of Caesar coin, the sole surviving relic of the Carstairs Collection in The Head of Caesar, clenched in Father Brown's fist as he unravels the theft and blackmail scheme.

nodes.label: Scarborough sea-front

citations.quote: THE consulting-rooms of Dr Orion Hood... lay along the sea-front at Scarborough

nodes.description: The Scarborough sea-front in The Absence of Mr Glass in The Wisdom of Father Brown, location of Dr Orion Hood's consulting rooms where Father Brown exposes the non-existence of the supposed Mr Glass.

nodes.label: Paradise of Thieves, Apennine hollow

citations.quote: 'This pretty little nest where we are all picnicking... is known by the name of the Paradise of Thieves.'
the eyrie is invisible both from the road above and from the valley below

nodes.description: The Paradise of Thieves, a hidden Apennine hollow in The Paradise of Thieves in The Wisdom of Father Brown, invisible from both road and valley, where a bandit band shelters and a staged kidnapping scheme unravels.

nodes.label: Apollo Theatre passage, Adelphi

citations.quote: a sort of passage running along the side of the Apollo Theatre in the Adelphi

nodes.description: A passage running along the side of the Apollo Theatre in the Adelphi, London, the scene of Aurora Rome's murder in The Man in the Passage, where a sliding looking-glass panel created contradictory witness accounts.

nodes.label: Pilgrim's Pond, Ireton Todd's estate

citations.quote: Pilgrim's Pond, the estate, as you know, of the millionaire Ireton Todd
he was keeping his bullet for Pilgrim's Pond

nodes.description: Pilgrim's Pond, the country estate of millionaire Ireton Todd in The Mistake of the Machine in The Wisdom of Father Brown, where a staged shooting is set up and exposed by Father Brown.

nodes.label: Pendragon tower, Cornwall coast

citations.quote: the sign of the flaming tower
whenever this tower, with its pitch and resin-wood, really caught fire, the spark on the horizon always looked like the twin light to the coast light-house

nodes.description: Pendragon Tower on the Cornish coast in The Perishing of the Pendragons in The Wisdom of Father Brown, a pitch-and-resin-wood structure whose deliberate burning is used to fake the family curse's flaming-tower omen.

nodes.label: Conjurer's props (ropes, cards, top hat, sword)

citations.quote: Mr Todhunter is learning to be a professional conjurer, as well as juggler, ventriloquist, and expert in the rope trick.
Rabbits, ribbons, sweetmeats, goldfish, rolls of coloured paper

nodes.description: The conjurer's props belonging to Todhunter in 'The Absence of Mr Glass', including ropes, cards, a top hat, and a sword, which Father Brown deduces indicate not a criminal situation but a man practising to become a professional conjurer and ventriloquist.

nodes.label: Psychometric machine (pulsometer)

citations.quote: this new psychometric method... they put a pulsometer on a man's wrist and judge by how his heart goes at the pronunciation of certain words

nodes.description: A pulsometer-based psychometric device in 'The Mistake of the Machine' used by Greywood Usher to test a subject's guilt by monitoring heart rate at the pronunciation of significant words, which Father Brown argues cannot replace moral inference.

nodes.label: Noiseless explosive formula (Hirsch)

citations.quote: He had lately even discovered a noiseless explosive, the secret of which the Government was carefully guarding.
'Tell the man the formula for powder is in grey envelope in first drawer to the left of Secretary's desk, War Office'

nodes.description: Dr. Hirsch's secret formula for a noiseless explosive, carefully guarded by the French government, which becomes the subject of a forged spy document planted to discredit Hirsch in 'The Duel of Dr Hirsch.'

nodes.label: Semi-official investigator (Flambeau)

citations.quote: his friend Flambeau, a semi-official investigator, was sitting opposite him

nodes.description: Flambeau's role as a semi-official private investigator in 'The Head of Caesar', sitting opposite Father Brown as the cases are discussed.

nodes.label: Dubosc disguised as Dr Hirsch

citations.quote: he had already fitted round his face a sort of framework of rank red hair

Slipping on a loose black coat, the figure vanished towards the front of the house. A few moments later a roar of popular applause from the street beyond announced that Dr Hirsch had once more appeared upon the balcony.

nodes.description: Colonel Dubosc disguised as Dr. Hirsch in The Duel of Dr Hirsch from The Wisdom of Father Brown, using a framework of red hair fitted around his face and slipping on a loose black coat to impersonate the scientist.

nodes.label: Arthur Carstairs disguised as crooked-nosed blackmailer

citations.quote: his nose, which was otherwise of a good shape, was just slightly turned sideways at the tip; as if, when it was soft, it had been tapped on one side with a toy hammer.
'This man is Mr Arthur Carstairs,' said the priest positively, 'and he is a coin-collector of a somewhat singular kind.'

nodes.description: Arthur Carstairs's disguise as a crooked-nosed blackmailer in The Head of Caesar from The Wisdom of Father Brown, identified by Father Brown as a coin-collector of a singular kind despite his altered appearance.

nodes.label: Dubosc and Hirsch are the same person

citations.quote: 'They are the opposite of each other. They contradict each other. They cancel out, so to speak.'
The solidity and squareness of Dubosc's chest and shoulders was all a powerful piece of padding and came off with his coat.

nodes.description: In The Duel of Dr Hirsch, Father Brown demonstrates that the apparent spy Dubosc and the accused Dr Hirsch are one and the same person, their physical differences explained entirely by padding and disguise.

nodes.label: Mr Glass does not exist (ventriloquist misheard)

citations.quote: 'I mean he is absent from everywhere; he is absent from the Nature of Things, so to speak.'
When our juggling friend here threw up the three glasses in turn, he counted them aloud... 'One, two and three--missed a glass one, two--missed a glass.'

nodes.description: In The Absence of Mr Glass, Father Brown proves that 'Mr Glass' does not exist: a juggler counting aloud the three glasses he tossed was misheard by witnesses as a two-man conversation.

nodes.label: A Thief in the Night

citations.quote: A Thief in the Night [A Book of Raffles' Adventures] by E. W. Hornung

nodes.description: A Thief in the Night, a Raffles short-story collection by E. W. Hornung in which Bunny Manders narrates further adventures of the amateur cracksman, concluding with Raffles volunteering for the Boer War.

nodes.label: E. W. Hornung

citations.quote: by E. W. Hornung
BY E.W. HORNUNG
Raffles FURTHER ADVENTURES OF THE AMATEUR CRACKSMAN by E. W. Hornung

nodes.description: E. W. Hornung, the British author who created the gentleman-burglar A. J. Raffles and his companion Bunny Manders, writing The Amateur Cracksman, Raffles: Further Adventures, A Thief in the Night, and Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Raffles saga

citations.quote: If I must tell more tales of Raffles, I can but back to our earliest days together, and fill in the blanks left by discretion in existing annals.
Raffles had vanished from the face of the town, and even I had no conception of his whereabouts until he cabled to me to meet the 7.31 at Charing Cross next night.
I was irresistibly reminded of my poor dear old Raffles. A smoke-ring worthy of the great A. J. was floating upward from the sick man's lips.
it was A. J. Raffles in the flesh!

nodes.description: The Raffles saga, a series of crime stories featuring gentleman-burglar A. J. Raffles and narrated by his accomplice Bunny Manders, comprising The Amateur Cracksman, Raffles: Further Adventures, A Thief in the Night, and Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Out of Paradise

citations.quote: Out of Paradise
If I must tell more tales of Raffles, I can but back to our earliest days together

nodes.description: Out of Paradise, a Raffles story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles burglarises Lord Lochmaben's Palace Gardens home to recover diamonds on behalf of Bunny's beloved, drawing on some of their earliest days together.

nodes.label: The Chest of Silver

citations.quote: The Chest of Silver
Like all the tribe of which I held him head, Raffles professed the liveliest disdain for unwieldy plunder of any description

nodes.description: The Chest of Silver, a story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles hides inside an iron-clamped silver chest to infiltrate a bank strong-room, with Crawshay also appearing.

nodes.label: The Rest Cure

citations.quote: The Rest Cure
I've taken a house near here for the holidays, where I'm going in for a Rest Cure of my own description.

nodes.description: The Rest Cure, a Raffles story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles occupies a house near Campden Hill under a vagrant disguise, conducting a break-in at Colonel Crutchley's as his personal version of a rest cure.

nodes.label: The Criminologists' Club

citations.quote: The Criminologists' Club
"But who are they, Raffles, and where's their house? There's no such club on the list in Whitaker."

nodes.description: The Criminologists' Club, a Raffles story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles accepts an invitation to a secret club of criminologists and uses the evening's dinner as cover for burglarising Lord Thornaby's Park Lane house.

nodes.label: The Field of Phillipi

citations.quote: The Field of Phillipi
Nipper Nasmyth had been head of our school when Raffles was captain of cricket.

nodes.description: The Field of Phillipi, a story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles robs his old school-head Nipper Nasmyth's bank funds as private justice, taking just over a hundred sovereigns earmarked for the Founder's Fund.

nodes.label: A Bad Night

citations.quote: A Bad Night
There was to be a certain little wedding in which Raffles and I took a surreptitious interest.

nodes.description: A Bad Night, a short story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles and Bunny carry out a burglary of wedding presents at East Molesey, involving a young man named Medlicott.

nodes.label: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman
nodes.source_location: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman

citations.section: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman
citations.quote: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman

citations.section: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman
citations.quote: I was just putting out my light when the telephone rang a furious tocsin in the next room.

nodes.description: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman, a Raffles story in A Thief in the Night in which prize-fighter Barney Maguire sets a drugged-whiskey trap to catch a burglar, but Raffles turns it to his own advantage and steals Maguire's trophies.

nodes.label: The Spoils of Sacrilege

citations.quote: The Spoils of Sacrilege
It was I, then, and I alone, who outraged natural sentiment, and trampled the expiring embers of elementary decency, by proposing and planning the raid upon my own old home.

nodes.description: The Spoils of Sacrilege, a Raffles story in A Thief in the Night in which Raffles, disguised in a riding suit, burglarises Mr. Guillemard's country house and carries off old silver.

nodes.label: The Raffles Relics

citations.quote: The Raffles Relics

Its subject was the so-called Black Museum at Scotland Yard; and from the catchpenny text we first learned that the gruesome show was now enriched by a special and elaborate exhibit known as the Raffles Relics.

nodes.description: The Raffles Relics, the concluding story of A Thief in the Night in which Raffles steals his own criminal relics from Scotland Yard's Black Museum, with Bunny Manders learning from Inspector Druce of Raffles's death volunteering for the Boer War.

nodes.label: Lady Lochmaben

citations.quote: Lady Lochmaben has just as good diamonds as Mrs. Carruthers ever had; and the chances are that she keeps them where Mrs. Carruthers kept hers

nodes.description: Lady Lochmaben, the wife of Lord Lochmaben (Hector Carruthers) who keeps her diamonds in the same place as Mrs. Carruthers once did, making her a target considered by Raffles in Out of Paradise from A Thief in the Night.

nodes.label: Hector Carruthers (Lord Lochmaben)

citations.quote: "Hector Carruthers!" murmured Raffles
old Carruthers headed the list of the Birthday Honors, and Lord Lochmaben is the title of his choice.

nodes.description: Hector Carruthers, who rises to become Lord Lochmaben on the Birthday Honours list and appears in Out of Paradise from A Thief in the Night.

nodes.label: Crawshay

citations.quote: I should call him the very prince of professional cracksmen.
'Ware Prince of Professors! He was in the offing when I left.

nodes.description: Crawshay, called 'the very prince of professional cracksmen,' a dangerous career criminal who appears in The Chest of Silver from A Thief in the Night and whose proximity is a warning to Raffles.

nodes.label: Colonel Crutchley

citations.quote: Colonel Crutchley, R.E., V.C., has been the victim of a dastardly outrage at his residence, Peter Street, Campden Hill.
of sappers, and a V.C. into the bargain, confound him! Got it at Rorke's Drift

nodes.description: Colonel Crutchley, a Royal Engineers officer and Victoria Cross winner (Rorke's Drift) who becomes the victim of an outrage at his Campden Hill residence in The Rest Cure.

nodes.label: Lord Thornaby

citations.quote: it was from the Right Hon. the Earl of Thornaby, K.G.; and it requested the honor of my company at dinner, at Thornaby House, Park Lane, to meet the members of the Criminologists' Club.

nodes.description: Lord Thornaby, an earl who hosts the Criminologists' Club dinner at Thornaby House, during which Raffles burrows his robes in The Criminologists' Club.

nodes.label: Nipper Nasmyth

citations.quote: Nipper Nasmyth had been head of our school when Raffles was captain of cricket.
I happen to be a trustee.

nodes.description: Nipper Nasmyth, a former head of school and trustee of the Founder's Fund who commits a bank robbery, confronted by Raffles in The Field of Phillipi.

nodes.label: Young Medlicott

citations.quote: "I know that, Mr. Medlicott," I rejoined on my own account.
young Medlicott was as helpless as he had been before; the flush had faded from his face, and his breathing alone would have spoiled everything.

nodes.description: Young Medlicott, an ailing young man in A Bad Night from A Thief in the Night, who lies helpless and breathing faintly while Bunny attends to him.

nodes.label: Barney Maguire
nodes.source_location: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman

citations.section: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman
citations.quote: it was filled with this frightful vision of the notorious pugilist known to fame and infamy as Barney Maguire.

citations.section: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman
citations.quote: Heavy-weight champion of the United States, the fellow was still drunk with his sanguinary triumphs on that side

nodes.description: Barney Maguire, the notorious American heavy-weight boxing champion whose trophies Raffles attempts to steal while Maguire is still drunk with his latest victory, as described in A Trap to Catch a Cracksman.

nodes.label: Mr. Guillemard

citations.quote: the bloated Guillemard usually sweeps the board with his fancy flyers.
An enormous fellow, with a great red face and cropped moustache, occupied my poor father's place

nodes.description: Mr. Guillemard, an enormously built man with a red face who owns a country house whose old silver is targeted by Raffles in The Spoils of Sacrilege.

nodes.label: Inspector Druce

citations.quote: "Inspector Druce," the clerk informed us in impressive whispers, "who had the Chalk Farm case in hand. He'd be the man for Raffles, if Raffles was alive to-day!"

nodes.description: Inspector Druce, a Scotland Yard detective known for the Chalk Farm case, who pursues A. J. Raffles in The Raffles Relics.

nodes.label: Bunny's Beloved (unnamed)
nodes.source_location: Out of Paradise / The Last Word

citations.section: Out of Paradise / The Last Word
citations.quote: one dearer to me than Raffles himself, one whose name shall not even now be sullied by association with ours.

citations.section: Out of Paradise / The Last Word
citations.quote: I omit her name and nothing else. Did I not say in the beginning that it should never be sullied by association with mine?

nodes.description: Bunny's unnamed beloved, a woman dearer to Bunny than Raffles himself, whose name is deliberately withheld throughout A Thief in the Night to prevent it from being sullied by association with the criminals' story.

nodes.label: Palace Gardens burglary (Lochmaben diamonds)

citations.quote: the only thing was to get out of the bank; for the stars were drowning
it was only an attempted robbery; nothing had been taken, after all.

nodes.description: Raffles's attempted burglary of a house in Palace Gardens, Kensington, targeting the Lochmaben diamonds, which ultimately fails as nothing is taken, recounted in Out of Paradise.

nodes.label: Silver chest bank strong-room scheme

citations.quote: So you were the burglar!
He had thrust his head through a lid within the lid, cut by himself between the two iron bands that ran round the chest like the straps of a portmanteau.

nodes.description: A scheme by A. J. Raffles in which he concealed himself inside an iron-clamped silver chest in the strong-room of the City and Suburban Bank in Sloane Street, as described in The Chest of Silver.

nodes.label: Campden Hill house occupation (Rest Cure)

citations.quote: I've taken a house near here for the holidays, where I'm going in for a Rest Cure of my own description.
You are actually staying in a house where you've broken in to steal?
"Not to steal, Bunny! I haven't stolen a thing."

nodes.description: Raffles's scheme of secretly occupying Colonel Crutchley's house near Campden Hill under the pretense of a self-styled rest cure, without actually stealing anything, as told in The Rest Cure.

nodes.label: Thornaby House robes burglary during Criminologists' dinner

citations.quote: I had the only hand in it, my dear Bunny.

he did it in a manner so characteristic of himself as surely to remove from their minds the last aura of the idea that he and himself were the same person. Carter Paterson was out of the question... But Raffles stabled the white elephants in the cloak-room at Charing Cross--and sent Lord Thornaby the ticket.

nodes.description: The Thornaby House robes burglary, a Raffles escapade in The Criminologists' Club in which Raffles steals Lord Thornaby's peer's robes and coronet from his Park Lane house during the evening of a criminologists' dinner.

nodes.label: Nasmyth's bank robbery (Founder's Fund)

citations.quote: It's going to mean old Nasmyth's subscription to the Founder's Fund, and that's to be a big one, I promise you!
It was but a little over a hundred sovereigns that Raffles had taken

nodes.description: Raffles's robbery of just over a hundred sovereigns from Nipper Nasmyth's bank funds—specifically the Founder's Fund subscription—as a form of private justice, recounted in The Field of Phillipi.

nodes.label: East Molesey wedding-present burglary

citations.quote: The wedding presents were so numerous as to fill several rooms at the pretty retreat upon the Mole, and of an intrinsic value calling for a special transaction with the Burglary Insurance Company in Cheapside.
these emerald links are all right, and I don't know what the bride was doing to leave this diamond comb behind.

nodes.description: Raffles's burglary of the numerous and intrinsically valuable wedding presents stored at a riverside retreat on the Mole at East Molesey, in which he takes emerald links and a diamond comb, recounted in A Bad Night.

nodes.description: The Barney Maguire trophies theft, a Raffles escapade in A Trap to Catch a Cracksman in which Raffles turns a prize-fighter's drugged-whiskey trap to his own advantage and steals Maguire's collection of trophies.

nodes.label: Guillemard country house burglary (old Bunny home)

citations.quote: It was I, then, and I alone, who outraged natural sentiment, and trampled the expiring embers of elementary decency, by proposing and planning the raid upon my own old home.
in one was a diamond tiara, and in the other a necklace of fine emeralds set in clusters of brilliants.

citations.quote: there, reposing on the cloth with the whiskey and sandwiches, was the whole collection of Raffles Relics which had occupied the lid of the silver-chest in the Black Museum at Scotland Yard!
I was a real Father Christmas, said Raffles

nodes.description: The Scotland Yard Black Museum Raffles Relics theft, the episode in The Raffles Relics in which Raffles breaks into Scotland Yard and steals back his own criminal tools from the Black Museum display.

nodes.label: Concealment inside silver chest / trunk
nodes.source_location: The Chest of Silver / The Raffles Relics

citations.section: The Chest of Silver / The Raffles Relics
citations.quote: He had thrust his head through a lid within the lid, cut by himself between the two iron bands that ran round the chest like the straps of a portmanteau.

citations.section: The Chest of Silver / The Raffles Relics
citations.quote: You push one of the handles down--which no one ever does--and the whole of that end opens like the front of a doll's house.

nodes.description: Raffles's false-bottomed silver chest hiding device in The Chest of Silver and The Raffles Relics in A Thief in the Night, fitted with a secret inner lid opened by pressing one of the handles down.

nodes.label: Rope-ladder and telescopic hook scaling

citations.quote: lapped round and round his waist was the finest of Manila ropes, with the neatest of foot-loops at regular intervals.

He unscrewed the ferule, and shook out of the cane a diminishing series of smaller canes, exactly like a child's fishing-rod... A double hook of steel was now produced and quickly attached to the tip of the top joint

nodes.description: Raffles's rooftop entry technique in 'The Criminologists' Club', combining a Manila rope with foot-loops worn around his waist and a telescopic hook fashioned from a series of diminishing canes screwed together.

nodes.label: Vagrant / workman disguise

citations.quote: "You silly ass, Bunny, don't tell all Kensington that I'm in town!" replied my tatterdemalion, shooting up and smoothing out into a merely shabby Raffles.
What with his flourishing beard and the increasing shabbiness of the only suit he had brought with him to the house, there was no denying that Raffles had now the advantage of a permanent disguise.

nodes.description: Raffles's tatterdemalion vagrant and workman disguise in The Rest Cure in A Thief in the Night, used to move around Kensington unrecognised while secretly occupying a targeted house over an extended period.

nodes.label: Velvet bag noiseless key-filing

citations.quote: one of his latest inventions was a little thick velvet bag in which he carried the keys; and this bag had two elastic mouths, which closed so tightly about either wrist that he could file away, inside, and scarcely hear it himself.
Raffles had taken up the bag that he had invented for the noiseless filing of keys.

nodes.description: Raffles's invention in 'The Field of Phillipi' of a thick velvet bag with two elastic mouths to muffle the sound of key-filing, allowing him to cut keys in silence.

nodes.label: Cricket celebrity as social cover

citations.quote: Raffles was the only man who could get a wicket up at Lord's

In the summer I have my cricket to provide me with decent employment in the sight of men. Keep yourself before the public from morning to night, and they'll never think of you in the still small hours.

nodes.description: Raffles's use of his fame as a first-class England cricketer as social cover in Out of Paradise in A Thief in the Night, keeping himself visible in respectable society while conducting burglaries.

nodes.label: False insurance detective impersonation

citations.quote: "A detective officer," I replied, "sent down by the Burglary Insurance Company."
Not a moment had I paused for my precious fable. It had all been prepared for me by Raffles, in case of need.

nodes.description: A cover story prepared by Raffles and executed by Bunny in 'A Bad Night', whereby Bunny poses as an insurance detective sent by a Burglary Insurance Company to deflect suspicion during a break-in.

nodes.label: Drug trap reversal (switching decanter labels)
nodes.source_location: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman

citations.quote: I guessed what it was, and couldn't resist making sure; the minute I was satisfied, I changed the label and the position of the two decanters

citations.section: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman
citations.quote: My trap for crooks and cracksmen is a bottle of hocussed whiskey, and I guess that's it on the table, with the silver label around its neck.

nodes.description: A method used by Raffles in 'A Trap to Catch a Cracksman' whereby he discovers and reverses a drugged whiskey trap set for burglars by switching the decanter labels, turning the device against its setter.

nodes.label: Lightning conductor descent escape

citations.quote: "Is there a lightning conductor?" "There was."
Raffles, slipping on a pair of white kid gloves, and stuffing his handkerchief into the palm of one. "The difficulty is to keep a grip; but I've been up and down them before to-night."

nodes.description: Raffles's escape method in 'The Spoils of Sacrilege' of descending from a building via its lightning conductor, wearing white kid gloves for grip.

nodes.label: Palace Gardens, Kensington

through the narrow passage into Palace Gardens. He knew the house as well as I did.

nodes.description: Palace Gardens, Kensington, a street of grand politicians' houses in Out of Paradise in A Thief in the Night, the scene of Raffles's burglary of a pompous politician's Lochmaben diamond.

nodes.label: City and Suburban Bank, Sloane Street

citations.quote: An audacious burglary and dastardly assault have been committed on the premises of the City and Suburban Bank in Sloane Street, W.

nodes.label: Campden Hill house (Colonel Crutchley's residence)

citations.quote: his residence, Peter Street, Campden Hill
It was ever such a quiet little street, turning out of one of those which climb right over the pleasant hill.

nodes.description: Colonel Crutchley's residence on Peter Street, Campden Hill, London, secretly occupied by Raffles during The Rest Cure in A Thief in the Night while he stages a prolonged internal burglary.

nodes.label: Thornaby House, Park Lane

citations.quote: it requested the honor of my company at dinner, at Thornaby House, Park Lane, to meet the members of the Criminologists' Club.
Thornaby House is almost at the end of my street

citations.section: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman
citations.quote: "No, no; at Maguire's." "You never said so. And where's Maguire?" "In Half-moon Street."

nodes.description: Half-moon Street, Mayfair, where boxing promoter Barney Maguire furnished a house in A Trap to Catch a Cracksman in A Thief in the Night, the scene of the drugged-whisky burglary.

nodes.label: East Molesey / banks of the Mole

the other affair had been all arranged for the Thursday night, the night of the wedding at East Molesey.

nodes.description: East Molesey on the Surrey banks of the River Mole in A Bad Night in A Thief in the Night, where Raffles targets wedding presents at a secluded family house on the night of a wedding.

nodes.label: Scotland Yard Black Museum

citations.quote: Its subject was the so-called Black Museum at Scotland Yard; and from the catchpenny text we first learned that the gruesome show was now enriched by a special and elaborate exhibit known as the Raffles Relics.

nodes.description: The Scotland Yard Black Museum, where artefacts from famous criminal cases—including Raffles relics such as the iron-clamped silver chest—are exhibited, as described in The Raffles Relics.

nodes.label: Ham Common (Raffles and Bunny's retirement base)

citations.quote: we still operated, as the spirit tempted us, from our latest and most idyllic base, on the borders of Ham Common.
I had to grope my way through a white fog from the river to Ham Common

nodes.description: Ham Common on the Surrey-Middlesex border in A Thief in the Night, the idyllic final base from which Raffles and Bunny operate their last series of burglaries before Raffles enlists for the Boer War.

nodes.label: Iron-clamped silver chest

citations.quote: I could only think that he was inspired by the same impulse toward the bronzed salvers and the tarnished teapots with which I found him surrounded, until my eyes lit upon the enormous silver-chest into which he was fitting them one by one.
that Noah's Ark of yours? Yes, so I hear; they'd just got to it when they were interrupted

nodes.description: The iron-clamped silver chest used by Raffles to conceal himself and infiltrate the City and Suburban Bank strong-room in The Chest of Silver, later exhibited as a Raffles relic in the Scotland Yard Black Museum.

nodes.label: Raffles Relics (tools at Scotland Yard)

citations.quote: There was the revolver that I had only once heard fired, and there the blood-stained life-preserver, brace-and-bit, bottle of rock-oil, velvet bag, rope-ladder, walking-stick, gimlets, wedges, and even the empty cartridge-case
These are the Raffles Relics, taken from his rooms in the Albany after his death and burial, and the most complete set we've got.

nodes.description: The Raffles Relics, a collection of A. J. Raffles's burglary tools — including a revolver, life-preserver, brace-and-bit, and bottles — displayed at Scotland Yard's Black Museum after his death.

citations.quote: "My peer's robes, with coronet complete!"

citations.quote: My trap for crooks and cracksmen is a bottle of hocussed whiskey, and I guess that's it on the table, with the silver label around its neck.

citations.quote: I got the poison from a blamed Indian away west, and it's ruther ticklish stuff.
