nodes.label: The Criminologists' Club

citations.quote: The Criminologists, my dear Bunny, are too few for a local habitation, and too select to tell their name in Gath. They are merely so many solemn students of contemporary crime, who meet and dine periodically at each other's clubs or houses.

nodes.description: The Criminologists' Club in 'The Criminologists' Club', a small and select London dining society for students of crime who meet without a fixed address, whose members invite Raffles to dine and are robbed by him the same night.

nodes.label: Bunny's loyalty / dependency motive

citations.quote: It was Raffles I loved. It was not the dark life we led together, still less its base rewards; it was the man himself, his gayety, his humor, his dazzling audacity, his incomparable courage and resource.

nodes.description: Bunny Manders's driving motive in the Raffles stories, expressed in 'Out of Paradise' as personal love for Raffles himself rather than attraction to the criminal life or its rewards.

nodes.label: Raffles's private justice / redistribution motive

citations.quote: It's going to mean old Nasmyth's subscription to the Founder's Fund
He merely refused to allow me to implicate myself in its execution.
He posted his own first contribution of twenty-five pounds to the Founder's Fund immediately on our return to town

nodes.description: Raffles's redistributive motive in 'The Field of Phillipi': robbing old Nasmyth to fund the Founder's Fund, contributing twenty-five pounds of his own immediately on returning to town, while refusing to let Bunny share in the execution.

nodes.label: Bunny Manders narrator role

citations.quote: If I must tell more tales of Raffles, I can but back to our earliest days together, and fill in the blanks left by discretion in existing annals.
In so doing I may indeed fill some small part of an infinitely greater blank, across which you may conceive me to have stretched my canvas for the first frank portrait of my friend.
And eventually I had my reward, in such a recital of malignant privation as I cannot trust myself to set down in any words but his.
I am still uncertain which surprised me more, the telegram calling my attention to the advertisement, or the advertisement itself.
As I have had occasion to remark elsewhere, the pick of our exploits, from a frankly criminal point of view, are of least use for the comparatively pure purposes of these papers.

nodes.description: Bunny Manders's role as retrospective narrator of the Raffles saga, recounting his criminal adventures with Raffles from their earliest days together and filling in gaps across all four collections.

nodes.label: Raffles's vagrant / shabby-beard disguise

citations.quote: among others, I have long had a wish to grow my own beard; under the next lamppost you will agree that it's training on very nicely.
His face was grimy, and dexterously plastered with a growth of reddish hair; his clothes were those in which he had followed cabs from the London termini

nodes.description: Raffles's vagrant and shabby-beard disguise in The Rest Cure from A Thief in the Night, combining a grimy face with a dexterously applied growth of reddish hair and worn-out clothes to pass unrecognised.

nodes.label: Raffles's riding-suit disguise (Guillemard escape)

citations.quote: this riding-suit makes a new man of me, on top of my own, and here's a youth's kit that should do you down to the ground.
there was a police officer in a peaked cap to see it off; but he was too busy peering into the compartments for a pair of very swell mobsmen that he took no notice of the huge man in riding-clothes

nodes.description: Raffles's riding-suit disguise in The Spoils of Sacrilege from A Thief in the Night, worn over his own clothes during the Guillemard escape and supplemented by a youth's kit for Bunny, successfully passing a police officer at the station.

nodes.label: Raffles volunteers for Boer War and dies
nodes.source_location: The Raffles Relics / The Last Word

citations.section: The Raffles Relics / The Last Word
citations.quote: Was he thinking even then of volunteering for the front? Had he already set his heart on the one chance of some atonement for his life--nay, on the very death he was to die?

citations.section: The Raffles Relics / The Last Word
citations.quote: he is dead, and you are punished. The world forgives, if it does not forget.

nodes.description: Raffles volunteers for service in the Boer War and dies at the front, as related in The Raffles Relics and The Last Word, his death seen as a final act of atonement.

nodes.label: Raffles Relics displayed in Scotland Yard Black Museum

citations.quote: These are the Raffles Relics, taken from his rooms in the Albany after his death and burial, and the most complete set we've got. That's his centre-bit, and this is the bottle of rock-oil he's supposed to have kept dipping it in to prevent making a noise.

nodes.description: In The Raffles Relics, items taken from Raffles's Albany rooms after his death are displayed in Scotland Yard's Black Museum as the most complete set of a criminal's tools ever exhibited.

nodes.label: Raffles recognised as greatest burglar of his age

citations.quote: "The greatest burgular we ever had," said he, "till good old Raffles knocked him out!"
"The greatest of the pre-Raffleites," the master murmured

nodes.description: In The Raffles Relics, Scotland Yard officers describe Raffles as the greatest burglar of his era, a master who knocked out the best pre-Raffleite thief before him.

nodes.label: Trap to catch a cracksman (Maguire's man-trap)
nodes.source_location: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman

citations.section: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman
citations.quote: Maguire replying that he had a trap to catch the cleverest cracksman alive, but flatly refusing to tell us what it was.

citations.section: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman
citations.quote: "In that trap he bragged about. It serves me right. I didn't believe in it. But I'm caught at last ... caught ... at last!"

nodes.description: Maguire's man-trap devised to catch the cleverest cracksman alive, which ultimately ensnares A. J. Raffles in A Trap to Catch a Cracksman.

nodes.label: Mr. Justice Raffles

citations.quote: MR. JUSTICE RAFFLES / BY E.W. HORNUNG / 1909

nodes.description: Mr. Justice Raffles, a 1909 Raffles novel by E. W. Hornung in which Raffles takes vigilante justice against the moneylender Dan Levy to protect the indebted Garland family.

nodes.label: Dan Levy

citations.quote: 'I should think I did,' said I; 'you mean the great Dan Levy, otherwise Mr. Shylock?'
Dan Levy, as I must try to call him when Raffles is not varnishing my tale, looked a very big man at his enormous desk

nodes.description: Dan Levy, a ruthless moneylender and the principal antagonist of Mr. Justice Raffles, who ensnares the Garland family in usurious debt and is ultimately kidnapped and coerced into restitution by Raffles.

nodes.label: Teddy Garland

citations.quote: E.M. Garland (Eton and Trinity) was the Cambridge wicketkeeper, and one of the many young cricketers who owed a good deal to Raffles.
'I couldn't let poor Teddy keep at Lord's,' explained Raffles, 'and me not there to egg him on!'

nodes.description: Teddy Garland, a young Cambridge cricketer in Mr. Justice Raffles who falls into Dan Levy's debt trap through a cheque forgery attempt, prompting Raffles to take altruistic action against the moneylender.

nodes.label: Camilla Belsize

citations.quote: 'Miss Belsize has just wired to say she is coming up early to go with us to the match, instead of meeting at Lord's'
Camilla Belsize had refused to be released

nodes.description: Camilla Belsize, a young woman in Mr. Justice Raffles who is engaged to Teddy Garland and refuses to be released from the engagement despite his debts to Dan Levy, her loyalty prompting Raffles's intervention.

nodes.label: Mr. Garland senior

citations.quote: I shook hands instead with a gentle, elderly man, whose kindly eyes beamed bravely amid careworn furrows
'He was born into a big business, but he wasn't born a business man.'

nodes.description: Mr. Garland senior, the gentle, elderly father in Mr. Justice Raffles whose Kensington house and business assets have fallen entirely under the control of the moneylender Dan Levy through years of usurious debt.

nodes.label: Mackenzie

citations.quote: The man was Mackenzie, the incorrigibly Scotch detective whom we had met at Milchester Abbey, who I always thought had kept an eye on Raffles ever since.
'Ye ken Dan Levy's hoose by the river?' I heard him babble to his cabman, with wilful breadth of speech.

nodes.description: Mackenzie, the incorrigibly Scottish Scotland Yard detective who pursues Raffles across multiple cases and hurries to Dan Levy's riverside house after his murder in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: The foreign assassin

citations.quote: 'Nutting, sare!' replied an alien tongue, a gleam of good teeth in the shadow of his great soft hat.
'That blessed little beggar on my tracks down here! I've 'ad him thrown out of the office in Jermyn Street; he's threatened me by letter and telegram'

nodes.description: The foreign assassin, an unnamed man of Latin appearance who pursues Dan Levy to his riverside house and kills him, as recounted in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Lady Laura Belsize

citations.quote: Camilla's mother took shape in my mind as the Lady Laura Belsize, an apparently impecunious widow reduced to 'semi-detachment down the river'

nodes.description: Lady Laura Belsize, an apparently impecunious widow living in semi-detached circumstances, described as Camilla's mother in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Maharajah of Hathipur

citations.quote: it seems he's been in our Mr. Shylock's clutches for years, but instead of taking his pound of flesh he's always increasing the amount.

nodes.description: The Maharajah of Hathipur, an Indian prince who has been in the financial clutches of the moneylender Dan Levy for years, whose debt Levy uses to increase his hold over him, mentioned in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Dan Levy emeralds affair

citations.quote: 'Have you forgotten how you saw the emeralds under their table when they'd gone, and how _I_ forgot myself and ran after them with the best necklace I'd handled since the days of Lady Melrose?'

nodes.description: The Dan Levy emeralds affair, the opening incident of Mr. Justice Raffles in which Raffles glimpses a valuable emerald necklace under Levy's table at a cricket match at Lord's and allows the moneylender to suspect he has retrieved it.

nodes.label: Garland debt rescue

citations.quote: 'That boy's in a bigger hole than he thinks. But we must pull him out between us before play begins. It's one clear call for us, Bunny!'

nodes.description: The Garland debt rescue, a subplot of Mr. Justice Raffles in which Raffles sets out to extricate young Teddy Garland from the financial entanglement with the moneylender Dan Levy, the immediate spur to Raffles's campaign of private justice.

nodes.label: Burroughs and Burroughs letter theft

citations.quote: 'I shall take a sheet of your paper with me, and forge the forgery!'
'And you merely wish to have their letter--withdrawn, Mr. Levy?' 'That's the way to put it.'

nodes.description: The Burroughs and Burroughs letter theft, an episode in Mr. Justice Raffles in which Raffles breaks into Gray's Inn Square to steal back a compromising letter that Dan Levy has used to blackmail through the magazine Fact.

nodes.label: Kidnapping of Dan Levy

citations.quote: 'I shall hold him up to ransom,' said Raffles, 'at the top of this ruddy tower, until he pays through both nostrils for the privilege of climbing down alive.'

nodes.description: The kidnapping of Dan Levy, an episode in Mr. Justice Raffles in which Raffles holds the moneylender captive in a tower house on the Thames until he agrees to release the Garland family from their usurious debt.

nodes.label: Usury and debt entrapment of the Garlands

citations.quote: 'he and every stick, brick and branch he is supposed to possess have been in Dan Levy's hands for months and years.'
He borrowed three hundred and signed a promissory note for four hundred and fifty-six.

nodes.description: The usury and debt entrapment of the Garlands, the scheme in Mr. Justice Raffles by which the moneylender Dan Levy ensnares Mr. Garland senior and his son Teddy in escalating promissory notes, taking possession of their Kensington house.

nodes.label: Carlsbad emerald necklace theft

citations.quote: He told me how he waited for a dark night, and then slid down from his sitting-room balcony to the one below.
'Out with those emeralds,' says I in low German mugged up in case of need.

nodes.description: Raffles's theft of the Levy emerald necklace at Carlsbad, accomplished by sliding from his balcony to the one below and threatening the occupant in low German, as told in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Levy's blackmailing letter forgery scheme

citations.quote: 'They think it was written by me,' said he. 'It's a forgery, written on my office paper; if that isn't my property, I should like to know what is?'
'the crucial letter in your case against Fact'

nodes.description: Dan Levy's scheme involving a forged letter written on his own office paper purporting to be the crucial document in the libel case against Fact magazine, in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Raffles private justice scheme

citations.quote: 'You are indicted with extortion and sharp practice in all your dealings, with cheating and misleading your customers, attempting to cheat and betray your friends, and breaking all the rules of civilised crime.'

nodes.description: Raffles's scheme of private justice in Mr. Justice Raffles, in which he formally indicts Dan Levy for extortion, sharp practice, cheating, and misleading his customers, demanding financial restitution from him.

nodes.label: Teddy Garland's attempted cheque forgery

citations.quote: it was no note young Garland was writing, but a cheque which he was laboriously copying into Raffles's cheque-book
A sheet of notepaper lay covered with trial signatures.

nodes.description: Teddy Garland's attempted cheque forgery in Mr. Justice Raffles, discovered when Bunny finds him laboriously copying Raffles's signature into his cheque-book, with a sheet of trial signatures beside him.

nodes.label: Murder of Dan Levy

citations.quote: TRAGIC DEATH OF FAMOUS MONEYLENDER / Mr. Daniel Levy, the financier, reported shot dead at front gates of his residence in Thames Valley at 5.30 this afternoon
'It was a case of stabbing in accordance with the best traditions of the Latin races.'

nodes.description: The murder of moneylender Dan Levy, shot at the front gates of his riverside house and stabbed by a foreign assassin taking revenge, as reported in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: University cricket match at Lord's

citations.quote: That was on the Tuesday before the 'Varsity match
E.M. Garland (Eton and Trinity) was the Cambridge wicketkeeper

nodes.description: The Cambridge University versus Oxford cricket match at Lord's, at which E. M. Garland keeps wicket for Cambridge and which is mentioned in connection with the Garland debt affair in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Levy's assault on Raffles at riverside

citations.quote: the nobbler of crude spirit was hurled with most sober aim, glass and all, full in the face of Raffles, and the letter plucked from his grasp and flung upon the fire, while Raffles was still reeling in his blindness

nodes.description: Dan Levy's assault on Raffles at the riverside, hurling a glass of crude spirit full in his face and seizing the crucial forged letter back during a confrontation in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Levy signs mortgage deed in tower

citations.quote: Signed it was, however, and with less demur than might have been expected of so inveterate a fighter as Dan Levy.
an INDENTURE, in fourteenth century capitals... its eventual plain provision for 'the said sum of £15,000 to remain charged upon the security of the hereditaments'

nodes.description: Dan Levy's reluctant signing of the mortgage indenture for fifteen thousand pounds in the sound-proof tower room, secured by Raffles's coercion, in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Raffles and Bunny flee to the Continent

citations.quote: 'Australia, if you like! We'll discuss it in our flight across Europe.'
We were starting off round the world for a change.

nodes.description: Raffles and Bunny's escape by hansom and then across Europe following the resolution of the Dan Levy affair, setting out on a journey around the world in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Teddy Garland borrowed £300 from Levy, now owes £700

citations.quote: He borrows three hundred for a year at the outside, and you blackmail him for eight hundred when the year's up.
Seven hundred was the actual figure.

nodes.description: In Mr Justice Raffles, Teddy Garland borrowed £300 from Dan Levy for one year and now owes £700 through usurious compounding, the debt Raffles schemes to eliminate.

nodes.label: Garland's Kensington house owned by Levy

citations.quote: 'The whole place happens to be _mine_--'freehold and everything!'' replied Levy
he and every stick, brick and branch he is supposed to possess have been in Dan Levy's hands for months and years.

nodes.description: In Mr Justice Raffles, Dan Levy gloats that the Garland Kensington house and every asset the family appears to own has legally been his through usurious debt for months and years.

nodes.label: Dan Levy murdered by foreign creditor, not Raffles

citations.quote: 'It was that poor unlucky little alien who was waiting for him the other morning in Jermyn Street, and again last night near his own garden gate. That's where he got him in the end.'

nodes.description: In Mr Justice Raffles, it emerges that Dan Levy was murdered not by Raffles but by a foreign creditor who had been lying in wait for him first in Jermyn Street and then on the night of his death.

nodes.label: Levy suing magazine Fact for libel over Maharajah letters

citations.quote: 'Another old rascal, the Maharajah of Hathipur, and his perfectly fabulous debts... _Fact_ has exposed the whole thing, and printed blackmailing letters which Shylock swears are forgeries.'

nodes.description: In Mr Justice Raffles, Dan Levy is revealed to be pursuing a libel action against the magazine Fact over its exposure of his dealings with the Maharajah of Hathipur.

nodes.label: Raffles was romantically attached to Camilla but stepped aside for Teddy

citations.quote: 'we were getting on like a semi-detached house on fire! I burnt my fingers, I don't mind telling you'
'It was I who brought those two together. But it was none of my mundane business to put them asunder again.'

nodes.description: In Mr Justice Raffles, Raffles confesses he was romantically drawn to Camilla Belsize but deliberately stepped aside and brought her together with Teddy Garland.

nodes.label: Forgery substitution method

citations.quote: 'I shall take a sheet of your paper with me, and forge the forgery!' said Raffles, a light in his eye and a gusto in his voice that I knew only too well. 'But I shouldn't do my work as perfectly as--the other cove--did his.'

nodes.description: Raffles's technique in 'Mr Justice Raffles' of taking a sheet of paper and forging a copy of an existing forgery, enabling him to substitute documents and expose or neutralise the original fraud.

nodes.label: Kidnap and coercion to extract legal agreement

citations.quote: In fact, he had foreseen treachery from the first, and the desperate device of kidnapping the traitor proved to have been as deliberate a move as Raffles had ever planned to meet a probable contingency. He had brought down a pair of handcuffs as well as a sufficient supply of Somnol.

nodes.description: Raffles's desperate tactic in 'Mr Justice Raffles' of kidnapping a traitor to compel a legal agreement, a measure he had foreseen and pre-planned against anticipated treachery.

nodes.label: Disguise after return

citations.quote: He had taken a hansom to the Burlington Gardens entrance to the Albany, and kept it waiting while he went in and changed his clothes; then he had sent Barraclough to pay off the cab

nodes.description: Raffles's technique of immediately changing disguise on returning to the Albany — taking a hansom to the Burlington Gardens entrance and altering his appearance inside — used to evade detection in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Somnol sedation method

citations.quote: Raffles had administered his opiate before the patient knew much more about it
'Somnol. The latest thing out, and quite the best.'
'he's full of a judicious blend of his own whiskey and the stuff poor Teddy had the other night'

nodes.description: Somnol sedation method used by A. J. Raffles in Mr. Justice Raffles, described as 'the latest thing out,' administered covertly in whiskey to incapacitate a victim.

nodes.label: Raffles torpedo-beard canoeist disguise

citations.quote: 'didn't I tell you I've been down the river all the time; down the river,' added Raffles, chuckling, 'in a Canadian canoe and a torpedo beard!'

nodes.description: Raffles's torpedo-beard canoeist disguise in Mr. Justice Raffles Chapter XII, in which he poses as a man who has been down the river in a Canadian canoe to explain his movements and evade suspicion.

nodes.label: Raffles as well-known military man at bank

citations.quote: he had issued on this occasion in the living image of a well-known military man about town who was also well known to be a client of Dan Levy's. Raffles said the cashier stared at him, but the cheque was cashed without a word.

nodes.description: Raffles's disguise as a well-known military man about town, used in Chapter XVI of Mr. Justice Raffles to enter a bank without arousing suspicion.

nodes.label: Dan Levy's Jermyn Street office

citations.quote: down we went together to the jaws of Jermyn Street
Dan Levy, as I must try to call him when Raffles is not varnishing my tale, looked a very big man at his enormous desk

nodes.description: Dan Levy's Jermyn Street office, where the moneylender receives clients at an enormous desk and where Raffles and Teddy Garland negotiate a rescue from debt in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Lord's Cricket Ground

citations.quote: The historic sward had just been cleared for action when Raffles and I met at Lord's next day.
Raffles and I met at Lord's next day. I blush to own I had been knave and fool enough to suggest that he should smuggle me into the pavilion

nodes.description: Lord's Cricket Ground, where Raffles and Bunny meet to watch the university match in Mr Justice Raffles, the historic ground that is Raffles's natural social habitat as a celebrated cricketer.

nodes.label: Garland's Kensington house

citations.quote: It stood just off the thoroughfare, in grounds of its own out of all keeping with their metropolitan environment; they ran from one side-street to another
a cross between the two. But the mixture was exactly what attracted these good people

nodes.description: The Garland family's Kensington house set in its own grounds in Mr Justice Raffles, effectively owned by Dan Levy through years of usurious debt, Levy's hold confirmed when he declares the freehold his own.

nodes.label: Dan Levy's riverside house

citations.quote: They were wooden gates without a lodge, yet the house set well beyond them, on the river's brim, was a mansion of considerable size and still greater peculiarity. It was really two houses, large and small, connected by a spine of white posts and joists and glimmering glass.

nodes.description: Dan Levy's substantial mansion set on the riverbank beyond wooden gates, where Raffles is assaulted by Levy and where the foreign assassin ultimately murders Levy in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Empty tower house on the Thames

citations.quote: 'What's this place?' I whispered as Raffles made fast to a post. 'An unoccupied house, Bunny.'
it finishes in a room as sound-proof as old Carlyle's crow's-nest in Cheyne Row

nodes.description: An unoccupied tower house on the Thames, moored to by Raffles and used as a sound-proof location to coerce Dan Levy into signing the mortgage deed in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Gray's Inn Square

citations.quote: Gray's Inn a basin drained to the last dreg of visible humanity. In one moment I passed through gateway and alley from the voices and lights of Holborn into a perfectly deserted square of bare ground and bright stars.

nodes.description: Gray's Inn Square, Fetter Lane, where the offices of the solicitors Burroughs and Burroughs are located and from which Raffles steals a letter in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Carlsbad

citations.quote: The telegram was from Carlsbad, of all places for Raffles of all men!
'The Shylocks were off to Carlsbad the very next day. It was the old man's last orgy before his annual cure'

nodes.description: Carlsbad, the Bohemian spa town where Raffles sends a telegram and where Dan Levy and his family go for an annual cure, the starting point of the events in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Lady Laura Belsize's riverside cottage

citations.quote: One of a semi-detached couple built close to the road, with narrow strips of garden to the river's brim, its dingy stucco front and its green Venetian blinds conveyed no conceivable attraction
the riparian retreat to which Lady Laura Belsize had retired in her impoverished widowhood

nodes.description: Lady Laura Belsize's semi-detached riverside cottage in Mr Justice Raffles, a dingy stucco property near the Thames to which she retired in impoverished widowhood and where her daughter Camilla resides.

nodes.label: Levy emerald necklace

citations.quote: you saw the emeralds under their table when they'd gone, and how _I_ forgot myself and ran after them with the best necklace I'd handled since the days of Lady Melrose

nodes.description: The Levy emerald necklace, a fine piece of jewellery stolen by Raffles at Carlsbad from under Dan Levy's possession, central to the opening of Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Teddy Garland's promissory note

citations.quote: The evil little document was in my possession at last.
He borrowed three hundred and signed a promissory note for four hundred and fifty-six.

nodes.description: Teddy Garland's promissory note in 'Mr Justice Raffles', by which he borrowed three hundred pounds from Dan Levy and signed for four hundred and fifty-six, later retrieved by Raffles as evidence against Levy's usurious practices.

nodes.label: Fact magazine blackmailing letter

citations.quote: 'I'm speaking of the crucial letter in your case against Fact?' 'I am,' said Levy, 'but 'ow did you know I was?'
the letter by which Levy had set such store, for which Raffles had risked so much

nodes.description: The crucial letter held by solicitors Burroughs and Burroughs by which Dan Levy blackmails a client, stolen by Raffles at considerable personal risk as the linchpin of the justice scheme in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Garland mortgage indenture deed

citations.quote: The little document turned out to be a veritable Deed, engrossed on parchment, embossed with a ten-shilling stamp, and duly calling itself an INDENTURE
its eventual plain provision for 'the said sum of £15,000 to remain charged upon the security of the hereditaments'

nodes.description: A parchment mortgage indenture deed signed by Dan Levy in Mr. Justice Raffles while held captive in a tower, charging a £15,000 sum upon the security of the Garland estate.

nodes.label: Raffles's Manila rope

citations.quote: I had hold of Raffles's favourite Manila, which united a silken flexibility with the strength of any hawser. It was tied to the window-post, and it dangled into a room

nodes.description: Raffles's favourite Manila rope in 'Mr Justice Raffles', noted for its silken flexibility combined with the strength of a hawser, used to descend from and ascend to upper-storey windows during burglaries.

nodes.label: Somnol sedative pills

citations.quote: Raffles returned with a tiny bottle from which he was shaking little round black things into his left palm.
'Somnol. The latest thing out, and quite the best.'

nodes.description: Somnol sedative pills in 'Mr Justice Raffles', described by Raffles as the latest and best thing out, shaken from a tiny bottle and used to drug Dan Levy's food or drink before the kidnapping.

nodes.label: Levy's plated revolver

citations.quote: He had opened the top drawer in the right-hand pedestal, and taken therefrom a big bulldog revolver; it was the work of few moments to empty its five chambers
a plated revolver twinkled and flashed in the electric light as Levy drew it from his hip pocket and flourished it in our faces

nodes.description: Dan Levy's plated bulldog revolver in 'Mr Justice Raffles', drawn from a desk pedestal and flourished in the electric light when Raffles and Bunny confront him, then countered by Raffles's physical speed.

nodes.label: Camilla's enamel coat button

citations.quote: And from my waistcoat pocket I produced the missing button of enamel.
'Where did you find it?' inquired Miss Belsize... 'In an empty house not far from here.'

nodes.description: An enamel coat button recovered by Bunny from an empty house in 'Mr Justice Raffles', identified by Miss Belsize as belonging to Camilla and serving as physical evidence of her presence at the scene of Dan Levy's kidnapping.

nodes.label: Teddy's promissory note and instalment receipts

citations.quote: 'I ought to have the receipts for the various instalments you have paid, and perhaps that letter saying it was your last chance.' 'Here they all are,' said Garland, producing a bulky envelope.

nodes.description: Teddy Garland's promissory note and instalment receipts in Mr Justice Raffles, the documentary proof of Dan Levy's usurious lending scheme that Raffles seeks to recover.

nodes.label: Levy's signed confession receipt

citations.quote: 'A plain statement of all he had suborned me to do for him, and what he had given me for doing it,' said Raffles. 'One might almost call it a receipt for the letter I stole and he destroyed.'

nodes.description: A signed confession from Levy acknowledging everything he suborned Raffles's associate to do, obtained by Raffles and used as leverage in Mr Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Levy's cheque for 1,500 guineas

citations.quote: the latest prospect appeared to disturb Mr. Levy less than, for obvious reasons, it did me. Certainly for him it was the lesser of the two evils, and as such he seemed to accept it when he finally wrote out the cheque for fifteen hundred guineas

nodes.description: A cheque for 1,500 guineas from Dan Levy in Mr Justice Raffles, forming part of the financial evidence Raffles assembles to expose Levy's usurious scheme.

nodes.label: Raffles altruistic revenge against Levy

citations.quote: 'Do you suppose I want to be beaten by a brute like Levy, Garlands or no Garlands? Besides, there's far less risk in what I mean to do than in what I've been doing; at all events it's in my line.'

nodes.description: Raffles's stated motive in 'Mr Justice Raffles': refusing to be beaten by a brute like Levy, combined with a practical assessment that kidnapping carried less risk than other methods of settling the debt.

nodes.label: Levy's motive: usury and power over debtors

citations.quote: 'They love you to owe more than you can pay; it's not their principal that they care about nearly so much as your interest; what they hate is to lose you when once they've got you.'

nodes.description: Dan Levy's business motive in 'Mr Justice Raffles': keeping debtors in perpetual arrears because it is the interest rather than the principal that matters to him, giving him continuing power over those he has ensnared.

nodes.label: Foreign assassin's revenge on Levy

citations.quote: 'Levy's treated him scandalously, of course; it all came out in a torrent the other morning.'
'he's threatened me by letter and telegram; so now he thinks he'll come and try it on in person down 'ere.'

nodes.description: The motive in 'Mr Justice Raffles': a foreign assassin's personal grievance against Dan Levy for scandalous treatment, escalating from threatening letters to a direct attempt on Levy's life.

nodes.label: Burroughs and Burroughs solicitors

citations.quote: 'Who are the solicitors to Fact?' 'Burroughs and Burroughs.' 'Of Gray's Inn Square?' 'That's right.' 'The strongest firm in England for a criminal case'

nodes.description: Burroughs and Burroughs, the Gray's Inn Square solicitors described in Mr. Justice Raffles as the strongest firm in London and acting as legal counsel to the blackmail magazine Fact.

nodes.label: Fact magazine

citations.quote: '_Fact_ has exposed the whole thing, and printed blackmailing letters which Shylock swears are forgeries. That's both their cases in a philippine!'

nodes.description: Fact, a magazine in 'Mr Justice Raffles' that exposes the Maharajah of Hathipur affair and prints letters which Dan Levy claims are forgeries.

nodes.label: Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman

citations.quote: Raffles FURTHER ADVENTURES OF THE AMATEUR CRACKSMAN by E. W. Hornung

nodes.description: Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman, an E. W. Hornung short-story collection presenting more of Raffles and Bunny's escapades, including a burglary at the British Museum and a confrontation with the Camorra.

nodes.label: Mr. Maturin (invalid persona)

citations.quote: 'See Mr. Maturin's advertisement _Daily Mail_ might suit you earnestly beg try will speak if necessary'
old Australian without a friend in old country—ordered Engadine as last chance—no go—not an earthly—sentimental wish to die in London—that's the history of Mr. Maturin.
I am dead—I'm at the bottom of the sea—and don't you forget it for a minute.

nodes.description: Raffles's persona as Mr. Maturin, a dying Australian invalid, under which he rents a flat at Earl's Court and advertises for a companion while hiding from the law after his presumed death in No Sinecure.

nodes.label: Ralph (colonial brother persona)

citations.quote: My brother Ralph, who now lived with me on the edge of Ham Common, had come home from Australia with a curious affection of the eyes, due to long exposure to the glare out there

It was owing to our landlady that I took to calling him Ralph, for the first time in our lives, on her beginning to speak of and to him as 'Mr. Raffles.' 'This won't do,' said he to me. 'It's a name that sticks.'

nodes.description: The Ralph colonial persona adopted by Raffles in The Wrong House from Raffles: Further Adventures, presenting himself to their landlady as Bunny's brother Ralph, newly returned from Australia with a curious affliction.

nodes.label: No Sinecure

citations.quote: NO SINECURE
I am still uncertain which surprised me more, the telegram calling my attention to the advertisement, or the advertisement itself.

nodes.description: No Sinecure, a Raffles story in Further Adventures in which Raffles and Bunny return under disguise and carry out a duplicate cigarette-box jewel swindle after their earlier disappearance.

nodes.label: A Jubilee Present

citations.quote: A JUBILEE PRESENT
The Room of Gold, in the British Museum, is probably well enough known to the inquiring alien and the travelled American.

nodes.description: A Jubilee Present, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny steal a gold cup from the British Museum's Room of Gold.

nodes.label: The Fate of Faustina

citations.quote: THE FATE OF FAUSTINA
It was in the early heat of August, and the hour that of the lawful and necessary siesta for such as turn night into day.

nodes.description: The Fate of Faustina, a Raffles story in Further Adventures set near the Bay of Naples in August in which Raffles takes vengeance on Count Corbucci for the murder of a woman named Faustina.

nodes.label: The Last Laugh

citations.quote: THE LAST LAUGH
there was a certain house in Palace Gardens, and when we got there Raffles would pass on.

nodes.description: The Last Laugh, a Raffles story in Further Adventures in which Count Corbucci springs a clock-pistol revenge trap on Raffles, who escapes using a flask of cyanide of cacodyl before Corbucci can trigger the mechanism.

nodes.label: To Catch a Thief

citations.quote: TO CATCH A THIEF
Society persons are not likely to have forgotten the series of audacious robberies by which so many of themselves suffered in turn during the brief course of a recent season.

nodes.description: To Catch a Thief, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles unmasks Lord Ernest Belville as the perpetrator of a series of audacious society house burglaries carried out from the inside.

nodes.label: An Old Flame

citations.quote: AN OLD FLAME
The square shall be nameless, but if you drive due west from Piccadilly the cab-man will eventually find it on his left

nodes.description: An Old Flame, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles stages his own death and funeral to escape a French antagonist named Jacques Saillard.

nodes.label: The Wrong House

citations.quote: THE WRONG HOUSE
My brother Ralph, who now lived with me on the edge of Ham Common, had come home from Australia with a curious affection of the eyes

nodes.description: The Wrong House, a story in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which Raffles and Bunny conduct a series of Thames Valley bicycle burglaries and accidentally break into a house belonging to Bunny's brother Ralph.

nodes.label: The Knees of the Gods

citations.quote: THE KNEES OF THE GODS
'The worst of this war,' said Raffles, 'is the way it puts a fellow off his work.'
It was, of course, the winter before last

nodes.description: The Knees of the Gods, a Raffles story in Further Adventures in which Raffles and Bunny enlist in an irregular horse unit during the Boer War, and Raffles arranges to sabotage a rival soldier's horse.

nodes.label: Dr. Theobald

citations.quote: 'His doctor,' said Theobald, with a satisfied eye. 'Mr. Maturin's doctor. He is having a male nurse and attendant by my advice, and he wants a gentleman if he can get one.'
he was going to be married next month. That's a pity in some ways, but a good thing in others; he will want more money than he foresees, and he may always be of use to us at a pinch.

nodes.description: Dr. Theobald, Mr. Maturin's physician in the Raffles story No Sinecure, who arranges a male nurse and attendant for his patient and is about to be married.

nodes.label: Faustina

citations.quote: I called the girl Eve. Her real name was Faustina, and she was one of a vast family who hung out in a hovel on the inland border of the vineyard.

It was the most exquisite face I ever saw or shall see in this life. Absolutely perfect features; a skin that reminded you of old gold, so delicate was its bronze; magnificent hair, not black but nearly

Dead?... And he nodded to the wall. 'There was not a sound in the cave. There was no answer to my voice. Then I went in, and my foot touched hers, and it was colder than the rock ... Bunny, they had stabbed her to the heart.'

nodes.description: Faustina, a young Italian woman of exceptional beauty from an inland family near the Bay of Naples, who dies tragically at Count Corbucci's vineyard in The Fate of Faustina.

nodes.label: Count Corbucci

citations.quote: the brute who had it in my day... He had the great high sensual Roman nose, eyes that were sinks of iniquity in themselves... a huge gray moustache, like a flying gull, and the most courteous manners even to his men
His very name sounded unhealthy—Corbucci. I suppose I ought to add that he was a Count
This old sinner Corbucci turns out to have been no end of a boss in the Camorra—says so himself. One of the _capi paranze_, my boy, no less

nodes.description: Count Corbucci, a former high-ranking Camorra boss and the antagonist of The Fate of Faustina and The Last Laugh in the Raffles Further Adventures, who plots an elaborate clock-pistol revenge trap against Raffles.

nodes.label: Stefano

citations.quote: Stefano, of all creatures on God's earth—for her! ... he was a miserable little undersized wretch—ill-favored—servile—surly—and second only to his master in bestial cunning and hypocrisy.
He was Corbucci's own confidential body-servant
He was in the cave! I saw him—I spotted him—it was broad twilight after those stairs—and I went for him with my bare hands.

nodes.description: Stefano, Count Corbucci's small, ill-favoured confidential body-servant who betrayed the count's ward Faustina, killed by Raffles in a smoky cave as an act of personal vengeance in The Fate of Faustina.

nodes.label: Lord Ernest Belville

citations.quote: Lord Ernest Belville was at all those functions. Know anything about him, Bunny?
A good-looking, well-built, iron-gray, iron-jawed man; but a fool and a weakling at that moment
the apostle of Rational Drink

nodes.description: Lord Ernest Belville, a good-looking iron-jawed temperance advocate known as the apostle of Rational Drink, exposed by Raffles as the inside man behind a series of society house burglaries in To Catch a Thief.

nodes.label: Jacques Saillard

citations.quote: 'Jacques Saillard,' he said, as though now I must know.
She does man's work over a man's name, has the will of any ten men I ever knew, and I don't mind telling you that I fear her more than any person on God's earth.
a dark and handsome woman whose profile, as I saw it first in the electric light, is cut like a cameo in my memory.

nodes.description: Jacques Saillard, a dark and handsome woman of formidable will who operates under a man's name and whom Raffles fears, encountered by him in An Old Flame.

nodes.label: Corporal Connal

citations.quote: The fellow was a mongrel type, a Glasgow Irishman by birth and upbringing, but he had been in South Africa for years
Connal was a hulking ruffian, and in me had ideal game.

'The fellow is a spy!' I said to Raffles, with no nursery oath, as we strolled within the lines that night. He merely smiled in my face. 'And have you only just found it out, Bunny? I have known it almost ever since we joined'

nodes.description: Corporal Connal, a Glasgow-Irish man long resident in South Africa who acts as a Boer spy in the British lines and sabotages horses, identified by Raffles as a spy in The Knees of the Gods.

nodes.label: Captain Bellingham

citations.quote: Captain Bellingham is best known for his position in the batting averages a year or two ago, and for his subsequent failure to obtain a place in any of the five Test Matches. But I only think of him as the officer who recognized Raffles.
'Peter Bellingham!' gasped Raffles under his breath, and then we saluted and tried to pass on

nodes.description: Captain Bellingham, a cricketer known for his batting averages who appears in The Knees of the Gods and is recognised by Raffles in disguise.

nodes.label: Duplicate cigarette-box jewel swindle

citations.quote: Duplicate boxes, my brainy Bunny. One was already packed and weighted, and in my pocket.
the diamond ring that cost £95, the necklet for £200, and my flaming star at another £100, all three lay safe and snug in the jeweller's own cotton-wool!

nodes.description: Raffles's jewel swindle in No Sinecure, substituting a pre-packed duplicate cigarette-box for a jeweller's open display box to steal a diamond ring, an emerald necklace, and a diamond star worth nearly £400 in total.

nodes.label: British Museum gold cup theft (Jubilee gift)

citations.quote: It was the temptation of a minute—the final impulse of the fraction of a second
It stood for days upon his chimney-piece, this costly trophy whose ancient history and final fate filled newspaper columns
I've sent it to Sir Arthur Bigge, to present to her Majesty, with the loyal respects of the thief

nodes.description: The British Museum gold cup theft (Jubilee gift), a Raffles escapade in Further Adventures in which Raffles steals the ancient gold cup from the Room of Gold on a momentary impulse and eventually sends it to the Queen as a loyal Jubilee present.

nodes.label: Lord Ernest Belville's society burglaries

citations.quote: Raid after raid was made upon the smartest houses in town, and within a few weeks more than one exalted head had been shorn of its priceless tiara. The Duke and Duchess of Dorchester lost half the portable pieces of their historic plate
all of these so-called burglaries has been done from the inside, by one of the guests
The clubs were as hollow as drinking-horns, the pair of them ... the counterpane was glittering with diamonds where it was not shimmering with pearls.

nodes.description: Lord Ernest Belville's series of audacious burglaries at London society houses carried out from the inside as a guest, the stolen diamonds concealed in hollow Indian clubs, exposed by Raffles in To Catch a Thief.

nodes.label: Camorra revenge trap (Corbucci's clock-pistol)

citations.quote: his hands lashed to a ring-bolt high above his head, his toes barely touching the floor, his neck pinioned by a strap passing through smaller ring-bolts under either ear

ticking like a sledge-hammer, its only hand upon the stroke of twelve, stood a simple, old-fashioned, grandfather's clock ... a revolver smoking in a vice screwed below the dial, an arrangement of wires sprouting from the dial itself
He meant catching me before he'd done, and scoring me off in exactly the same way that I scored off him, only going one better of course.

nodes.description: The Camorra revenge trap (Corbucci's clock-pistol), the elaborate revenge scheme in The Last Laugh in which Count Corbucci lashes Raffles to a ring-bolt and constructs a grandfather-clock mechanism set to fire a pistol at midnight.

nodes.label: Thames Valley bicycle burglaries

citations.quote: That was the winter when there were so many burglaries in the Thames Valley from Richmond upward. It was said that the thieves used bicycles in every case
Raffles smeared vaseline upon the plated parts of his Beeston Humber before starting
It proved sufficient to unscrew the bars at the bottom only, and then to wrench them to either side.

nodes.description: A series of bicycle-assisted night burglaries carried out by Raffles across the Thames Valley from Richmond upward one winter, using a greased Beeston Humber bicycle, as recounted in The Wrong House.

nodes.label: Corporal Connal's horse sabotage for Boers

citations.quote: 'The fellow is a spy!'
Raffles pointing downward through the bushes to where Corporal Connal stood with his back to us, shooing a last charger out of the mouth of the donga towards the Boer trenches.

Corporal Connal paid the penalty of his crime before the sun was far above the hill held by the enemy. There was abundance of circumstantial evidence against him ... and the wretch was shot at last with little ceremony and less shrift.

nodes.description: Corporal Connal's act of sabotage on behalf of the Boers by driving British cavalry horses towards the enemy lines, for which he is shot in The Knees of the Gods.

nodes.label: Raffles's vengeance for Faustina (killing of Stefano)

citations.quote: I just stood over him, with the revolver in both hands, feeling the chambers with my thumb; and as I stood he stabbed at me; but I stepped back to that one, and brought him down with a bullet in his guts.
Then I started coughing and wheezing like the Count himself, for the place was full of smoke. When it cleared my man was very dead, and I tipped him into the sea

nodes.description: Raffles's killing of Stefano in Count Corbucci's smoky cave using a revolver, enacted as personal vengeance for Stefano's betrayal of the count's ward Faustina, described in The Fate of Faustina.

nodes.label: Raffles's staged death and funeral (escape from Jacques Saillard)

citations.quote: 'He's not even at the bottom of that grave! He's no more dead than you or I, and a sham burial is his latest piece of villainy!'
Raffles had bought the doctor for a thousand pounds, and the doctor had bought a 'nurse' of his own kidney, on his own account
a library of books (largely acquired for the occasion) had been impiously interred at Kensal Green.

nodes.description: Raffles's elaborately staged death and sham funeral in An Old Flame in the Further Adventures, engineered with a bribed doctor and nurse and a library of books buried at Kensal Green in his place.

nodes.label: Raffles and Bunny enlist in irregular horse for the Boer War

citations.quote: 'The worst of this war,' said Raffles, 'is the way it puts a fellow off his work.'
No, Bunny, we go out to the Cape on our own, and that's where we enlist. One of these regiments of irregular horse is the thing for us
he was a ginger-headed Raffles by the end of January, and it was extraordinary what a difference it made.

nodes.description: The event in which Raffles and Bunny Manders travel to the Cape of their own accord and enlist in a regiment of irregular horse to fight in the Boer War, with Raffles dyeing his hair ginger as a disguise, as described in The Knees of the Gods.

nodes.label: Death of Raffles on the South African veldt

citations.quote: 'It's not only been the best time I ever had, old Bunny, but I'm not half sure—' Of what I can but guess; the sentence was not finished, and never could be in this world.
He was talking all the time, but for my sake, and I knew it. Can you wonder that I could not see an inch beyond him? He was the battle to me then; he is the whole war to me as I look back now.

nodes.description: The death of A. J. Raffles during the Boer War, shot on the South African veldt while serving in an irregular horse unit alongside Bunny, in The Knees of the Gods.

nodes.label: Bunny wounded in the leg on the veldt

citations.quote: The bullet went clean through my thigh, drilling the bone, but happily missing the sciatic nerve
I am more or less lame for life.

nodes.description: Bunny wounded in the leg on the veldt during the Boer War in The Knees of the Gods from Raffles: Further Adventures, the bullet drilling through his thigh bone and leaving him more or less lame for life.

nodes.label: The Camorra

citations.quote: 'The Camorra!' I repeated the word with a different intonation. Not that I had never heard of that most powerful and sinister of secret societies
This old sinner Corbucci turns out to have been no end of a boss in the Camorra—says so himself. One of the _capi paranze_, my boy, no less
half the organ-grinders in London belong, and the whole lot of them were put on my tracks by secret instructions.

nodes.description: The Camorra, a powerful and sinister Neapolitan criminal organisation featuring in Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman, whose London network of organ-grinders was directed by the capo paranzo Count Corbucci to track Raffles.

nodes.label: Earl's Court flat (Raffles's hideout as Mr. Maturin)

citations.quote: The address given in the advertisement was that of a flat at Earl's Court
a man recommended these on the boat; such a good chap, Bunny; he was my reference when it came to signing the lease.

nodes.description: The flat at Earl's Court rented by Raffles under the invalid persona of Mr. Maturin, used as his hiding place and base of operations after his supposed death, in No Sinecure.

nodes.label: The Room of Gold, British Museum

citations.quote: The Room of Gold, in the British Museum, is probably well enough known to the inquiring alien and the travelled American.
number 43, upstairs and sharp round to the right
A hundred and thirty-nine yards from this to the open street

nodes.description: The Room of Gold in the British Museum, the setting of Raffles's audacious jubilee gold-cup theft in A Jubilee Present in the Further Adventures, approximately 139 yards from the open street.

nodes.label: Corbucci's vineyard near Bay of Naples

citations.quote: The place was a vineyard, but it overhung the sea, and I got taken on as tame sailorman and emergency bottle-washer.
the house above was said to be on the identical site of a suburban retreat of the admirable Tiberius
a wedge of rock sticking out into the bay, thatched with vines, and with the rummiest old house on the very edge of all

nodes.description: Count Corbucci's vineyard near the Bay of Naples, a sea-girt wedge of rock thatched with vines on the site of a reputed retreat of Tiberius, where the young Italian woman Faustina meets her fate in The Fate of Faustina.

nodes.label: Ham Common cottage (Thames Valley base)

citations.quote: My brother Ralph, who now lived with me on the edge of Ham Common
It was an ideal cottage, as I said when I found it, and in it our delicate man became rapidly robust.

nodes.description: A cottage on the edge of Ham Common in the Thames Valley rented by Bunny and his brother Ralph, which Raffles uses as a base for his bicycle burglaries after his return from the Boer War in The Wrong House.

nodes.label: South Africa / Boer War veldt

citations.quote: No, Bunny, we go out to the Cape on our own, and that's where we enlist.
We became strategists. We knew exactly what Buller was to do on landing
so there we lay together on the veldt, under blinding sun and withering fire

nodes.description: The South African veldt during the Boer War, where Raffles and Bunny serve in an irregular horse unit and where Raffles is fatally shot under withering fire in The Knees of the Gods.

nodes.label: Gold Cup from the Room of Gold (Jubilee present to Queen Victoria)

citations.quote: It's as thin as paper, and enamelled like a middle-aged lady of quality! But, by Jove, it's one of the most beautiful things I ever saw in my life, Bunny.
It stood for days upon his chimney-piece, this costly trophy
I've sent it to Sir Arthur Bigge, to present to her Majesty, with the loyal respects of the thief

nodes.description: A thin, enamel-decorated gold cup from the Museum's Room of Gold, stolen by Raffles in 'A Jubilee Present' and anonymously returned to Queen Victoria via Sir Arthur Bigge as the loyal respects of the thief.

nodes.label: Lord Belville's hollow Indian clubs (jewel hiding-place)

citations.quote: The clubs were as hollow as drinking-horns, the pair of them
'They're not heavy enough for their size,' said he rapidly; 'and I'll take my oath they're not the same weight!'
the counterpane was glittering with diamonds where it was not shimmering with pearls.

nodes.description: Lord Ernest Belville's hollow Indian exercise clubs in 'To Catch a Thief', which Raffles discovers are not the same weight and proves on opening to be filled with diamonds and pearls rather than solid wood.

nodes.label: Flask of cyanide of cacodyl (Raffles's last resort poison)

citations.quote: the deadliest poison yet known to science. It is cyanide of cacodyl, and I have carried that small flask of it about with me for months.
a bottle of this stuff is calculated to stiffen an ordinary roomful of ordinary people within five minutes
That beast Corbucci! ... He took the flask; he would give me none; he flicked me in the face instead ... he thought it brandy; he must take it downstairs to drink to my destruction!

nodes.description: A small flask of cyanide of cacodyl carried by Raffles as a last resort in 'The Last Laugh', described as the deadliest poison known to science, calculated to kill an ordinary roomful of people within five minutes.

nodes.label: Grandfather clock with concealed revolver mechanism (Corbucci's revenge device)

citations.quote: ticking like a sledge-hammer, its only hand upon the stroke of twelve, stood a simple, old-fashioned, grandfather's clock
I saw a revolver smoking in a vice screwed below the dial, an arrangement of wires sprouting from the dial itself, and the single hand at once at its zenith and in contact with these.
Electric connection. Simple enough. Hour-hand only

nodes.description: Count Corbucci's revenge device in 'The Last Laugh': a grandfather clock fitted with an electric mechanism connecting the hour-hand to a revolver mounted in a vice below the dial, set to fire at the stroke of twelve.

nodes.label: Raffles's patriotic motive (Boer War enlistment)

citations.quote: 'Dye for my country,' he cried, swelling. 'Dulce et decorum est, Bunny, my boy!'
'I am fighting, sir,' said he, as simply as any subaltern in the army.

nodes.description: Raffles's motive in 'The Knees of the Gods': a straightforward patriotic desire to serve his country during the Boer War, expressed in the motto 'Dulce et decorum est' when he and Bunny enlist in an irregular unit.

nodes.label: Corbucci's Camorra vendetta against Raffles

citations.quote: He meant catching me before he'd done, and scoring me off in exactly the same way that I scored off him, only going one better of course.
If I killed him the Camorra would certainly kill me; he kept on telling me so

nodes.description: Count Corbucci's vendetta against Raffles in 'The Last Laugh', sworn to repay in kind the humiliation Raffles inflicted on him, backed by the Camorra's threat to kill Raffles if he killed Corbucci first.

nodes.label: Raffles's invalid/bath-chair disguise method

citations.quote: I'm dead—I'm at the bottom of the sea—and don't you forget it for a minute.
Raffles and I drove to Kew Gardens in a hired landau ... The porter had assisted me to carry my invalid downstairs, in a carrying-chair hired (like the landau) from Harrod's Stores for the occasion.
I simply dare not show my nose out of doors—by day.

nodes.description: Raffles's concealment method in 'No Sinecure' of posing as an invalid in a bath-chair to move about in daylight while officially presumed dead and unable to show his face outdoors.

nodes.label: Raffles's duplicate cigarette-box switch technique

citations.quote: Duplicate boxes, my brainy Bunny. One was already packed and weighted, and in my pocket. I don't know whether you noticed me weighing the three things together in my hand?
I know that neither of you saw me change the boxes

nodes.description: A. J. Raffles's technique of switching a genuine cigarette box for a pre-packed duplicate, used in the No Sinecure jewel swindle without either victim noticing the exchange.

nodes.label: Bicycle-assisted Thames Valley night burglary method

citations.quote: Raffles rode a Beeston Humber, a Royal Sunbeam was good enough for me, but he insisted on our both having Dunlop tires.
Raffles smeared vaseline upon the plated parts of his Beeston Humber before starting
Raffles went armed these nights with a better light than gas ... the electric torch, fitted by Raffles with a dark hood to fulfil the functions of a slide.

nodes.description: Bicycle-assisted night burglary method used by A. J. Raffles in the Thames Valley, involving Beeston Humber bicycles with Dunlop tyres, vaseline-coated plated parts to avoid reflection, and an electric torch fitted with a dark hood.

nodes.label: Raffles's hair turned white (from Mediterranean swim and Italian exile)

citations.quote: His hair was white; there was no trick about that
you have often asked me what turned my hair gray, and now you are going to hear.
I have often thought it was that long swim that started it.

nodes.description: In the Further Adventures, Raffles's hair has turned completely white — no trick — a change he attributes to the long Mediterranean swim after his staged death and Italian exile.

nodes.label: Bunny served prison time (18 months) before this collection opens

citations.quote: I bolted my skilly at Wormwood Scrubbs, and tightened my belt in a Holloway attic
One does not do eighteen months for nothing.

nodes.description: In the Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman, Bunny has already served eighteen months of imprisonment (at Wormwood Scrubbs and a Holloway attic) before the new stories begin.

nodes.label: The world and police believe Raffles drowned in the Mediterranean

citations.quote: I tell you I am dead; and my one terror is of coming to life again by accident.
if I went to Scotland Yard this minute, to give myself up, they'd chuck me out for a harmless lunatic.
'His favorite name was Raffles then.' 'It was his real name,' I said, indignantly. 'And he has been dead for years.'

nodes.description: In the Further Adventures, Raffles lives concealed, knowing the world and Scotland Yard alike believe him drowned in the Mediterranean, his name belonging to a dead man.

nodes.label: His Last Bow

citations.quote: His Last Bow / by Arthur Conan Doyle
Several previous experiences which have lain long in my portfolio have been added to His Last Bow so as to complete the volume.

nodes.description: His Last Bow, an Arthur Conan Doyle short-story collection that includes Holmes's wartime counter-espionage mission against the German agent Von Bork and several earlier untold cases.

nodes.label: Arthur Conan Doyle

citations.quote: His Last Bow / by Arthur Conan Doyle
THE CASE-BOOK OF SHERLOCK HOLMES BY ARTHUR CONAN DOYLE
ARTHUR CONAN DOYLE.

by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle

THE VALLEY OF FEAR / By Sir Arthur Conan Doyle

nodes.label: Sherlock Holmes saga

citations.quote: The friends of Mr. Sherlock Holmes will be glad to learn that he is still alive and well, though somewhat crippled by occasional attacks of rheumatism.
Holmes made his début in A Study in Scarlet and in The Sign of Four... It was in 1891 that 'A Scandal in Bohemia,' the first of the long series of short stories, appeared in The Strand Magazine.
THE MEMOIRS OF SHERLOCK HOLMES by Arthur Conan Doyle

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: Mr. Sherlock Holmes was a very busy man

The only unofficial consulting detective. I am the last and highest court of appeal in detection. When Gregson or Lestrade or Athelney Jones are out of their depths—which, by the way, is their normal state—the matter is laid before me.
You have heard me speak of Professor Moriarty?

nodes.description: The Sherlock Holmes saga, the complete body of detective fiction by Arthur Conan Doyle featuring Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson, encompassing four novels and five short-story collections.

nodes.label: Mycroft Holmes
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans

citations.section: The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans
citations.quote: Well, well! What next? Brother Mycroft is coming round.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans
citations.quote: Mycroft has his rails and he runs on them. His Pall Mall lodgings, the Diogenes Club, Whitehall—that is his cycle.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans
citations.quote: All other men are specialists, but his specialism is omniscience.
"Because my brother Mycroft possesses it in a larger degree than I do."
Mycroft Holmes was a much larger and stouter man than Sherlock.
The Diogenes Club is the queerest club in London, and Mycroft one of the queerest men.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: I had only one confidant—my brother Mycroft.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: Mycroft had preserved my rooms and my papers exactly as they had always been

nodes.description: Mycroft Holmes, Sherlock Holmes's elder brother, a government official of omniscient knowledge whose work takes him between his Pall Mall lodgings, the Diogenes Club, and Whitehall, and who occasionally assists in critical cases.

nodes.label: Von Bork

nodes.label: The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax

nodes.description: The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax, a His Last Bow story in which Holmes investigates the vanishing of a wealthy English lady, eventually tracing her to Henry Peters (Holy Peters) and a drugged-coffin scheme.

nodes.description: Wisteria Lodge near Esher and Oxshott in Surrey in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge in His Last Bow, home of the murdered Aloysius Garcia and the focal point of Holmes's investigation into the fugitive San Pedro dictator.

nodes.description: Before the events of His Last Bow, Holmes had retired to a small farm on the South Downs five miles from Eastbourne, dividing his time between philosophy and bee-farming.

nodes.description: Kitty Winter, a woman wronged by Baron Adelbert Gruner who throws vitriol in his face and faces a grave criminal charge, in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.

nodes.label: Shinwell Johnson
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: Johnson, I grieve to say, made his name first as a very dangerous villain and served two terms at Parkhurst. Finally, he repented and allied himself to Holmes, acting as his agent in the huge criminal underworld of London

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: I rather fancy that Shinwell Johnson might be a help.

nodes.description: Shinwell Johnson, a reformed criminal who served two terms at Parkhurst and now acts as Holmes's informant in the London underworld, assisting in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.

nodes.label: Sir James Damery
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: Sir James Damery presents his compliments to Mr. Sherlock Holmes

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: He has rather a reputation for arranging delicate matters which are to be kept out of the papers.

nodes.description: Sir James Damery, a discreet intermediary who presents the compliments of an illustrious but unnamed client to Holmes in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client, noted for his ability to keep delicate matters out of the papers.

nodes.label: James M. Dodd
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: I find from my notebook that it was in January, 1903, just after the conclusion of the Boer War, that I had my visit from Mr. James M. Dodd, a big, fresh, sunburned, upstanding Briton.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: 'From South Africa, sir, I perceive.' 'Yes, sir,' he answered, with some surprise.

nodes.description: James M. Dodd, a South African veteran who visits Holmes in January 1903 just after the Boer War in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier to investigate the disappearance of his friend Godfrey Emsworth.

nodes.label: Godfrey Emsworth
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: young Godfrey Emsworth had joined the same squadron. He was Colonel Emsworth's only son

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: 'Don't touch me, Jimmie. Keep your distance. Yes, you may well stare! I don't quite look the smart Lance-Corporal Emsworth, of B Squadron, do I?'

nodes.description: Godfrey Emsworth, Colonel Emsworth's only son and a Lance-Corporal who returned from the Boer War displaying symptoms that alarmed his family and led to his concealment in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier.

nodes.label: Colonel Emsworth
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: He is a hard nail, is Colonel Emsworth. The greatest martinet in the Army in his day

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: Emsworth, the Crimean V.C.

nodes.description: Colonel Emsworth, a Crimean V.C. described as the greatest martinet in the army in his day, who secretly confines his son Godfrey at home in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier.

nodes.label: Sir James Saunders
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: I foresaw this situation, and I have brought with me a friend whose discretion may absolutely be trusted. I was able once to do him a professional service, and he is ready to advise as a friend rather than as a specialist. His name is Sir James Saunders.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: It is not leprosy. A well-marked case of pseudo-leprosy or ichthyosis

nodes.description: Sir James Saunders, the dermatologist brought in by Holmes in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier who diagnoses Godfrey Emsworth's condition not as leprosy but as pseudo-leprosy or ichthyosis.

nodes.label: Count Negretto Sylvius
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: Sylvius is the name--Count Negretto Sylvius. Write it down, man, write it down! 136 Moorside Gardens, N.W.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: The famous game-shot, sportsman, and man-about-town was a big, swarthy fellow, with a formidable dark moustache

nodes.description: Count Negretto Sylvius, a big, swarthy man-about-town and noted game-shot living at 136 Moorside Gardens who steals the Mazarin Stone (crown diamond), pursued by Holmes in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone.

nodes.label: Sam Merton
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: The other is Sam Merton, the boxer. Not a bad fellow, Sam, but the Count has used him. Sam's not a shark. He is a great big silly bull-headed gudgeon.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: The prize-fighter, a heavily built young man with a stupid, obstinate, slab-sided face, stood awkwardly at the door

nodes.description: Sam Merton, a heavily built but not malicious prize-fighter who is duped into assisting Count Negretto Sylvius in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone, described by Holmes as not a shark but a great strong fool.

nodes.label: Lord Cantlemere
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: there is Lord Cantlemere---- He's a stiff 'un, sir, if I may say so. I can get along with the Prime Minister, and I've nothing against the Home Secretary

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: Too bad, Lord Cantlemere, too bad! My old friend here will tell you that I have an impish habit of practical joking.

nodes.description: Lord Cantlemere, a stiff and sceptical government representative in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone who is subjected to Holmes's practical joke of producing the recovered diamond from Cantlemere's own coat pocket.

nodes.label: Isadora Klein
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Three Gables

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: I think it would perhaps be as well that you should come with me, for it is safer to have a witness when you are dealing with such a lady as Isadora Klein.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: She was pure Spanish, the real blood of the masterful Conquistadors, and her people have been leaders in Pernambuco for generations. She married the aged German sugar king, Klein

nodes.description: Isadora Klein, a wealthy woman of pure Spanish blood in The Adventure of the Three Gables who schemes to purchase and destroy the manuscript that fictionalises her affair with Douglas Maberley, in order to protect her advantageous marriage prospects.

nodes.label: Mary Maberley
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Three Gables

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: Here is Mrs. Maberley's note. 'DEAR MR. SHERLOCK HOLMES,--I have had a succession of strange incidents occur to me in connection with this house'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: I believe that my late husband, Mortimer Maberley, was one of your early clients.

nodes.description: Mary Maberley, the widow of Mortimer Maberley — an early client of Holmes — who experiences a series of strange incidents at Three Gables and consults Holmes in The Adventure of the Three Gables.

nodes.label: Steve Dixie
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Three Gables

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: 'I've wanted to meet you for some time,' said Holmes. 'I won't ask you to sit down, for I don't like the smell of you, but aren't you Steve Dixie, the bruiser?'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: He is one of the Spencer John gang and has taken part in some dirty work of late

nodes.description: Steve Dixie, a member of the Spencer John gang who visits Holmes in a threatening manner in The Adventure of the Three Gables and has been involved in dirty work directed by Isadora Klein.

nodes.label: Robert Ferguson
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire

citations.section: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire
citations.quote: Our client, Mr. Robert Ferguson, of Ferguson and Muirhead, tea brokers, of Mincing Lane

citations.section: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire
citations.quote: 'Hullo, Watson,' said he, and his voice was still deep and hearty. 'You don't look quite the man you did when I threw you over the ropes into the crowd at the Old Deer Park.'

nodes.description: Robert Ferguson, a tea broker of Ferguson and Muirhead in Mincing Lane and a former rugby acquaintance of Watson's, who consults Holmes about his wife's strange behaviour in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire.

nodes.label: Mrs. Ferguson (Peruvian wife)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire

citations.section: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire
citations.quote: This gentleman married some five years ago a Peruvian lady, the daughter of a Peruvian merchant

citations.section: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire
citations.quote: Your wife is a very good, a very loving, and a very ill-used woman.

nodes.description: Mrs. Ferguson, the Peruvian wife of Robert Ferguson in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire, who is wrongly suspected of vampirism but whom Holmes declares a very good, loving, and ill-used woman.

nodes.label: Jack Ferguson
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire

citations.section: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire
citations.quote: I am more uneasy about poor little Jack, for, as I told you in my note, he has twice been assaulted by her.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire
citations.quote: It was not the chain that broke, but it was the collar that slipped... His very soul is consumed with hatred for this splendid child, whose health and beauty are a contrast to his own weakness.

nodes.description: Jack Ferguson, the son of Robert Ferguson's first marriage in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire, whose intense hatred for his Peruvian stepmother leads him to secretly poison the baby.

nodes.label: Killer Evans
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs
citations.quote: He is none other than 'Killer' Evans, of sinister and murderous reputation.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs
citations.quote: James Winter, alias Morecroft, alias Killer Evans, was the inscription below. Aged forty-four. Native of Chicago.

nodes.description: Killer Evans, also known as James Winter and Morecroft, a forty-four-year-old Chicago-born murderer who impersonates a Garrideb claimant to gain access to a concealed counterfeiting press, investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs.

nodes.label: Nathan Garrideb
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs
citations.quote: 'Garrideb, N.,' he read, '136 Little Ryder Street, W.'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs
citations.quote: Mr. Nathan Garrideb proved to be a very tall, loose-jointed, round-backed person, gaunt and bald, some sixty-odd years of age. He had a cadaverous face, with the dull dead skin of a man to whom exercise was unknown.

nodes.description: Nathan Garrideb, a tall, loose-jointed, round-backed collector of some sixty years living at 136 Little Ryder Street, who is deceived by the Three Garridebs scheme and whose house conceals a counterfeiting press in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs.

nodes.label: Neil Gibson

citations.quote: You have heard of Neil Gibson, the Gold King? 'You mean the American Senator?'
I can't see the best woman God ever made go to her death without doing all that is possible to save her.

nodes.description: Neil Gibson, the Gold King and American senator in The Problem of Thor Bridge, who implores Holmes to save his wife's governess Grace Dunbar from a murder charge.

nodes.label: Grace Dunbar

citations.quote: Then came Miss Grace Dunbar. She answered our advertisement and became governess to our two children.
I had expected from all that we had heard to see a beautiful woman, but I can never forget the effect which Miss Dunbar produced upon me.

nodes.description: Grace Dunbar, the governess to Neil Gibson's two children who becomes the object of his affection and is framed for the murder of Maria Gibson at Thor Bridge, in The Problem of Thor Bridge.

nodes.label: Maria Gibson

citations.quote: I met my wife when I was gold-hunting in Brazil. Maria Pinto was the daughter of a Government official at Manaos, and she was very beautiful.
She adored me in those English woods as she had adored me twenty years ago on the banks of the Amazon.

nodes.description: Maria Gibson, née Maria Pinto, the Brazilian-born wife of Gold King Neil Gibson whose jealousy of the governess drives her to shoot herself on Thor Bridge and frame the governess with a planted note, as revealed by Holmes in The Problem of Thor Bridge.

nodes.label: Professor Presbury
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Creeping Man

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: The Professor, Watson, is a man of European reputation. His life has been academic. There has never been a breath of scandal. He is a widower with one daughter, Edith.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: He was crawling, Mr. Holmes--crawling! He was not quite on his hands and knees.

nodes.description: Professor Presbury, a man of blameless European academic reputation whose nocturnal crawling on all fours is investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Creeping Man, where it is revealed he has been secretly self-injecting langur monkey serum in search of rejuvenation.

nodes.label: Trevor Bennett
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Creeping Man

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: this gentleman, Mr. Trevor Bennett, is professional assistant to the great scientist, lives under his roof, and is engaged to his only daughter.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: He was a tall, handsome youth about thirty, well dressed and elegant

nodes.description: Trevor Bennett, a tall, handsome professional assistant to the great scientist Professor Presbury in The Adventure of the Creeping Man, who lives under the professor's roof and is engaged to his daughter.

nodes.label: Fitzroy McPherson
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
