citations.quote: Fitzroy McPherson was the science master, a fine upstanding young fellow whose life had been crippled by heart trouble following rheumatic fever.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: he threw up his hands, and, with a terrible cry, fell upon his face... He was obviously dying.

nodes.description: Fitzroy McPherson, the science master at Harold Stackhurst's coaching establishment in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane, a fine young man whose life had been crippled by heart trouble, who dies suddenly crying out with his hands raised.

nodes.label: Harold Stackhurst
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: Half a mile off, however, is Harold Stackhurst's well-known coaching establishment. The Gables

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: He and I were always friendly from the day I came to the coast, and he was the one man who was on such terms with me that we could drop in on each other in the evenings without an invitation.

nodes.description: Harold Stackhurst, proprietor of the well-known coaching establishment The Gables, who is Holmes's closest neighbour on the Sussex coast and a trusted friend in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane.

nodes.label: Maud Bellamy
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: there were several which showed an intimate correspondence with a certain Miss Maud Bellamy, of Fulworth.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: Maud Bellamy will always remain in my memory as a most complete and remarkable woman.

nodes.description: Maud Bellamy, a remarkable woman of Fulworth with whom Fitzroy McPherson conducted an intimate correspondence, remembered by Holmes as a most complete and remarkable woman in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane.

nodes.label: Ian Murdoch
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: Ian Murdoch was the mathematical coach at the establishment, a tall, dark, thin man, so taciturn and aloof that none can be said to have been his friend.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: 'And incidentally exonerates me,' remarked Ian Murdoch with a wry smile.

nodes.description: Ian Murdoch, the tall, dark, taciturn mathematical coach at Harold Stackhurst's establishment who is initially a suspect in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane before being exonerated.

nodes.label: Eugenia Ronder
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger

citations.section: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger
citations.quote: 'A poor wounded beast that has crawled into its hole to die--that is the end of Eugenia Ronder.'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger
citations.quote: Mrs. Ronder has been your lodger for seven years and that you have only once seen her face. 'And I wish to God I had not!'

nodes.description: Eugenia Ronder, a circus performer who conspired with Leonardo the strongman to murder her brutal husband using a lion-paw club, and later lives as a veiled recluse, in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger.

nodes.label: Leonardo the strongman
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger

citations.section: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger
citations.quote: 'That is Leonardo,' she said. 'Leonardo, the strongman, who gave evidence?'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger
citations.quote: He was clearly a professional acrobat, a man of magnificent physique, taken with his huge arms folded across his swollen chest

nodes.description: Leonardo, the magnificently built circus strongman and professional acrobat who fashions a lion-paw club to kill Ronder and is implicated in Eugenia Ronder's scheme, in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger.

nodes.label: Sir Robert Norberton
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: What about Sir Robert Norberton? Does the name recall anything? 'Well, I should say so. He lives at Shoscombe Old Place'

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: He is about the most daredevil rider in England--second in the Grand National a few years back.

nodes.description: Sir Robert Norberton, one of the most daredevil riders in England and second in the Grand National, who conceals his dead sister's body to prevent forfeiture of a racing loan in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place.

nodes.label: Lady Beatrice Falder
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: He lives with his widowed sister, Lady Beatrice Falder.

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: my sister, Lady Beatrice, for everything... my sister did die just a week ago.

nodes.description: Lady Beatrice Falder, the widowed sister of Sir Robert Norberton who controls the Shoscombe estate, and whose death is secretly concealed by her brother in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place.

nodes.label: Josiah Amberley
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: Josiah Amberley. He says he was junior partner of Brickfall and Amberley, who are manufacturers of artistic materials.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: The old colourman had the strength of a lion in that great trunk of his, but he was helpless in the hands of the two experienced man-handlers.

nodes.description: Josiah Amberley, the retired colourman and murderer in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman who, in a jealous rage, kills his wife and her lover in a gas-chamber he constructs in his Essex property before feigning their disappearance.

nodes.label: Dr. Ray Ernest
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: Not far from him at Lewisham there lives a young doctor who is also a chess-player. I have noted his name as Dr. Ray Ernest.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: Ernest was frequently in the house, and an intimacy between him and Mrs. Amberley was a natural sequence

nodes.description: Dr. Ray Ernest, a young chess-playing doctor from Lewisham who carries on an intimacy with Mrs. Amberley and is murdered alongside her by Josiah Amberley in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.

nodes.label: Holmes as narrator (self-narrated stories)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier / The Adventure of the Lion's Mane

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier / The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: The ideas of my friend Watson, though limited, are exceedingly pertinacious. For a long time he has worried me to write an experience of my own.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier / The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: It is a most singular thing that a problem... should have come to me after my retirement... Thus I must act as my own chronicler.

evidence_refs: corpus/sherlock-holmes/the-case-book-of-sherlock-holmes/text.txt#The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier / The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
nodes.description: Holmes as narrator, a self-narrated role taken by Sherlock Holmes in two Case Book stories — The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier and The Adventure of the Lion's Mane — when Watson was unavailable and Holmes was obliged to write up the cases himself.

nodes.label: Illustrious Client case (Baron Gruner)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: I obtained permission to put on record what was, in some ways, the supreme moment of my friend's career.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: we are dealing on this occasion, Mr. Holmes, with a man to whom violence is familiar and who will, literally, stick at nothing.

nodes.description: The Illustrious Client case, described in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client as the supreme moment of Holmes's career, in which he works to prevent a young woman from marrying the dangerous and murderous Baron Gruner.

nodes.label: Blanched Soldier / Godfrey Emsworth case
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: The following case can hardly fail to do so, as it is among the strangest happenings in my collection

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: It is not leprosy. A well-marked case of pseudo-leprosy or ichthyosis, a scale-like affection of the skin, unsightly, obstinate, but possibly curable, and certainly non-infective.

nodes.description: The Blanched Soldier case, narrated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier, in which he investigates Godfrey Emsworth's mysterious isolation and determines he has pseudo-leprosy (ichthyosis), not true leprosy.

nodes.label: Mazarin Stone theft case
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: 'It's this case of the Crown diamond.' 'What--the hundred-thousand-pound burglary?'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: Yes, the great yellow Mazarin stone. I've cast my net and I have my fish. But I have not got the stone.

nodes.description: The Mazarin Stone theft case, the crime in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone in which the hundred-thousand-pound Crown diamond is stolen by Count Negretto Sylvius, recovered by Holmes through the use of a wax dummy and hidden surveillance.

nodes.label: Thor Bridge mystery (Maria Gibson death)

citations.quote: The wife was found in the grounds nearly half a mile from the house, late at night, clad in her dinner dress, with a shawl over her shoulders and a revolver bullet through her brain.
She had the note upon her making an appointment at that very place and signed by the governess.

nodes.description: The Thor Bridge mystery, in The Problem of Thor Bridge, in which Holmes investigates the death of Maria Gibson found shot on a bridge with a note from the governess, revealing it to be a suicide staged to frame an innocent woman.

nodes.label: Creeping Man / Professor Presbury case
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Creeping Man

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: the true history of this curious case remained entombed in the tin box which contains so many records of my friend's adventures

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: He was crawling, Mr. Holmes--crawling! He was not quite on his hands and knees.

nodes.description: The Creeping Man case, in The Adventure of the Creeping Man, in which Holmes investigates Professor Presbury's alarming nocturnal crawling behaviour on a nine-day cycle and discovers he has been self-injecting a langur monkey rejuvenation serum.

nodes.label: Lion's Mane / McPherson death case
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: He uttered two or three words with an eager air of warning. They were slurred and indistinct, but to my ear the last of them, which burst in a shriek from his lips, were 'the lion's mane.'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: 'Cyanea!' I cried. 'Cyanea! Behold the Lion's Mane!'

nodes.description: The Lion's Mane case, narrated by the retired Holmes from his Sussex cottage at Fulworth, in which he investigates the mysterious agonised death of Fitzroy McPherson and identifies a Cyanea capillata jellyfish as the killer.

nodes.label: Retired Colourman / Amberley double murder
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: 'What did you do with the bodies?' The man sprang to his feet with a hoarse scream.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: The bodies cannot be far away. Try the cellars and the garden. It should not take long to dig up the likely places.

nodes.description: The Retired Colourman case, in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman, in which Holmes exposes Josiah Amberley for gassing his wife and her lover Dr. Ray Ernest to death in a sealed strong-room and burying them in his garden.

nodes.label: Vitriol attack on Baron Gruner
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: An arm--a woman's arm--shot out from among the leaves. At the same instant the Baron uttered a horrible cry... The vitriol was eating into it everywhere and dripping from the ears and the chin.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: the first police-court hearing of the proceedings against Miss Kitty Winter on the grave charge of vitriol-throwing

nodes.description: Kitty Winter's vitriol attack on Baron Adelbert Gruner, throwing the acid in his face from among the leaves outside his house, leading to her police-court prosecution in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.

nodes.label: Crown diamond (Mazarin Stone) theft
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: The stone is here in my secret pocket. I take no chances leaving it about. It can be out of England to-night and cut into four pieces in Amsterdam before Sunday.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: With a single spring Holmes had leaped from the dummy's chair and had grasped the precious jewel.

nodes.description: The theft of the Mazarin Stone, a great Crown diamond, by Count Negretto Sylvius in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone, who carries the stone in a secret pocket intending to have it cut and smuggled out of England before Holmes can recover it.

nodes.label: Three Gables burglary (manuscript theft)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Three Gables

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: Mrs. Maberley was chloroformed and the house was----

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: She had planned to burn the manuscript: 'There was a calcined mass which she broke up with the poker.'

nodes.description: The Three Gables burglary in The Adventure of the Three Gables, in which Mrs. Maberley is chloroformed and the house ransacked on behalf of Isadora Klein in order to steal and destroy a manuscript.

nodes.label: Three Garridebs counterfeit press scheme
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs
citations.quote: 'A printing press--a counterfeiter's outfit,' said Holmes. 'Yes, sir,' said our prisoner... 'The greatest counterfeiter London ever saw. That's Prescott's machine'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs
citations.quote: He wanted to get this good old fossil up to Birmingham... The whole of this Garrideb invention was apparently for no other end.

nodes.description: The Three Garridebs counterfeit press scheme in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs, devised by Killer Evans to lure Nathan Garrideb away from his house in order to recover the hidden counterfeiting press beneath it.

nodes.label: Thor Bridge suicide framing scheme

citations.quote: Her first resolution was to end her own life. Her second was to do it in such a way as to involve her victim in a fate which was worse far than any sudden death could be.
A note was extracted very cleverly from Miss Dunbar which would make it appear that she had chosen the scene of the crime.

nodes.description: Maria Gibson's scheme to frame Grace Dunbar for her own suicide at Thor Bridge, arranging a false note from Miss Dunbar and staging the scene to implicate her rival, in The Problem of Thor Bridge.

nodes.label: Professor Presbury langur serum self-experiment
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Creeping Man

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: 'Langur,' he read, 'the great black-faced monkey of the Himalayan slopes, biggest and most human of climbing monkeys.'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: It is possible that the Serum of Anthropoid would have been better. I have, as I explained to you, used black-faced Langur because a specimen was accessible.

nodes.description: Professor Presbury's clandestine self-experimentation with a langur monkey serum from Lowenstein, causing him to exhibit creeping animal behaviour, investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Creeping Man.

nodes.label: Ronder murder / lion-paw club scheme
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger

citations.section: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger
citations.quote: We made a club--Leonardo made it--and in the leaden head he fastened five long steel nails, the points outwards, with just such a spread as the lion's paw. This was to give my husband his death-blow, and yet to leave the evidence that it was the lion which we would loose who had done the deed.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger
citations.quote: I heard the crash as the club smashed my husband's skull.

nodes.description: Eugenia Ronder and Leonardo's murder of Mr. Ronder using a leaden club fitted with five long steel nails to simulate a lion's paw attack, inside the circus at night in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger.

nodes.label: Shoscombe Old Place corpse concealment scheme
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: my sister did die just a week ago... her maid's husband--this man here--is an actor. It came into our heads... that he could for that short period personate my sister.

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: the body could not remain there... We carried the body to the crypt of the church.

nodes.description: Sir Robert Norberton's scheme to conceal his sister's natural death by moving her body to the church crypt and using an actor to impersonate her, to avoid losing a racing loan, in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place.

nodes.label: Amberley gas-chamber double murder
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: The pipe runs out into the strong-room... and ends in that plaster rose in the centre of the ceiling... At any moment by turning the outside tap the room could be flooded with gas.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: Just above the skirting is scribbled with a purple indelible pencil: 'We we----' He was writing, 'We were murdered.'

nodes.description: Josiah Amberley's double murder of his wife and Dr. Ray Ernest by feeding gas through a pipe into his sealed strong-room at The Haven in Lewisham, the victims leaving a dying scrawl 'We we----' on the wall, as solved by Holmes in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.

nodes.label: Baron Gruner's love/lust diary
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: It's a book he has--a brown leather book with a lock, and his arms in gold on the outside.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: Here is the book the woman talked of. If this will not break off the marriage, nothing ever could. It is his own writing.

nodes.description: Baron Gruner's locked brown leather diary containing his own written record of crimes against women, stolen by Holmes and used to break his engagement in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.

nodes.label: Ming dynasty saucer (distraction prop)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: Holmes handed me a card upon which was printed: 'Dr. Hill Barton, 369 Half Moon Street.'... you will say that you are bringing him a specimen of an absolutely unique set of Ming china.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: This is the real egg-shell pottery of the Ming dynasty. No finer piece ever passed through Christie's.

nodes.description: A genuine Ming dynasty egg-shell saucer used by Holmes as a distraction prop to gain entry to Baron Gruner's house in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.

nodes.label: The Mazarin Stone (Crown diamond)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: Yes, the great yellow Mazarin stone.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: In an instant the amazed peer was standing, blinking and stammering, with the great yellow stone on his shaking palm.

nodes.description: The great yellow Mazarin Stone, a Crown diamond stolen by Count Sylvius and recovered by Holmes in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone by switching it while the count played the violin.

nodes.label: Wax dummy of Holmes
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: There was a facsimile of his old friend, dressing-gown and all, the face turned three-quarters towards the window and downwards, as though reading an invisible book

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: 'It's a pretty little thing,' said Holmes, advancing towards the image. 'Tavernier, the French modeller, made it.'

nodes.description: A wax bust of Holmes made by the French modeller Tavernier, used in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone to deceive Count Sylvius into thinking Holmes was seated by the window while Holmes moved freely about.

nodes.label: Douglas Maberley's manuscript (roman à clef)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Three Gables

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: 'You have noticed the number on the top of the page. It is two hundred and forty-five. Where are the odd two hundred and forty-four pages?'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: He wrote a book in which he described his own story. I, of course, was the wolf; he the lamb. It was all there, under different names, of course

nodes.description: Douglas Maberley's roman à clef manuscript in The Adventure of the Three Gables, in which he described his own story of betrayal under disguised names, providing Isadora Klein's motive for stealing it.

nodes.label: Curare-tipped arrows (Sussex Vampire)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire

citations.section: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire
citations.quote: When I saw that little empty quiver beside the small bird-bow, it was just what I expected to see. If the child were pricked with one of those arrows dipped in curare or some other devilish drug, it would mean death if the venom were not sucked out.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire
citations.quote: A South American household. My instinct felt the presence of those weapons upon the wall before my eyes ever saw them.

nodes.description: Curare-tipped arrows from a small South American blowpipe, discovered by Holmes in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire as the true source of the infant's wounds, not the mother's bite.

nodes.label: Prescott's counterfeiting press and forged notes
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs
citations.quote: 'A printing press--a counterfeiter's outfit,' said Holmes.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Garridebs
citations.quote: 'those bundles on the table are two thousand of Prescott's notes worth a hundred each and fit to pass anywhere.'

nodes.description: A counterfeiter's printing press and two thousand forged Prescott banknotes hidden beneath a floorboard in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs, the object of Nathan Garrideb's elaborate ruse.

nodes.label: Chipped parapet at Thor Bridge

citations.quote: The stonework was grey, but at this one point it showed white for a space not larger than a sixpence. When examined closely one could see that the surface was chipped as by a sharp blow.
'Was there ever a more exact demonstration?' he cried. 'See, Watson, your revolver has solved the problem!'

nodes.description: A chip in the stone parapet at Thor Bridge in The Problem of Thor Bridge, made by a pistol tied to a weight that pulled it into the water after firing, proving Maria Gibson staged her own death.

nodes.label: Cyanea Capillata (Lion's Mane jellyfish)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: 'Cyanea!' I cried. 'Cyanea! Behold the Lion's Mane!'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: Cyanea Capillata is the miscreant's full name, and he can be as dangerous to life as, and far more painful than, the bite of the cobra.

nodes.description: Cyanea capillata, the Lion's Mane jellyfish, identified by Holmes as the creature responsible for Fitzroy McPherson's death, described as more dangerous and painful than a biting reptile, in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane.

nodes.label: Prussic acid bottle (Eugenia Ronder's temptation)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger

citations.section: The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger
citations.quote: he pointed with some pride to a small blue bottle upon his mantelpiece... 'Prussic acid?' said I. 'Exactly. It came by post. I send you my temptation. I will follow your advice.'

nodes.description: A small blue bottle of prussic acid kept by Eugenia Ronder in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger, a temptation Holmes defuses by persuading her to choose to live.

nodes.label: Hidden gas pipe in Amberley's strong-room
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: You see the gas-pipe along the skirting here... The pipe runs out into the strong-room, as you can see, and ends in that plaster rose in the centre of the ceiling

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: That was our first clue. You can thank Dr. Watson's observation for that... Why should this man at such a time be filling his house with strong odours? Obviously, to cover some other smell

nodes.description: A hidden gas pipe running along the skirting into Josiah Amberley's strong-room, discovered by Holmes as the murder weapon used to asphyxiate his wife and her companion in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.

nodes.label: Systematic observation and logical deduction
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: That process starts upon the supposition that when you have eliminated all which is impossible, then whatever remains however improbable, must be the truth.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: I see no more than you, but I have trained myself to notice what I see.

nodes.description: Sherlock Holmes's systematic method of eliminating the impossible so that whatever remains must be the truth, combined with training himself to notice what he sees, applied in the Blanched Soldier case.

nodes.label: Holmes's undercover / disguise surveillance
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: Yesterday he was out as a workman looking for a job. To-day he was an old woman. Fairly took me in, he did

citations.section: The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone
citations.quote: I was never more convincing. He actually picked up my parasol for me once.

nodes.description: Holmes's undercover disguise and surveillance technique used in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone, where he successively posed as a workman and an old woman, convincingly deceiving those under observation.

nodes.label: Microscopic evidence analysis
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: Sherlock Holmes had been bending for a long time over a low-power microscope. 'It is glue, Watson,' said he. 'Unquestionably it is glue.'

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: Since I ran down that coiner by the zinc and copper filings in the seam of his cuff they have begun to realize the importance of the microscope.

nodes.description: Microscopic evidence analysis used by Sherlock Holmes in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place to identify substances such as glue from crime-scene samples.

nodes.label: Nine-day interval cycle deduction (Creeping Man)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Creeping Man

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: This excellent young man's diary shows that there was trouble upon July 2, and from then onwards it seems to have been at nine-day intervals

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: Let us, then, form the provisional theory that every nine days the Professor takes some strong drug which has a passing but highly poisonous effect.

nodes.description: A deduction method used by Sherlock Holmes in The Adventure of the Creeping Man, identifying a nine-day interval pattern in Professor Presbury's erratic behaviour to suggest regular ingestion of a powerful drug.

nodes.label: Box-office chart verification (Retired Colourman)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: I was already certain that the case was serious, for I had examined the box-office chart at the Haymarket Theatre... and ascertained that neither B thirty nor thirty-two of the upper circle had been occupied that night.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: Therefore, Amberley had not been to the theatre, and his alibi fell to the ground.

nodes.description: Alibi-breaking technique used by Sherlock Holmes in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman, verifying the box-office chart at the Haymarket Theatre to prove that Amberley had not attended the performance he claimed as his alibi.

nodes.label: Gruner's scheme to protect advantageous marriage
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: The Baron has little waxed tips of hair under his nose... 'I have been fortunate enough to win the entire affection of this lady.'

citations.section: The Adventure of the Illustrious Client
citations.quote: He proposes to marry her next month.

nodes.description: Baron Adelbert Gruner's motive in 'The Adventure of the Illustrious Client': safeguarding his engagement to a wealthy young woman by suppressing evidence of his criminal past, including the 'lust diary' that catalogued his crimes against women.

nodes.label: Isadora Klein's motive to suppress manuscript
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Three Gables

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: I am about to marry the young Duke of Lomond... His Grace's ma might overlook the age, but a big scandal would be a different matter

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Gables
citations.quote: He wrote a book in which he described his own story. I, of course, was the wolf; he the lamb.

nodes.description: Isadora Klein's motive in 'The Adventure of the Three Gables': suppressing a roman-à-clef manuscript written by a former lover who portrayed her as the wolf, to prevent a scandal that would endanger her impending marriage to the young Duke of Lomond.

nodes.label: Maria Gibson's jealousy / suicide-as-revenge motive

citations.quote: Her first resolution was to end her own life. Her second was to do it in such a way as to involve her victim in a fate which was worse far than any sudden death could be.
There is a soul-jealousy that can be as frantic as any body-jealousy

nodes.description: Maria Gibson's motive in 'The Problem of Thor Bridge': a frantic soul-jealousy over her husband's love for Miss Dunbar that drove her to contrive her own suicide in such a way as to make the governess appear guilty of murder.

nodes.label: Professor Presbury's rejuvenation motive
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Creeping Man

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: The real source lies, of course, in that untimely love affair which gave our impetuous Professor the idea that he could only gain his wish by turning himself into a younger man.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: He became engaged to the daughter of Professor Morphy, his colleague in the chair of Comparative Anatomy. It was not... the reasoned courting of an elderly man, but rather the passionate frenzy of youth

nodes.description: Professor Presbury's motive in 'The Adventure of the Creeping Man': an untimely love affair with the much younger daughter of his colleague Professor Morphy, driving him to seek Lowenstein's langur serum to appear younger.

nodes.label: Amberley's jealousy and miser's revenge
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: Like all misers, he was a jealous man, and his jealousy became a frantic mania. Rightly or wrongly, he suspected an intrigue. He determined to have his revenge, and he planned it with diabolical cleverness.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: He has, to a high degree, the sort of mind which one associates with the mediaeval Italian nature

nodes.description: Josiah Amberley's motive in 'The Adventure of the Retired Colourman': a frantic, miser-like jealousy over his wife's suspected infidelity that drove him to a mediaeval Italian-style revenge.

nodes.label: Thor Bridge

citations.quote: This bridge--a single broad span of stone with balustraded sides--carries the drive over the narrowest part of a long, deep, reed-girt sheet of water. Thor Mere it is called.
In the mouth of the bridge lay the dead woman.

nodes.description: Thor Bridge, a single broad stone span across a narrow lake at the Gibson estate in The Problem of Thor Bridge in the Case Book, where Maria Gibson's body is found and a chip in the parapet proves her suicide-as-murder scheme.

nodes.label: Camford (university town)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Creeping Man

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: Monday morning found us on our way to the famous University town

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: Professor Presbury of Camford had need of my services.

nodes.description: Camford, the fictional university town in 'The Adventure of the Creeping Man' where Professor Presbury holds his chair and to which Holmes and Watson travel to investigate the professor's disturbing nocturnal behaviour.

nodes.label: Fulworth, Sussex (Holmes's retirement coast)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: My villa is situated upon the southern slope of the Downs, commanding a great view of the Channel.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: the little cove and village of Fulworth break the line.

nodes.description: The village of Fulworth on the Sussex coast in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane, near Holmes's retirement villa on the southern Downs commanding a broad view of the Channel.

nodes.label: Shoscombe Old Place
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: What about Sir Robert Norberton? Does the name recall anything? 'Well, I should say so. He lives at Shoscombe Old Place'

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: the famous Shoscombe stud and training quarters are to be found there.

nodes.description: Shoscombe Old Place, Sir Robert Norberton's famous racing estate in The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place in the Case Book, where Holmes uncovers a scheme to conceal a death until after a key Epsom race.

nodes.label: The Haven, Lewisham (Amberley's house)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: 'The Haven is the name of Mr. Josiah Amberley's house,' I explained.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Retired Colourman
citations.quote: He made his little pile, retired from business at the age of sixty-one, bought a house at Lewisham

nodes.description: The Haven in Lewisham in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman, Josiah Amberley's retirement house that conceals the gas-chamber strong-room where he murdered his wife and Dr Ray Ernest.

nodes.label: Lowenstein's langur serum
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Creeping Man

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: It is possible that the Serum of Anthropoid would have been better. I have, as I explained to you, used black-faced Langur because a specimen was accessible.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Creeping Man
citations.quote: Lowenstein of Prague! Lowenstein with the wondrous strength-giving serum, tabooed by the profession because he refused to reveal its source.

nodes.description: Lowenstein of Prague's langur-monkey serum in 'The Adventure of the Creeping Man', tabooed by the medical profession because its maker refused to reveal the formula, self-administered by Professor Presbury to arrest ageing.

nodes.label: Shoscombe Prince (racehorse)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: I believe Shoscombe Prince and the Derby have turned his brain. 'That is a colt you are running?' 'The best in England, Mr. Holmes.'

citations.section: The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place
citations.quote: Shoscombe Prince did win the Derby, the sporting owner did net eighty thousand pounds in bets

nodes.description: The racehorse Shoscombe Prince in 'The Adventure of Shoscombe Old Place', the best colt in England according to his trainer, who ultimately wins the Derby and nets his owner eighty thousand pounds in bets.

nodes.label: Holmes narrates The Blanched Soldier himself
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: The ideas of my friend Watson, though limited, are exceedingly pertinacious. For a long time he has worried me to write an experience of my own.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
citations.quote: And here it is that I miss my Watson. By cunning questions and ejaculations of wonder he could elevate my simple art

nodes.description: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier is narrated by Holmes himself at Watson's urging, noting wryly that without Watson's ejaculations of wonder he misses an element of artful presentation.

nodes.label: Holmes narrates The Lion's Mane himself (post-retirement)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: It is a most singular thing that a problem... should have come to me after my retirement... Thus I must act as my own chronicler.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: At this period of my life the good Watson had passed almost beyond my ken.

nodes.description: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane is narrated directly by Holmes from his Sussex retirement, acting as his own chronicler since Watson had passed almost beyond his ken by that period.

nodes.label: Holmes retired to Sussex coast with bees
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: It occurred after my withdrawal to my little Sussex home, when I had given myself up entirely to that soothing life of Nature

citations.section: The Adventure of the Lion's Mane
citations.quote: My house is lonely. I, my old housekeeper, and my bees have the estate all to ourselves.

nodes.description: By the time of The Adventure of the Lion's Mane, Holmes had withdrawn entirely to his little Sussex home near Fulworth, with only an old housekeeper and his bees for company.

nodes.label: The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes

citations.quote: THE MEMOIRS OF SHERLOCK HOLMES
by Arthur Conan Doyle

nodes.description: The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, an Arthur Conan Doyle short-story collection that includes the first appearance of Professor Moriarty and culminates in Holmes's apparent death at the Reichenbach Falls.

nodes.label: Professor Moriarty

citations.quote: "You have probably never heard of Professor Moriarty?" said he.
He is the Napoleon of crime, Watson. He is the organizer of half that is evil and of nearly all that is undetected in this great city.
He is extremely tall and thin, his forehead domes out in a white curve

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: I had little doubt that I had come to the end of my career when I perceived the somewhat sinister figure of the late Professor Moriarty standing upon the narrow pathway

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: Professor James Moriarty, who had one of the great brains of the century
the greatest schemer of all time, the organizer of every deviltry, the controlling brain of the underworld
I can tell a Moriarty when I see one. This crime is from London, not from America.

nodes.description: Professor Moriarty, the 'Napoleon of crime' in the Sherlock Holmes stories, an extremely tall and thin criminal mastermind who organises much of London's undetected crime and becomes Holmes's most dangerous adversary.

nodes.label: Silver Blaze

citations.quote: I. Silver Blaze
"Silver Blaze," said he, "is from the Isonomy stock, and holds as brilliant a record as his famous ancestor."

nodes.description: Silver Blaze, a Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes story in which Holmes investigates the disappearance of the racehorse Silver Blaze and the murder of its trainer John Straker, solving the case partly through the curious incident of the dog in the night.

nodes.label: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
nodes.source_location: II. The Adventure of the Cardboard Box

citations.section: II. The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: II. The Adventure of the Cardboard Box

citations.section: II. The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: Miss Susan Cushing, living at Cross Street, Croydon, has been made the victim of what must be regarded as a peculiarly revolting practical joke

evidence_refs: corpus/sherlock-holmes/the-memoirs-of-sherlock-holmes/text.txt#II. The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
nodes.description: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box, a story in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes and Lestrade investigate the sending of two severed human ears to Miss Susan Cushing of Cross Street, Croydon, with Jim Browner identified as the killer.

nodes.label: The Yellow Face

citations.quote: III. The Yellow Face
I am the more interested in this case, Watson, because it is one where, as in the investigations which you have chronicled under the names of 'A Study in Scarlet'

nodes.description: The Yellow Face, a story in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes notable as one of Holmes's rare failures, in which his confident but mistaken deductions about Grant Munro's wife Effie and the yellow-masked figure in the cottage are corrected by the truth.

nodes.label: The Stockbroker's Clerk

citations.quote: IV. The Stockbroker's Clerk
Shortly after my marriage I had bought a connection in the Paddington district.

nodes.description: The Stockbroker's Clerk, a Sherlock Holmes story from The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Watson, newly settled in Paddington, investigates the suspicious employment of Hall Pycroft.

nodes.label: The Gloria Scott

citations.quote: V. The "Gloria Scott"
A few seconds later a roar like thunder burst upon our ears, and as the smoke thinned away there was no sign left of the _Gloria Scott_.

nodes.description: The Gloria Scott, the fifth story in 'The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes', in which Holmes recounts his earliest case involving a convict ship, mutiny, and a coded message that destroys an old man's peace.

nodes.label: The Musgrave Ritual

citations.quote: VI. The Musgrave Ritual
"These," said he, "are all that I have left to remind me of the adventure of the Musgrave Ritual."

nodes.description: The Musgrave Ritual, a Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes story in which Holmes decodes an ancient family ritual document at Hurlstone Manor in Sussex, leading to the discovery of the English Crown of Charles I and the fate of butler Richard Brunton.

nodes.label: The Reigate Squires

citations.quote: VII. The Reigate Squires
"Arrest these men, Inspector!" he gasped. "On what charge?" "That of murdering their coachman, William Kirwan!"

nodes.description: The Reigate Squires, a Sherlock Holmes story from The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes instructs Inspector Lestrade to arrest two men for the murder of their coachman, William Kirwan.

nodes.label: The Crooked Man

citations.quote: VIII. The Crooked Man
"Who killed Colonel James Barclay, then?" "It was a just providence that killed him."

nodes.description: The Crooked Man, a story in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the death of Colonel James Barclay and concludes that a just providence—and the sudden appearance of the disfigured Henry Wood—killed him.

nodes.label: The Resident Patient

citations.quote: IX. The Resident Patient
"Blessington has committed suicide!" Holmes whistled. "Yes, he hanged himself during the night."

nodes.description: The Resident Patient, the ninth story in 'The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes', in which a patient named Blessington is found hanged in his room, a suicide that Holmes exposes as murder by former associates.

nodes.label: The Greek Interpreter

citations.quote: X. The Greek Interpreter
"I have asked Mr. Melas to step across," said he. "He lodges on the floor above me, and I have some slight acquaintance with him"

nodes.description: The Greek Interpreter, a story in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes first introduces Mycroft Holmes and investigates the abduction of the Greek interpreter Mr. Melas, forced to serve as unwitting intermediary in a criminal scheme.

nodes.label: The Naval Treaty

citations.quote: XI. The Naval Treaty
"This," said he, taking a grey roll of paper from his bureau, "is the original of that secret treaty between England and Italy"

nodes.description: The Naval Treaty, a Sherlock Holmes story from The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the theft of a secret treaty between England and a foreign power, recovered on behalf of Percy Phelps.

nodes.label: The Final Problem

citations.quote: XII. The Final Problem
"You have probably never heard of Professor Moriarty?" said he.
It was the last that I was ever destined to see of him in this world.

nodes.description: The Final Problem, the story in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes first names Professor Moriarty as the Napoleon of Crime and both men plunge into the Reichenbach Falls, leaving Watson to believe Holmes dead.

nodes.label: Silver Blaze disappearance and trainer murder

citations.quote: the singular disappearance of the favourite for the Wessex Cup, and the tragic murder of its trainer
"I presume that you have looked into this matter of the murder of John Straker and the disappearance of Silver Blaze?"

nodes.description: The Silver Blaze case, in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Holmes investigates the disappearance of the Wessex Cup favourite Silver Blaze and the murder of its trainer John Straker at King's Pyland stables on Dartmoor.

nodes.label: Cardboard Box severed ears mystery
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box

citations.section: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: a small packet, wrapped in brown paper, was handed in by the postman. A cardboard box was inside, which was filled with coarse salt. On emptying this, Miss Cushing was horrified to find two human ears

nodes.description: The Cardboard Box severed ears mystery in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, in which Sherlock Holmes investigates the delivery of a cardboard box filled with two human ears.

nodes.label: Theft of the Naval Treaty

citations.quote: "All was exactly as I left it, save only that the papers which had been committed to my care had been taken from the desk on which they lay. The copy was there, and the original was gone."

nodes.description: The theft of a secret Anglo-Italian naval treaty from Percy Phelps's desk at the Foreign Office, investigated by Sherlock Holmes in The Naval Treaty.

nodes.label: Moriarty criminal organisation investigation

citations.quote: For years past I have continually been conscious of some power behind the malefactor, some deep organizing power which forever stands in the way of the law
In three days—that is to say, on Monday next—matters will be ripe, and the Professor, with all the principal members of his gang, will be in the hands of the police.

nodes.description: Sherlock Holmes's investigation of Professor Moriarty's criminal organisation in The Final Problem, uncovering a deep organising power behind half the evil in London and planning a coordinated arrest for the following Monday.

nodes.label: John Straker

citations.quote: The trainer, John Straker, is a retired jockey who rode in Colonel Ross's colours before he became too heavy for the weighing-chair.
the dead body of the unfortunate trainer. His head had been shattered by a savage blow from some heavy weapon

nodes.description: John Straker, the retired jockey turned trainer at King's Pyland who conspires to secretly lame Silver Blaze for betting gain and is fatally struck by the horse's hoof, in Silver Blaze.

nodes.label: Colonel Ross

citations.quote: Colonel Ross, the well-known sportsman
"I have always found him an excellent servant." — Colonel Ross on Straker

nodes.description: Colonel Ross, the well-known sportsman and owner of the racehorse Silver Blaze in the Sherlock Holmes story of the same name, who praised his trainer Straker as an excellent servant.

nodes.label: Fitzroy Simpson

citations.quote: His name, it appears, was Fitzroy Simpson. He was a man of excellent birth and education, who had squandered a fortune upon the turf
bets to the amount of five thousand pounds had been registered by him against the favourite

nodes.description: Fitzroy Simpson, a well-born man who has squandered a fortune on betting and wagered five thousand pounds against Silver Blaze, making him a suspect in the horse's disappearance in Silver Blaze.

nodes.label: Jim Browner
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box

citations.section: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: "I presume that this Jim Browner, the steward of a Liverpool boat, is the man whom you suspect?"

citations.section: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: "You can hang me, or you can leave me alone. I don't care a plug which you do."

nodes.description: Jim Browner, a Liverpool steward who murders his unfaithful wife and her lover Alec Fairbairn and sends their severed ears in a cardboard box, in The Adventure of the Cardboard Box.

nodes.label: Grant Munro

citations.quote: "Kindly let me have the facts, Mr. Munro," said Holmes
"I am a hop merchant myself, and as I have an income of seven or eight hundred"

nodes.description: Grant Munro, a hop merchant earning seven or eight hundred a year who consults Holmes about his wife Effie's secret in The Yellow Face.

nodes.label: Effie Munro

citations.quote: "Effie loves me. Don't let there be any mistake about that. She loves me with her whole heart and soul"
"Her name then was Mrs. Hebron. She went out to America when she was young, and lived in the town of Atlanta"

nodes.description: Effie Munro, née Hebron, who had lived in Atlanta before her marriage to Grant Munro and whose devotion to her husband is central to the misunderstanding in The Yellow Face.

nodes.label: Hall Pycroft

citations.quote: "My client is outside in a four-wheeler."
"I used to have a billet at Coxon & Woodhouse, of Drapers' Gardens"

nodes.description: Hall Pycroft, a former clerk at Coxon and Woodhouse of Drapers' Gardens who is lured into a fraudulent job offer, becoming the client in The Stockbroker's Clerk.

nodes.label: Reginald Musgrave

citations.quote: Reginald Musgrave looked at me in astonishment. 'Now that you call it to my mind,' he answered, 'Brunton _did_ ask me about the height of the tree'

nodes.description: Reginald Musgrave, the owner of Hurlstone and keeper of the Musgrave family ritual, who consults Holmes about the disappearance of his butler Brunton in The Musgrave Ritual.

nodes.label: Richard Brunton

citations.quote: 'The butler is gone,' said she.
you caught him [Brunton] consulting the Ritual by candlelight

nodes.description: Richard Brunton, the butler at Hurlstone Manor who is caught consulting the Musgrave Ritual by candlelight and subsequently disappears, in The Musgrave Ritual.

nodes.label: Colonel James Barclay

citations.quote: It is the supposed murder of Colonel Barclay, of the Royal Mallows, at Aldershot
he lay with his feet tilted over the side of an armchair, and his head upon the ground near the corner of the fender, was lying the unfortunate soldier stone dead in a pool of his own blood

nodes.description: Colonel James Barclay, commanding officer of the Royal Mallows at Aldershot, found dead under suspicious circumstances in The Crooked Man — he had fallen with his feet over an armchair and his head on the ground near the fender.

nodes.label: Henry Wood

citations.quote: "Mr. Henry Wood, late of India, I believe," said Holmes, affably.
"You see me now with my back like a camel and my ribs all awry, but there was a time when Corporal Henry Wood was the smartest man in the 117th Foot."

nodes.description: Henry Wood, a former Corporal known as 'the smartest man in the regiment' who returned from India disfigured and crooked, the 'Crooked Man' at the centre of the Holmes story of that name.

nodes.label: Mr. Melas

citations.quote: "Mr. Melas is a Greek by extraction, as I understand, and he is a remarkable linguist. He earns his living partly as interpreter in the law courts"
"I do not believe that the police credit me—on my word, I do not"

nodes.description: Mr. Melas, a Greek interpreter who earns his living partly as interpreter and partly as guide to wealthy Orientals, and who is kidnapped and held captive in The Greek Interpreter from The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.

nodes.label: Percy Phelps

citations.quote: "I was, as Watson may have told you, in the Foreign Office, and through the influences of my uncle, Lord Holdhurst, I rose rapidly to a responsible position."
"You have saved my honour." Phelps seized his hand and kissed it.

nodes.description: Percy Phelps, a Foreign Office clerk in The Naval Treaty who receives the secret treaty through his uncle Lord Holdhurst's influence and whose honour is saved when Holmes recovers it.

nodes.label: Straker's plot to maim Silver Blaze

citations.quote: it is possible to make a slight nick upon the tendons of a horse's ham, and to do it subcutaneously, so as to leave absolutely no trace.
Straker was leading a double life, and keeping a second establishment.

nodes.description: Trainer John Straker's plot to secretly nick the tendons of racehorse Silver Blaze to throw the Wessex Cup, motivated by debts from keeping a secret mistress, as uncovered by Holmes in Silver Blaze.

nodes.label: Moriarty's criminal empire

citations.quote: He is the Napoleon of crime, Watson. He is the organizer of half that is evil and of nearly all that is undetected in this great city.
Is there a crime to be done, a paper to be abstracted, we will say, a house to be rifled, a man to be removed—the word is passed to the Professor, the matter is organized and carried out.

nodes.description: Professor Moriarty's criminal empire in The Final Problem, described by Holmes as the Napoleon of crime who organises nearly all the evil that goes undetected in London, directing every crime at one remove through his web of agents.

nodes.label: Theft of the naval treaty document

citations.quote: "All was exactly as I left it, save only that the papers which had been committed to my care had been taken from the desk on which they lay."
he had thrust it into his pocket and was gone

nodes.description: The theft of the secret naval treaty between England and Italy from the Foreign Office in 'The Naval Treaty', in which the document is snatched from Phelps's desk while he briefly left the room.

nodes.label: Browner's double murder of wife and lover
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box

citations.section: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: "We know who the author of the revolting business is"

citations.section: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: "She had some reason to be disgusted with me now … And then this Alec Fairbairn chipped in, and things became a thousand times blacker."

nodes.description: Jim Browner's double murder of his unfaithful wife Mary and her lover Alec Fairbairn, the severed ears of both victims being sent in a cardboard box to Miss Cushing, in The Adventure of the Cardboard Box.

nodes.label: Holmes and Moriarty at Reichenbach Falls

citations.quote: Two lines of footmarks were clearly marked along the farther end of the path, both leading away from me. There were none returning.
It was the last that I was ever destined to see of him in this world.

nodes.description: The confrontation between Sherlock Holmes and Professor Moriarty at the Reichenbach Falls in Switzerland, from which two sets of footmarks lead toward the edge but none return, ending with both men's apparent deaths in The Final Problem.

nodes.label: Moriarty confronts Holmes at 221B Baker Street

citations.quote: I was sitting in my room thinking the matter over, when the door opened and Professor Moriarty stood before me.
"You must drop it, Mr. Holmes," said he, swaying his face about. "You really must, you know."

nodes.description: Professor Moriarty's direct visit to Holmes at 221B Baker Street in The Final Problem, demanding he drop his investigation and warning of lethal consequences if he does not.

nodes.label: Observation and deductive reasoning

citations.quote: "The difficulty is to detach the framework of fact—of absolute undeniable fact—from the embellishments of theorists and reporters."
"See the value of imagination. It is the one quality which Gregory lacks. We imagined what might have happened, acted upon the supposition, and find ourselves justified."
Observation tells me that you have a little reddish mould adhering to your instep...So much is observation. The rest is deduction.
How often have I said to you that when you have eliminated the impossible whatever remains, however improbable, must be the truth?

nodes.description: Sherlock Holmes's core investigative method, articulated in 'Silver Blaze', of separating absolute undeniable fact from theorists' embellishments and then applying imagination to reconstruct what happened.

nodes.label: The curious incident of the dog in the night-time

citations.quote: "To the curious incident of the dog in the night-time." "The dog did nothing in the night-time." "That was the curious incident," remarked Sherlock Holmes.

nodes.description: Holmes's celebrated deduction from a dog that did not bark in the night — proving the stable intruder was known to the animal — which identifies the trainer John Straker as the culprit in Silver Blaze.

nodes.label: Handwriting analysis to detect two authors and family relation

citations.quote: "it has been written by two persons doing alternate words"
"There is something in common between these hands. They belong to men who are blood-relatives."

nodes.description: Holmes's analysis of the Reigate quarrel note to determine it was written by two people alternating words, and that the two hands share family characteristics showing the writers are blood-relatives, in The Reigate Squires.

nodes.label: Geometric decoding of the Musgrave Ritual

citations.quote: "If a rod of six feet threw a shadow of nine, a tree of sixty-four feet would throw one of ninety-six"
"I proceeded to step, having first taken the cardinal points by my pocket-compass."

nodes.description: Geometric decoding of shadow lengths and compass bearings used by Sherlock Holmes in The Musgrave Ritual to locate the hidden crown of Charles I by following the ancient family ritual's instructions.

nodes.label: Anatomical ear comparison to identify family victim
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box

citations.section: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: "Each ear is as a rule quite distinctive and differs from all other ones. In last year's _Anthropological Journal_ you will find two short monographs from my pen upon the subject."

citations.section: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: "when on looking at Miss Cushing I perceived that her ear corresponded exactly with the female ear which I had just inspected"

nodes.description: Holmes's forensic method of identifying the recipient of a cardboard box by comparing the anatomical characteristics of the enclosed severed ear with Miss Cushing's own ear, establishing a family connection in The Adventure of the Cardboard Box.

nodes.label: Silver Blaze (racehorse)

citations.quote: "Silver Blaze," said he, "is from the Isonomy stock, and holds as brilliant a record as his famous ancestor."
"A child would know Silver Blaze, with his white forehead and his mottled off-foreleg."

nodes.description: Silver Blaze, a famous racehorse of Isonomy stock with a white forehead and mottled off-foreleg in 'Silver Blaze', stolen and hidden by his trainer Straker as part of a scheme to maim him for betting profit.

nodes.label: Crown of King Charles I

citations.quote: "They have the crown down at Hurlstone—though they had some legal bother and a considerable sum to pay before they were allowed to retain it."

nodes.description: The crown of King Charles I, concealed beneath Hurlstone Manor and recovered through the Musgrave Ritual in 'The Musgrave Ritual', subsequently retained by the Musgrave family after legal proceedings and the payment of a considerable sum.

nodes.label: Naval Treaty document (England-Italy)

citations.quote: "This," said he, taking a grey roll of paper from his bureau, "is the original of that secret treaty between England and Italy of which, I regret to say, some rumours have already got into the public press."

nodes.description: A grey roll of paper constituting the secret treaty between England and Italy in 'The Naval Treaty', stolen from the Foreign Office and recovered by Holmes.

nodes.label: Two severed human ears in a cardboard box
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box

citations.section: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: "Miss Cushing was horrified to find two human ears, apparently quite freshly severed."

citations.section: The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: "You have observed, of course, that the ears are not a pair."

nodes.description: A cardboard box sent to Miss Cushing in 'The Adventure of the Cardboard Box' containing two freshly severed but mismatched human ears, which Holmes uses to trace the murder of Jim Browner's wife and her lover.

nodes.label: Musgrave family ritual document

citations.quote: 'Whose was it?' 'His who is gone.' 'Who shall have it?' 'He who will come.' 'Where was the sun?' 'Over the oak.'

nodes.description: The ancient Musgrave family ritual document — a cryptic question-and-answer verse beginning 'Whose was it? His who is gone' — which, decoded by Holmes, reveals the hiding place of the Crown of King Charles I in The Musgrave Ritual.

nodes.label: Dartmoor / King's Pyland stables

citations.quote: "Go! Where to?" "To Dartmoor; to King's Pyland."
the north of Dartmoor

nodes.description: Dartmoor and King's Pyland training stables in Silver Blaze in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, the moorland setting where the champion racehorse vanishes and trainer John Straker is found dead.

nodes.label: Reichenbach Falls, Switzerland

citations.quote: the falls of Reichenbach, which are about half-way up the hill
It is indeed, a fearful place. The torrent, swollen by the melting snow, plunges into a tremendous abyss

nodes.description: The Reichenbach Falls in Switzerland in The Final Problem in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, the fearful torrent-swollen abyss into which Holmes and Moriarty both fall during their struggle on the ledge.

nodes.label: Diogenes Club, Pall Mall

citations.quote: "The Diogenes Club is the queerest club in London, and Mycroft one of the queerest men. He's always there from quarter to five to twenty to eight."
No member is permitted to take the least notice of any other one.

nodes.description: The Diogenes Club on Pall Mall in The Greek Interpreter in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, the eccentric club where Mycroft Holmes spends his fixed daily hours and where members are forbidden to address one another.

nodes.label: Hurlstone Manor, Sussex

citations.quote: "The same afternoon saw us both at Hurlstone. Possibly you have seen pictures and read descriptions of the famous old building"

nodes.description: Hurlstone Manor, the famous ancient manor house in Sussex that is the seat of the Musgrave family and the site of the buried Crown of King Charles I uncovered by Sherlock Holmes in The Musgrave Ritual.

nodes.label: Moriarty criminal syndicate

citations.quote: "He is the Napoleon of crime, Watson. He is the organizer of half that is evil and of nearly all that is undetected in this great city."
"you are now playing a double-handed game with me against the cleverest rogue and the most powerful syndicate of criminals in Europe"

nodes.description: Professor Moriarty's criminal syndicate in 'The Final Problem', described by Holmes as the organiser of half that is evil and of nearly all that is undetected in London, directed by Moriarty from a central position of apparent respectability.

nodes.label: Holmes as aged Italian priest (Final Problem)

citations.quote: I found that the porter, in spite of the ticket, had given me my decrepit Italian friend as a traveling companion.
"Good heavens!" I cried. "How you startled me!" "Every precaution is still necessary," he whispered.
Holmes had gone as quickly as he had come

nodes.description: Holmes's disguise as a decrepit aged Italian priest, adopted to travel safely with Watson to the Continent while evading Moriarty's agents in The Final Problem.

nodes.label: Straker's financial debt from secret mistress

citations.quote: "I have no doubt that this woman had plunged him over head and ears in debt, and so led him into this miserable plot."
"As a man of the world, Colonel, you know that men do not carry other people's bills about in their pockets."

nodes.description: Straker's motive in 'Silver Blaze': financial ruin incurred by a secret mistress who had plunged him into debt, leading him to plot maiming Silver Blaze to profit from a bet on the race.

nodes.label: Moriarty's revenge against Holmes for exposing his network

citations.quote: "If I read his character right he will devote his whole energies to revenging himself upon me."
"If you are clever enough to bring destruction upon me, rest assured that I shall do as much to you."

nodes.description: Professor Moriarty's personal motive in 'The Final Problem': to devote his entire energies to destroying Holmes in revenge for Holmes's exposure of his criminal network, having warned Holmes directly that he would do so.

nodes.label: Watson narrates and chronicles Holmes's cases in this collection

citations.quote: In publishing these short sketches based upon the numerous cases in which my companion's singular gifts have made us the listeners to, and eventually the actors in, some strange drama

nodes.description: Dr Watson narrates The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes, explaining in The Yellow Face that he publishes short sketches based on the cases in which Holmes's singular gifts have involved them both.

nodes.label: Mycroft audits government accounts and occasionally acts as a de facto government intelligence clearing-house

citations.quote: He has an extraordinary faculty for figures, and audits the books in some of the government departments.
"Some of my most interesting cases have come to me in this way through Mycroft."

nodes.description: In The Greek Interpreter, Mycroft Holmes is described as auditing government accounts and, when exceptional synthesis is needed, serving as a de facto clearing-house for all British government intelligence.

nodes.label: The Return of Sherlock Holmes

citations.quote: The Return of Sherlock Holmes
by Sir Arthur Conan Doyle

nodes.description: The Return of Sherlock Holmes, an Arthur Conan Doyle short-story collection in which Holmes reappears after the Reichenbach affair and resumes investigations including the Empty House murder.

nodes.label: Colonel Sebastian Moran
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Empty House

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: This, gentlemen, is Colonel Sebastian Moran, once of Her Majesty's Indian Army, and the best heavy-game shot that our Eastern Empire has ever produced

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: Moran, Sebastian, Colonel. Unemployed. Formerly 1st Bangalore Pioneers. Born London, 1840.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: The second most dangerous man in London.
His chief of staff is Colonel Sebastian Moran, as aloof and guarded and inaccessible to the law as himself.

nodes.description: Colonel Sebastian Moran, formerly of the 1st Bangalore Pioneers and described as the second most dangerous man in London, who serves Professor Moriarty and uses a Von Herder air-gun to commit murder in The Adventure of the Empty House.

nodes.label: Inspector Stanley Hopkins

citations.quote: Stanley Hopkins, a young police inspector, for whose future Holmes had high hopes
Hopkins has called me in seven times, and on each occasion his summons has been entirely justified

nodes.description: Inspector Stanley Hopkins, a young Scotland Yard detective in whom Holmes had high hopes, who called Holmes in seven times on fully justified cases, including The Adventure of Black Peter, The Adventure of the Abbey Grange, and The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez.

nodes.label: The Adventure of the Empty House
nodes.source_location: THE ADVENTURE OF THE EMPTY HOUSE

citations.quote: THE ADVENTURE OF THE EMPTY HOUSE

citations.section: THE ADVENTURE OF THE EMPTY HOUSE
citations.quote: It was in the spring of the year 1894 that all London was interested, and the fashionable world dismayed, by the murder of the Honourable Ronald Adair under most unusual and inexplicable circumstances.

nodes.description: The Adventure of the Empty House, the Return of Sherlock Holmes story set in 1894 in which Holmes reappears after three years, having survived the Reichenbach Falls, and captures the assassin Colonel Sebastian Moran.

nodes.label: The Adventure of the Norwood Builder
nodes.source_location: THE ADVENTURE OF THE NORWOOD BUILDER

citations.section: THE ADVENTURE OF THE NORWOOD BUILDER
citations.quote: London has become a singularly uninteresting city since the death of the late lamented Professor Moriarty

nodes.description: The Adventure of the Norwood Builder, a Sherlock Holmes story from The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates a case brought to him after the perceived death of Professor Moriarty, with Inspector Lestrade involved.

nodes.label: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
nodes.source_location: THE ADVENTURE OF THE DANCING MEN

citations.section: THE ADVENTURE OF THE DANCING MEN

nodes.description: The Adventure of the Dancing Men, a Return of Sherlock Holmes story in which Holmes deciphers a series of cryptic stick-figure messages and uncovers the American criminal Abe Slaney's pursuit of Elsie Cubitt.

nodes.label: The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist
nodes.source_location: THE ADVENTURE OF THE SOLITARY CYCLIST

citations.section: THE ADVENTURE OF THE SOLITARY CYCLIST
citations.quote: the facts connected with Miss Violet Smith, the solitary cyclist of Charlington

nodes.description: The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes concerning Miss Violet Smith, who is mysteriously followed on her weekly cycle rides near Charlington, leading Holmes to expose Jack Woodley's abduction scheme.

nodes.label: The Adventure of the Priory School
nodes.source_location: THE ADVENTURE OF THE PRIORY SCHOOL

nodes.description: The Adventure of the Priory School, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes investigates the abduction of the only son of the Duke of Holdernesse from his preparatory school, with James Wilder revealed as the instigator.

nodes.label: The Adventure of Black Peter

citations.quote: THE ADVENTURE OF BLACK PETER
the very obscure circumstances which surrounded the death of Captain Peter Carey

nodes.description: The Adventure of Black Peter, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes and Inspector Stanley Hopkins investigate the harpooning murder of retired sea captain Peter Carey, with Patrick Cairns revealed as the killer.

nodes.label: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton
nodes.source_location: THE ADVENTURE OF CHARLES AUGUSTUS MILVERTON

citations.quote: THE ADVENTURE OF CHARLES AUGUSTUS MILVERTON

nodes.description: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton, a story in The Return of Sherlock Holmes in which Holmes and Watson break into the blackmailer Milverton's home—described as the king of all blackmailers—and witness his murder by one of his victims.
