nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Empty House

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: Sherlock Holmes was standing smiling at me across my study table. I rose to my feet, stared at him for some seconds in utter amazement, and then it appears that I must have fainted for the first and the last time in my life.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: I had imagined that I had reached the end of my adventures

nodes.description: Holmes's dramatic reappearance to a stunned Watson across his study table in The Adventure of the Empty House, revealing he had survived the Reichenbach Falls by feigning death for three years.

nodes.label: Park Lane Mystery / Ronald Adair murder
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Empty House

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: the murder of the Honourable Ronald Adair under most unusual and inexplicable circumstances

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: Such were the circumstances of the Park Lane Mystery

nodes.description: The Park Lane Mystery, the investigation by Sherlock Holmes into the murder of the Honourable Ronald Adair under unusual and inexplicable circumstances, described in The Adventure of the Empty House.

nodes.label: Chain of Deductive Inferences
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Dancing Men

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: it is not really difficult to construct a series of inferences, each dependent upon its predecessor and each simple in itself

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: I have forged and tested every link of my chain, Professor Coram, and I am sure that it is sound.

nodes.description: Holmes's step-by-step chain of deductive inferences in The Adventure of the Dancing Men and others, each inference depending on its predecessor, a chain Holmes claims he has forged and tested at every link.

nodes.label: Dancing Men Cipher
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Dancing Men

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: I am fairly familiar with all forms of secret writings, and am myself the author of a trifling monograph upon the subject

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: The object of those who invented the system has apparently been to conceal that these characters convey a message, and to give the idea that they are the mere random sketches of children.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: AM HERE ABE SLANEY

nodes.description: A substitution cipher using stick-figure 'dancing men' to represent English letters, decoded by Holmes using frequency analysis in The Adventure of the Dancing Men.

nodes.label: Hilton Cubitt
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Dancing Men

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: Mr. Hilton Cubitt, of Riding Thorpe Manor, Norfolk

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: Mr. Hilton Cubitt had been shot through the heart. It was equally conceivable that he had shot her and then himself

nodes.description: Hilton Cubitt, the Norfolk squire of Riding Thorpe Manor who is found shot through the heart in The Adventure of the Dancing Men, the case involving the dancing men cipher sent by Abe Slaney.

nodes.label: Abe Slaney
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Dancing Men

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: The most dangerous crook in Chicago.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: The American, Abe Slaney, was condemned to death at the winter assizes at Norwich

nodes.description: Abe Slaney, described as the most dangerous crook in Chicago, who is condemned to death at the Norwich winter assizes for his crimes in The Adventure of the Dancing Men.

nodes.label: Elsie Cubitt (née Patrick)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Dancing Men

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: There was an American young lady there—Patrick was the name—Elsie Patrick.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: She broke away from your influence when she found the man that you are. She fled from America to avoid you

nodes.description: Elsie Cubitt, née Patrick, an American woman who had fled to England to escape Abe Slaney and married Hilton Cubitt in The Adventure of the Dancing Men.

nodes.label: Miss Violet Smith
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist

citations.section: The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist
citations.quote: the facts connected with Miss Violet Smith, the solitary cyclist of Charlington

citations.section: The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist
citations.quote: Miss Violet Smith did indeed inherit a large fortune, and she is now the wife of Cyril Morton

nodes.description: Miss Violet Smith, the solitary cyclist of Charlington in The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist, who eventually inherits a large fortune and becomes the wife of Cyril Morton after her ordeal.

nodes.label: Jack Woodley
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist

citations.section: The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist
citations.quote: a bully to everyone else, but to me something infinitely worse

citations.section: The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist
citations.quote: Williamson and Woodley were both tried for abduction and assault, the former getting seven years and the latter ten

nodes.description: Jack Woodley, a bully and villain in The Adventure of the Solitary Cyclist who abducts Violet Smith and is sentenced to ten years' imprisonment for abduction and assault.

nodes.label: Duke of Holdernesse
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Priory School

citations.section: The Adventure of the Priory School
citations.quote: Holdernesse, 6th Duke, K.G., P.C.—half the alphabet! Baron Beverley, Earl of Carston

citations.section: The Adventure of the Priory School
citations.quote: The greatest and perhaps the wealthiest. I am aware, Mr. Holmes, that you take a very high line in professional matters

nodes.description: The Duke of Holdernesse, described as 'the greatest and perhaps the wealthiest' peer in The Adventure of the Priory School, holding the titles of Baron Beverley and Earl of Carston among others.

nodes.label: James Wilder
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Priory School

citations.section: The Adventure of the Priory School
citations.quote: No, sir, my son. It was Holmes's turn to look astonished.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Priory School
citations.quote: James Wilder, his secretary

nodes.description: James Wilder, secretary to the Duke of Holdernesse in The Adventure of the Priory School, who is revealed to be the Duke's illegitimate son.

nodes.label: Captain Peter Carey

citations.quote: Captain Peter Carey. He was born in '45—fifty years of age. He was a most daring and successful seal and whale fisher.

He was known in the trade as Black Peter, and the name was given him, not only on account of his swarthy features and the colour of his huge beard, but for the humours which were the terror of all around him.

nodes.description: Captain Peter Carey, known as Black Peter — a swarthy, violent seal and whale fisher born in 1845 — whose murder by harpoon in his cabin is investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of Black Peter.

nodes.label: Patrick Cairns

citations.quote: Patrick Cairns. Harpooner? Yes, sir. Twenty-six voyages.
I knew Black Peter, and when he pulled out his knife I whipped a harpoon through him sharp, for I knew that it was him or me.

nodes.description: Patrick Cairns, a harpooner with twenty-six voyages who kills Captain Black Peter Carey with a harpoon when Carey drew a knife, as investigated in The Adventure of Black Peter.

nodes.label: Charles Augustus Milverton
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton

citations.section: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton
citations.quote: He is the king of all the blackmailers. Heaven help the man, and still more the woman, whose secret and reputation come into the power of Milverton!

citations.section: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton
citations.quote: The worst man in London

nodes.description: Charles Augustus Milverton, described as the worst man in London and the king of all blackmailers, whose operations are the subject of The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton.

nodes.label: Beppo
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Six Napoleons

citations.section: The Adventure of the Six Napoleons
citations.quote: Do I know that photograph? No, I don't. Yes, I do, though. Why, it's Beppo. He was a kind of Italian piece-work man, who made himself useful in the shop.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Six Napoleons
citations.quote: His name, it appeared, was Beppo, second name unknown. He was a well-known ne'er-do-well among the Italian colony.

nodes.description: Beppo, an Italian piece-work man with a criminal record who concealed the Borgia Pearl inside a plaster Napoleon bust and broke into houses to retrieve it in The Adventure of the Six Napoleons.

nodes.label: Black Pearl of the Borgias
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Six Napoleons

citations.section: The Adventure of the Six Napoleons
citations.quote: Gentlemen, let me introduce you to the famous black pearl of the Borgias.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Six Napoleons
citations.quote: it is the most famous pearl now existing in the world, and it has been my good fortune, by a connected chain of inductive reasoning, to trace it from the Prince of Colonna's bedroom at the Dacre Hotel, where it was lost, to the interior of this, the last of the six busts of Napoleon

nodes.description: The most famous pearl in existence in 'The Adventure of the Six Napoleons', recovered by Holmes from its hiding place inside one of the plaster busts of Napoleon, having been concealed there after its theft from the Princess of Colonna.

nodes.label: Professor Coram (Sergius)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez

citations.section: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez
citations.quote: Some years ago this country house, Yoxley Old Place, was taken by an elderly man, who gave the name of Professor Coram.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez
citations.quote: I am this man's wife. He is not an Englishman. He is a Russian. His name I will not tell.

nodes.description: Professor Coram (real name Sergius), a Russian revolutionary living under a false identity at Yoxley Old Place, whose hidden past is exposed when his long-concealed wife emerges in The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez.

nodes.label: Anna (Wife of Professor Coram)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez

citations.section: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez
citations.quote: You are right! I am here. She was brown with the dust and draped with the cobwebs which had come from the walls of her hiding-place.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez
citations.quote: I am this man's wife. He is not an Englishman. He is a Russian.

nodes.description: Anna, the Russian wife of the man calling himself Professor Coram, who had been hiding in the concealed room of Yoxley Old Place for years before emerging in The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez.

nodes.label: Golden Pince-Nez
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez

citations.section: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez
citations.quote: Stanley Hopkins drew a small paper packet. He unfolded it and disclosed a golden pince-nez, with two broken ends of black silk cord dangling from the end of it.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez
citations.quote: Wanted, a woman of good address, attired like a lady. She has a remarkably thick nose, with eyes which are set close upon either side of it.

nodes.description: A golden pince-nez with broken black silk ends, discovered at the murder scene in The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez and used by Holmes to reconstruct the killer's physical appearance.

nodes.label: Godfrey Staunton
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter

citations.section: The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter
citations.quote: Godfrey Staunton—you've heard of him, of course? He's simply the hinge that the whole team turns on.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter
citations.quote: Godfrey Staunton, the crack three-quarter, Cambridge, Blackheath, and five Internationals.

nodes.description: Godfrey Staunton, the Cambridge and England rugby three-quarter described as the hinge the whole team turns on, whose sudden disappearance triggers the investigation in The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter.

nodes.label: Dr. Leslie Armstrong
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter

citations.section: The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter
citations.quote: he is not only one of the heads of the medical school of the university, but a thinker of European reputation in more than one branch of science

citations.section: The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter
citations.quote: Dr. Leslie Armstrong is certainly a man of energy and character

nodes.description: Dr. Leslie Armstrong, one of the heads of the Cambridge medical school and a thinker of European reputation, who protects his patient's secret in The Adventure of the Missing Three-Quarter and is characterised by Holmes as a man of energy and character.

nodes.label: Lady Brackenstall (Mary Fraser)
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Abbey Grange

citations.section: The Adventure of the Abbey Grange
citations.quote: Seldom have I seen so graceful a figure, so womanly a presence, and so beautiful a face.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Abbey Grange
citations.quote: she had never been known as anything but Miss Mary Fraser of Adelaide and Lady Brackenstall of Abbey Grange

nodes.description: Lady Brackenstall, born Mary Fraser of Adelaide, the graceful and beautiful wife of the brutal Sir Eustace Brackenstall in The Adventure of the Abbey Grange.

nodes.label: Captain Jack Crocker
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Abbey Grange

citations.section: The Adventure of the Abbey Grange
citations.quote: The first officer, Mr. Jack Crocker, had been made a captain

citations.section: The Adventure of the Abbey Grange
citations.quote: His record was magnificent. There was not an officer in the fleet to touch him.

nodes.description: Captain Jack Crocker, a newly made captain and officer of magnificent record in the naval fleet, who appears in The Adventure of the Abbey Grange.

nodes.label: Lady Hilda Trelawney Hope
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Second Stain

citations.section: The Adventure of the Second Stain
citations.quote: our modest apartment, already so distinguished that morning, was further honoured by the entrance of the most lovely woman in London. I had often heard of the beauty of the youngest daughter of the Duke of Belminster

citations.section: The Adventure of the Second Stain
citations.quote: Has my husband been here, Mr. Holmes?

nodes.description: Lady Hilda Trelawney Hope, described as the loveliest woman in London, who visits Holmes in The Adventure of the Second Stain to conceal her role in the disappearance of her husband's secret state document.

nodes.label: Trelawney Hope
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Second Stain

citations.section: The Adventure of the Second Stain
citations.quote: the Right Honourable Trelawney Hope, Secretary for European Affairs, and the most rising statesman in the country

nodes.description: Trelawney Hope, the Right Honourable Secretary for European Affairs and the most rising statesman in the country, whose missing state document is the subject of The Adventure of the Second Stain.

nodes.label: Moran's murder of Ronald Adair
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Empty House

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: Colonel Sebastian Moran, who shot the Honourable Ronald Adair with an expanding bullet from an air-gun through the open window of the second-floor front of No. 427, Park Lane, upon the thirtieth of last month.

nodes.description: Colonel Sebastian Moran's murder of Ronald Adair, shot with an expanding bullet from an air-gun through an open window to prevent Adair from exposing his card-cheating, as investigated by Holmes in The Adventure of the Empty House.

nodes.label: Milverton's blackmail operation
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton

citations.section: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton
citations.quote: He allows it to be known that he is prepared to pay very high sums for letters which compromise people of wealth and position.

citations.section: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton
citations.quote: with a smiling face and a heart of marble, he will squeeze and squeeze until he has drained them dry

nodes.description: Charles Augustus Milverton's blackmail operation in The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton, in which he pays high sums for compromising letters from wealthy and powerful people and then squeezes them dry with smiling ruthlessness.

nodes.label: Beppo's theft and concealment of the Borgia Pearl
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Six Napoleons

citations.section: The Adventure of the Six Napoleons
citations.quote: Beppo had the pearl in his possession. He may have stolen it from Pietro, he may have been Pietro's confederate

citations.section: The Adventure of the Six Napoleons
citations.quote: In an instant Beppo, a skilful workman, made a small hole in the wet plaster, dropped in the pearl, and with a few touches covered over the aperture once more.

nodes.description: Beppo's theft and concealment of the Borgia Pearl in The Adventure of the Six Napoleons, in which Beppo hid the pearl by pressing it into wet plaster inside a Napoleon bust in the workshop where he was employed.

nodes.label: Theft of the international letter from the despatch-box
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Second Stain

citations.section: The Adventure of the Second Stain
citations.quote: The letter, then, is from a certain foreign potentate who has been ruffled by some recent Colonial developments of this country.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Second Stain
citations.quote: I took an impression of his key. This man, Lucas, furnished a duplicate. I opened his despatch-box, took the paper, and conveyed it to Godolphin Street.

nodes.description: The theft of a secret letter from a foreign potentate out of Secretary Trelawney Hope's dispatch-box, carried out by Lady Hilda using a duplicate key obtained from the spy Eduardo Lucas, in The Adventure of the Second Stain.

nodes.label: Moran's motive: silence Adair who discovered card cheating
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Empty House

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: I believe that on the day of the murder Adair had discovered that Moran was cheating.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: The exclusion from his clubs would mean ruin to Moran, who lived by his ill-gotten card-gains.

nodes.description: Colonel Moran's motive for murdering Ronald Adair in 'The Adventure of the Empty House': silencing the only person who had discovered Moran's card cheating, since exposure would have meant expulsion from his clubs and financial ruin.

nodes.label: Lucas's leverage over Lady Hilda using indiscreet letter
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Second Stain

citations.section: The Adventure of the Second Stain
citations.quote: It was a letter of mine, Mr. Holmes, an indiscreet letter written before my marriage—a foolish letter, a letter of an impulsive, loving girl.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Second Stain
citations.quote: He said that he would return my letter if I would bring him a certain document which he described in my husband's despatch-box.

nodes.description: Eduardo Lucas's hold over Lady Hilda Trelawney Hope in 'The Adventure of the Second Stain', consisting of an indiscreet pre-marital letter which he used to coerce her into stealing a confidential document from her husband's despatch-box.

nodes.label: Holmes as elderly book collector
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Empty House

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: an elderly, deformed man, who had been behind me

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: He had thrown off the seedy frockcoat, and now he was the Holmes of old

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: the rest of that individual lay in a pile of white hair and old books upon the table

nodes.description: Holmes's disguise as an elderly deformed book collector in The Adventure of the Empty House, used to approach Watson unrecognised before casting off the frockcoat and old books to reveal himself.

nodes.label: Holmes as Escott the plumber
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton

citations.section: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton
citations.quote: I am a plumber with a rising business, Escott, by name. I have walked out with her each evening

nodes.description: Holmes's disguise as Escott the plumber in The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton, under which he courts Milverton's housemaid by walking out with her each evening to gain access to the house.

nodes.label: Frequency analysis of dancing-men cipher
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Dancing Men

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: Having once recognized, however, that the symbols stood for letters, and having applied the rules which guide us in all forms of secret writings, the solution was easy enough.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dancing Men
citations.quote: E is the most common letter in the English alphabet, and it predominates to so marked an extent that even in a short sentence one would expect to find it most often.

nodes.description: Holmes's application of English letter-frequency analysis to the dancing-men cipher in The Adventure of the Dancing Men, using the preponderance of E as the key to bootstrap identification of the full alphabet.

nodes.label: Bicycle tire track analysis
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Priory School

citations.section: The Adventure of the Priory School
citations.quote: I am familiar with forty-two different impressions left by tires. This, as you perceive, is a Dunlop, with a patch upon the outer cover.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Priory School
citations.quote: Here is Herr Heidegger, sure enough! The Palmer tires.

nodes.description: Sherlock Holmes's identification technique in 'The Adventure of the Priory School' of distinguishing bicycle tire impressions—identifying Dunlop, Palmer, and other makes—to trace a route and identify a victim.

nodes.label: Tobacco ash as detection trap
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez

citations.section: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez
citations.quote: I smoked a great number of those excellent cigarettes, and I dropped the ash all over the space in front of the suspected bookcase.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez
citations.quote: from the traces upon the cigarette ash, that the prisoner had in our absence come out from her retreat

nodes.description: Holmes's detection technique in 'The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez' of scattering cigarette ash across a floor space in front of a suspect's hiding place to record whether she emerged in his absence.

nodes.label: Holmes's safe-cracking technique
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton

citations.section: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton
citations.quote: the opening of safes was a particular hobby with him

citations.section: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton
citations.quote: Holmes laid out two drills, a jemmy, and several skeleton keys. For half an hour, Holmes worked with concentrated energy

nodes.description: Sherlock Holmes's safe-cracking technique, described in 'The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton', using drills, a jemmy, and skeleton keys, with safe-opening being a particular hobby of Holmes's.

nodes.label: Holmes's three-year absence after Reichenbach
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Empty House

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: I travelled for two years in Tibet, therefore, and amused myself by visiting Lhassa, and spending some days with the head lama.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: For three years I have permitted my name to be no more than a memory

nodes.description: After the Reichenbach confrontation, Holmes spent three years in disguise — including two years in Tibet visiting Lhassa and a period in Persia and France — before returning in The Adventure of the Empty House.

nodes.label: Moran served as Moriarty's chief of staff
nodes.source_location: The Adventure of the Empty House

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: It was at this time that he was sought out by Professor Moriarty, to whom for a time he was chief of the staff.

nodes.description: Colonel Sebastian Moran served as Professor Moriarty's chief of staff before being absorbed into the criminal organisation, as Holmes reveals in The Adventure of the Empty House.

nodes.label: The Sign of the Four

citations.quote: The Sign of the Four / by Arthur Conan Doyle

nodes.description: The Sign of the Four, the 1890 Sherlock Holmes novel by Arthur Conan Doyle in which Holmes and Watson investigate the Agra treasure mystery involving Jonathan Small and the criminal pact of four.

nodes.label: Mary Morstan

citations.quote: Miss Morstan entered the room with a firm step and an outward composure of manner. She was a blonde young lady, small, dainty, well gloved, and dressed in the most perfect taste.
My father was an officer in an Indian regiment who sent me home when I was quite a child.

nodes.description: Mary Morstan, the blonde young woman in The Sign of the Four who engages Holmes to investigate the annual pearls sent to her and the disappearance of her father, and who subsequently becomes Dr. Watson's wife.

nodes.label: Thaddeus Sholto

citations.quote: Mr. Thaddeus Sholto, said the little man, still jerking and smiling. That is my name.
He writhed his hands together as he stood, and his features were in a perpetual jerk, now smiling, now scowling, but never for an instant in repose.

nodes.description: Thaddeus Sholto, the nervous, perpetually jerking and smiling son of Major Sholto who contacts Mary Morstan to reveal his father's secret and guide Holmes and Watson to the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Bartholomew Sholto

citations.quote: Bartholomew is a clever fellow. How do you think he found out where the treasure was? He had come to the conclusion that it was somewhere indoors.
By the table, in a wooden arm-chair, the master of the house was seated all in a heap, with his head sunk upon his left shoulder, and that ghastly, inscrutable smile upon his face.

nodes.description: Bartholomew Sholto, a clever man who discovers the location of the Agra treasure hidden in his house at Pondicherry Lodge, and is found dead in a wooden armchair — murdered in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Major John Sholto

citations.quote: My father was, as you may have guessed, Major John Sholto, once of the Indian army. He retired some eleven years ago, and came to live at Pondicherry Lodge in Upper Norwood.
The cursed greed which has been my besetting sin through life has withheld from her the treasure, half at least of which should have been hers.

nodes.description: Major John Sholto, a former Indian army officer stationed at the Andaman Islands who betrayed his partner Captain Morstan and kept the entire Agra treasure, his deathbed confession setting the plot of The Sign of the Four in motion.

nodes.label: Jonathan Small

citations.quote: His name, I have every reason to believe, is Jonathan Small. He is a poorly-educated man, small, active, with his right leg off, and wearing a wooden stump which is worn away upon the inner side.
I am a Worcestershire man myself,—born near Pershore.

nodes.description: Jonathan Small, a one-legged ex-soldier from Worcestershire and one of the four conspirators in the Agra treasure pact who pursues revenge against those who betrayed him in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Tonga

citations.quote: It straightened itself into a little black man—the smallest I have ever seen—with a great, misshapen head and a shock of tangled, dishevelled hair.
Tonga—for that was his name—was a fine boatman, and owned a big, roomy canoe of his own.

nodes.description: Tonga, a small Andaman Islander who accompanies Jonathan Small in The Sign of the Four and kills Bartholomew Sholto with a poisoned blowpipe dart.

nodes.label: Mahomet Singh

citations.quote: The sign of the four,—Jonathan Small, Mahomet Singh, Abdullah Khan, Dost Akbar.
They were tall, fierce-looking chaps, Mahomet Singh and Abdullah Khan by name, both old fighting-men who had borne arms against us at Chilian-wallah.

nodes.description: Mahomet Singh, one of the four signatories of the Sign of Four criminal pact, a tall, fierce old fighting-man who conspires with Jonathan Small to seize the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Abdullah Khan

citations.quote: The sign of the four,—Jonathan Small, Mahomet Singh, Abdullah Khan, Dost Akbar.
Listen to me, Sahib, said the taller and fiercer of the pair, the one whom they called Abdullah Khan.

nodes.description: Abdullah Khan, one of the four signatories of the Sign of Four criminal pact, a tall and fierce fighting-man who conspires with Jonathan Small to seize the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Dost Akbar

citations.quote: The sign of the four,—Jonathan Small, Mahomet Singh, Abdullah Khan, Dost Akbar.
No; Dost Akbar must have his share. We can tell the tale to you while we await them.

nodes.description: Dost Akbar, one of the four signatories of the Sign of Four criminal pact who conspires with Jonathan Small, Abdullah Khan, and Mahomet Singh to seize the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Athelney Jones

citations.quote: I think you must recollect me, Mr. Athelney Jones, said Holmes, quietly.
A very stout, portly man in a grey suit strode heavily into the room. He was red-faced, burly and plethoric, with a pair of very small twinkling eyes.

nodes.description: Athelney Jones, a stout, red-faced, burly Scotland Yard inspector who is called to the Sholto case and initially arrests Thaddeus Sholto in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Mordecai Smith

citations.quote: Mordecai Smith was printed across it in large letters, and, underneath, Boats to hire by the hour or day.
I want her to-night at eight o'clock, said he,—eight o'clock sharp, mind, for I have two gentlemen who won't be kept waiting.

nodes.description: Mordecai Smith, the owner of the steam launch Aurora whose boat is hired by Jonathan Small and Tonga for their escape down the Thames in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Captain Arthur Morstan

citations.quote: My father was an officer in an Indian regiment who sent me home when I was quite a child.
There was Major Sholto, Captain Morstan, and Lieutenant Bromley Brown, who were in command of the native troops.

nodes.description: Captain Arthur Morstan, a British army officer stationed at the Andaman Islands with Major Sholto who disappears after arriving in London, his fate and his share of the Agra treasure forming the mystery at the heart of The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Wiggins

citations.quote: That wire was to my dirty little lieutenant, Wiggins, and I expect that he and his gang will be with us before we have finished our breakfast.
In future they can report to you, Wiggins, and you to me.

nodes.description: Wiggins, the leader of the Baker Street Irregulars street-urchin network who reports directly to Sherlock Holmes and coordinates the search for the steam launch Aurora in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Mrs. Cecil Forrester

citations.quote: I have come to you, Mr. Holmes, because you once enabled my employer, Mrs. Cecil Forrester, to unravel a little domestic complication.
She lives with Mrs. Cecil Forrester, in Lower Camberwell.

nodes.description: Mrs. Cecil Forrester, Mary Morstan's employer in Lower Camberwell in The Sign of the Four, on whose behalf Holmes had previously unravelled a domestic complication.

nodes.label: Achmet the Merchant

citations.quote: This pretended merchant, who travels under the name of Achmet, is now in the city of Agra, and desires to gain his way into the fort.
Your protection, Sahib, he panted,—your protection for the unhappy merchant Achmet.

nodes.description: Achmet, a merchant travelling under a false name who carries the secret of the Agra treasure and is murdered by the Four conspirators inside the Agra fortress in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Sholto treasure case

citations.quote: I can hardly imagine anything more strange, more utterly inexplicable, than the situation in which I find myself.
The rediscovery of the treasure immediately followed by the murder of the discoverer, the very singular accompaniments to the crime.

nodes.description: The Sholto treasure case, the central investigation of The Sign of the Four in which Mary Morstan engages Holmes to explain the origin of the pearls she has been sent annually and the murder of Bartholomew Sholto.

nodes.label: Murder of Bartholomew Sholto

citations.quote: It means murder, said he, stooping over the dead man. Ah, I expected it. Look here! He pointed to what looked like a long, dark thorn stuck in the skin just above the ear.
It was that little hell-hound Tonga who shot one of his cursed darts into him. I had no part in it, sir.

nodes.description: The murder of Bartholomew Sholto, a crime in The Sign of the Four in which Tonga shoots the discoverer of the Agra treasure with a poisoned blowpipe dart, leaving the card 'The sign of the four' at the scene.

nodes.label: Agra treasure theft and murder of Achmet

citations.quote: The Sikh was upon him, and buried his knife twice in his side. The man never uttered moan nor moved muscle, but lay were he had fallen.
It was an evil day for me when first I clapped eyes upon the merchant Achmet and had to do with the Agra treasure, which never brought anything but a curse yet upon the man who owned it.

nodes.description: The theft of the Agra treasure by Jonathan Small and his three Sikh confederates, who murdered the merchant Achmet at the Agra fortress and buried his body, the crime recounted in Small's confession in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Major Sholto's betrayal of the Four

citations.quote: The villain Sholto went off to India, but he never came back again.
The scoundrel had stolen it all, without carrying out one of the conditions on which we had sold him the secret.

nodes.description: Major John Sholto's betrayal of the Four pact, keeping the Agra treasure for himself without fulfilling his agreement with Jonathan Small, which drives Small's decades-long pursuit of revenge in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Small's vengeance against the Sholtos

citations.quote: From that day I lived only for vengeance. I thought of it by day and I nursed it by night. It became an overpowering, absorbing passion with me.
To escape, to track down Sholto, to have my hand upon his throat,—that was my one thought.

nodes.description: Jonathan Small's consuming motive in 'The Sign of the Four': vengeance against Major Sholto who stole the Agra treasure from the Four, an obsession he nursed by day and night that became his sole purpose.

nodes.label: Recovery of the Agra treasure

citations.quote: I tell you that no living man has any right to it, unless it is three men who are in the Andaman convict-barracks and myself.
It's been the sign of four with us always. Well I know that they would have had me do just what I have done.

nodes.description: The driving motive behind the Sign of the Four pact: Jonathan Small's insistence that the Agra treasure belongs only to the three surviving conspirators who shared in its acquisition, making its recovery a matter of honour and right.

nodes.label: Watson's love for Mary Morstan

citations.quote: My sympathies and my love went out to her, even as my hand had in the garden. I felt that years of the conventionalities of life could not teach me to know her sweet, brave nature as had this one day of strange experiences.
Because I love you, Mary, as truly as ever a man loved a woman. Because this treasure, these riches, sealed my lips.

nodes.description: Dr. Watson's love for Mary Morstan in 'The Sign of the Four', which prevents him speaking while the treasure exists and which he declares openly to her once the treasure is lost to the Thames.

nodes.label: Agra treasure

citations.quote: He computes the value of the jewels at not less than half a million sterling.

There were one hundred and forty-three diamonds of the first water, including one which has been called, I believe, the Great Mogul...ninety-seven very fine emeralds, and one hundred and seventy rubies.

nodes.description: The Agra treasure, the vast collection of jewels valued at not less than half a million sterling in The Sign of the Four, including one hundred and forty-three first-water diamonds, which Jonathan Small and his three co-conspirators seized from an Indian merchant.

nodes.label: Agra iron treasure chest

citations.quote: A solid iron chest of Indian workmanship stood upon the deck. This, there could be no question, was the same that had contained the ill-omened treasure of the Sholtos.
What a pretty box! she said, stooping over it. This is Indian work, I suppose? Yes; it is Benares metal-work.

nodes.description: A solid iron chest of Indian workmanship, central to The Sign of the Four, which had contained the Agra treasure but was found empty when Holmes and Watson recovered it on deck.

nodes.label: Agra fortress plan (chart of the Four)

citations.quote: The diagram upon it appears to be a plan of part of a large building with numerous halls, corridors, and passages. At one point is a small cross done in red ink, and above it is '3.37 from left.'

In the left-hand corner is a curious hieroglyphic like four crosses in a line with their arms touching. Beside it is written, in very rough and coarse characters, 'The sign of the four,—Jonathan Small, Mahomet Singh, Abdullah Khan, Dost Akbar.'

nodes.description: A diagram found in 'The Sign of the Four' showing the plan of a large building with numerous halls, corridors, and passages, marked in the corner with a hieroglyphic of four crosses whose arms touch, and the words 'The Sign of the Four.'

nodes.label: Pearls sent annually to Mary Morstan

citations.quote: Since then every year upon the same date there has always appeared a similar box, containing a similar pearl, without any clue as to the sender.
She opened a flat box as she spoke, and showed me six of the finest pearls that I had ever seen.

nodes.description: The six fine pearls sent annually in unmarked boxes to Mary Morstan since her father's disappearance, ultimately traced by Holmes to the Sholto family's guilt over the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Poisoned blowpipe thorn

citations.quote: He pointed to what looked like a long, dark thorn stuck in the skin just above the ear.
It was long, sharp, and black, with a glazed look near the point as though some gummy substance had dried upon it.

nodes.description: A long dark blowpipe thorn with a gummy poison on its tip, found above Bartholomew Sholto's ear in The Sign of the Four, identified as Tonga's murder weapon.

nodes.label: Wooden-leg stump prints at Pondicherry Lodge

citations.quote: Here on the sill is the boot-mark, a heavy boot with the broad metal heel, and beside it is the mark of the timber-toe.
The impression of a wooden stump. You see here on the sill is the boot-mark.

nodes.description: Wooden-leg stump prints alongside a heavy boot-mark on the window sill at Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four, used by Holmes to identify Jonathan Small as one of the two intruders.

nodes.label: Small naked footprints in roof garret

citations.quote: The floor was covered thickly with the prints of a naked foot,—clear, well defined, perfectly formed, but scarce half the size of those of an ordinary man.
Holmes, I said, in a whisper, a child has done the horrid thing.

nodes.description: Tiny bare footprints covering the roof garret floor at Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four, at first mistaken for a child's prints but identified by Holmes as belonging to Tonga.

nodes.label: Creosote trail from Tonga's foot

citations.quote: Number One has had the misfortune to tread in the creosote. You can see the outline of the edge of his small foot here at the side of this evil-smelling mess.
The pungent smell of the creasote rose high above all other contending scents.

nodes.description: A trail of creosote left by Tonga's foot across the ground at Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four, detected by Holmes's nose and used to track the small-footed killer.

nodes.label: 'The sign of the four' card left at Bartholomew's body

citations.quote: In the light of the lantern I read, with a thrill of horror, 'The sign of the four.'
Mad with hate, however, against the dead man, he enters the room that night, searches his private papers...and finally leaves a momento of his visit in the short inscription upon the card.

nodes.description: The card reading 'The sign of the four' left beside Bartholomew Sholto's body in The Sign of the Four, placed by Jonathan Small as a mark of revenge for the broken Agra treasure pact.

nodes.label: Toby dog creosote scent tracking

citations.quote: I know a dog that would follow that scent to the world's end. If a pack can track a trailed herring across a shire, how far can a specially-trained hound follow so pungent a smell as this?
I would rather have Toby's help than that of the whole detective force of London.

nodes.description: Toby dog creosote scent tracking used by Sherlock Holmes in The Sign of the Four, following a trail across London in the Sholto treasure case, with Holmes valuing Toby's help above the entire detective force.

nodes.label: Baker Street Irregulars network

citations.quote: No, it's not quite so bad as that. It is the unofficial force,—the Baker Street irregulars.
They can go everywhere, see everything, overhear every one. I expect to hear before evening that they have spotted her.

nodes.description: Holmes's unofficial network of Baker Street street-urchin informants who can go everywhere and overhear everyone, deployed by Wiggins to locate the steam launch Aurora during the Sholto treasure case in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Holmes's old sailor disguise

citations.quote: Here is the old man, said he, holding out a heap of white hair. Here he is,—wig, whiskers, eyebrows, and all. I thought my disguise was pretty good.
I have been working in that get-up all day. You see, a good many of the criminal classes begin to know me.

nodes.description: Holmes's old-sailor disguise in The Sign of the Four — comprising wig, whiskers, and eyebrows — used to gather intelligence along the Thames waterfront without being recognised by the criminal classes who know his face.

nodes.label: Holmes's forensic monographs (tobacco ash, footsteps, hands)

citations.quote: Here, for example, is one 'Upon the Distinction between the Ashes of the Various Tobaccoes.' In it I enumerate a hundred and forty forms of cigar-, cigarette-, and pipe-tobacco.

Here, too, is a curious little work upon the influence of a trade upon the form of the hand, with lithotypes of the hands of slaters, sailors, corkcutters, compositors, weavers, and diamond-polishers.

nodes.description: A body of specialist detective literature written by Sherlock Holmes, described in 'The Sign of the Four', including a monograph distinguishing the ashes of various tobaccos and another on the influence of trade upon the form of the hand.

nodes.label: The Sign of Four (criminal pact)

citations.quote: The sign of the four,—Jonathan Small, Mahomet Singh, Abdullah Khan, Dost Akbar.
We had sworn that we should each always act for all, so that none might take advantage. That is an oath that I can put my hand to my heart and swear that I have never broken.

nodes.description: The Sign of Four, the criminal pact sworn by Jonathan Small, Mahomet Singh, Abdullah Khan, and Dost Akbar to share the Agra treasure equally, a compact later betrayed by Major Sholto.

nodes.label: Baker Street Irregulars

citations.quote: As he spoke, there came a swift pattering of naked feet upon the stairs, a clatter of high voices, and in rushed a dozen dirty and ragged little street-Arabs.
You remember the Baker Street division of the detective police force whom I employed in the Jefferson Hope case?

nodes.description: The Baker Street Irregulars, a gang of street boys led by Wiggins and employed by Sherlock Holmes as an informal detective division, used in The Sign of the Four to search for the steam launch Aurora.

nodes.label: Agra fortress

citations.quote: I don't know if any of you gentlemen have ever read or heard anything of that old fort. It is a very queer place,—the queerest that ever I was in.
It is all full of great deserted halls, and winding passages, and long corridors twisting in and out, so that it is easy enough for folk to get lost in it.

nodes.description: Agra fortress, an immense, labyrinthine old fort full of great deserted halls, winding passages, and long corridors where the Agra treasure is hidden and Achmet is murdered by the Four conspirators in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Andaman Islands

citations.quote: Andaman Islands, situated 340 miles to the north of Sumatra, in the Bay of Bengal.
He had been one of the officers in charge of the convict-guard there.

nodes.description: A British penal colony in the Bay of Bengal, 340 miles north of Sumatra, where Captain Morstan, Major Sholto, and Jonathan Small all served, and where Tonga the Andaman Islander was found, as described in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: The Thames (river chase)

citations.quote: We had shot through the Pool, past the West India Docks, down the long Deptford Reach, and up again after rounding the Isle of Dogs.
Somewhere in the dark ooze at the bottom of the Thames lie the bones of that strange visitor to our shores.

nodes.description: The River Thames in The Sign of the Four, the setting of the steam-launch Aurora river chase ending in Tonga's death and Jonathan Small's capture, and the permanent watery grave of the scattered Agra treasure.

nodes.label: Lyceum Theatre (rendezvous)

citations.quote: Be at the third pillar from the left outside the Lyceum Theatre to-night at seven o'clock.
At the Lyceum Theatre the crowds were already thick at the side-entrances.

nodes.description: The Lyceum Theatre in the Strand in 'The Sign of the Four', the seven-o'clock rendezvous point at the third pillar from the left of the side entrance where Mary Morstan meets Holmes and Watson.

nodes.label: Steam launch Aurora

citations.quote: The Aurora, sir. Ah! She's not that old green launch with a yellow line, very broad in the beam? No, indeed. She's as trim a little thing as any on the river. She's been fresh painted, black with two red streaks.
I want to find the whereabouts of a steam launch called the Aurora, owner Mordecai Smith, black with two red streaks, funnel black with a white band.

nodes.description: The steam launch Aurora, a fast black boat with two red streaks owned by Mordecai Smith, used to flee down the Thames in The Sign of the Four and pursued by Holmes and Watson in the river chase.

nodes.label: Toby the tracking dog

citations.quote: Toby proved to be an ugly, long-haired, lop-eared creature, half spaniel and half lurcher, brown-and-white in colour, with a very clumsy waddling gait.
Yes,—a queer mongrel, with a most amazing power of scent. I would rather have Toby's help than that of the whole detective force of London.

nodes.description: Toby, an ugly long-haired lop-eared tracking dog of mixed spaniel and lurcher breeding in 'The Sign of the Four', borrowed from Sherman in Pinchin Lane and praised by Holmes as more useful than the entire detective force for his extraordinary scent.

nodes.label: Tonga's blowpipe and poisoned darts

citations.quote: Inside were half a dozen spines of dark wood, sharp at one end and rounded at the other, like that which had struck Bartholomew Sholto.
He plucked out from under his covering a short, round piece of wood, like a school-ruler, and clapped it to his lips.

nodes.description: Tonga's blowpipe and short, dark-wood poisoned spines used to murder Bartholomew Sholto in 'The Sign of the Four', clapped to his lips and discharged while concealed aboard the Aurora.

nodes.label: Watson proposes to Mary Morstan

citations.quote: Because I love you, Mary, as truly as ever a man loved a woman. Because this treasure, these riches, sealed my lips. Now that they are gone I can tell you how I love you.
Then I say, 'Thank God,' too, she whispered, as I drew her to my side. Whoever had lost a treasure, I knew that night that I had gained one.

nodes.description: Watson's declaration of love to Mary Morstan on the night the Agra treasure is lost to the Thames, with Mary accepting him, marking the romantic resolution of The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Murder of Achmet at Agra fort

citations.quote: The Sikh was upon him, and buried his knife twice in his side. The man never uttered moan nor moved muscle, but lay were he had fallen.
We carried him in, Abdullah, Akbar, and I...We took him to a place which the Sikhs had already prepared.

nodes.description: The killing of the treasure-carrier Achmet inside Agra fort by the four conspirators — a Sikh stabbing him twice — as recounted by Jonathan Small in Chapter XII of The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Thames river chase of the Aurora

citations.quote: I have never coursed many creatures in many countries during my checkered career, but never did sport give me such a wild thrill as this mad, flying man-hunt down the Thames.
We must catch her! cried Holmes, between his teeth. Heap it on, stokers! Make her do all she can! If we burn the boat we must have them!

nodes.description: The police steam launch's pursuit of the steam launch Aurora down the Thames, in which Holmes urges the stokers to heap it on and burn the boat if necessary, to recapture Jonathan Small in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Jonathan Small and Tonga escape the Andamans

citations.quote: He was stanch and true, was little Tonga. No man ever had a more faithful mate. At the night named he had his boat at the wharf.
I sat down in the darkness and unstrapped my wooden leg. With three long hops I was on him...I made for the boat, and in an hour we were well out at sea.

nodes.description: Jonathan Small and his faithful companion Tonga's nighttime escape by boat from the Andaman Islands penal settlement, as Small hopped on his wooden leg to reach the waiting vessel, in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Small throws Agra treasure into the Thames

citations.quote: Yes, I have put it away where you shall never lay hand upon it. It is my treasure; and if I can't have the loot I'll take darned good care that no one else does.
Now that they are scattered over five miles or so, it may be a harder job.

nodes.description: Jonathan Small throws the entire Agra treasure overboard into the Thames when cornered in The Sign of the Four, declaring that nobody else shall have it, leaving it scattered over five miles of riverbed.

nodes.label: Iron treasure box found empty

citations.quote: With trembling fingers I flung back the lid. We both stood gazing in astonishment. The box was empty!
The treasure is lost, said Miss Morstan, calmly.

nodes.description: The iron Agra treasure chest, when finally opened by Holmes and Watson in The Sign of the Four, proves to be completely empty — Jonathan Small having already thrown the jewels into the Thames.

nodes.label: The Four convicted and transported to Andamans

citations.quote: The three Sikhs got penal servitude for life, and I was condemned to death, though my sentence was afterwards commuted into the same as the others.
I who have a fair claim to nigh upon half a million of money should spend the first half of my life building a breakwater in the Andamans.

nodes.description: The Sign of the Four recounts that the three Sikh conspirators received life sentences and Jonathan Small was condemned to death (commuted to transportation) for the Agra murder of Achmet.

nodes.label: The Valley of Fear

citations.quote: THE VALLEY OF FEAR / By Sir Arthur Conan Doyle

nodes.description: The Valley of Fear, a Sherlock Holmes novel by Arthur Conan Doyle in which the murder at Birlstone Manor is linked to the criminal organisation the Scowrers in the Pennsylvania coalfields.

nodes.label: Dr. John H. Watson

citations.quote: Porlock, Watson, is a nom-de-plume, a mere identification mark
White Mason is a very live man, if I am any judge.

nodes.description: Dr. John H. Watson, Sherlock Holmes's trusted companion and chronicler, present with Holmes at the investigation of the Birlstone Manor murder in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: John Douglas

citations.quote: John Douglas of the Manor House had within five years won himself quite a reputation in Birlstone.
the man whom we had known as Douglas turned up his own coat and cuff to show a brown triangle within a circle

nodes.description: John Douglas, the popular American-born resident of Birlstone Manor in The Valley of Fear, whose apparent murder is later revealed to be a staged killing and who is identified as the former Pinkerton agent Birdy Edwards.

nodes.label: Birdy Edwards
nodes.source_location: Part II, Chapter VI; Part II, Chapter VII

citations.section: Part II, Chapter VI; Part II, Chapter VII
citations.quote: Pinkerton has taken hold under their orders, and his best man, Birdy Edwards, is operating.

citations.section: Part II, Chapter VI; Part II, Chapter VII
citations.quote: I am Birdy Edwards of Pinkerton's. I was chosen to break up your gang.

evidence_refs: corpus/sherlock-holmes/the-valley-of-fear/text.txt#Part II, Chapter VI; Part II, Chapter VII
nodes.description: Birdy Edwards, a Pinkerton detective who infiltrates the Scowrers under the aliases John McMurdo and Steve Wilson and gathers evidence that breaks up their criminal organisation in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Boss McGinty

citations.quote: Boss McGinty. He is the Bodymaster of Vermissa Lodge, and nothing can happen in these parts unless Black Jack McGinty wants it.
McGinty met his fate upon the scaffold, cringing and whining when the last hour came.

nodes.description: Boss McGinty (Black Jack McGinty), the Bodymaster of Vermissa Lodge and leader of the Scowrers in The Valley of Fear, a ruthless criminal who controls all violence in the Pennsylvania coalfields and eventually meets his fate on the scaffold.

nodes.label: Ted Baldwin
nodes.source_location: Part II, Chapter II; Part I, Chapter VII

citations.section: Part II, Chapter II; Part I, Chapter VII
citations.quote: he got both barrels in the face, and there I was, staring down at all that was left of Ted Baldwin.

citations.section: Part II, Chapter II; Part I, Chapter VII
citations.quote: Baldwin, if you don't take your hand off your pistol, you'll cheat the hangman yet.

evidence_refs: corpus/sherlock-holmes/the-valley-of-fear/text.txt#Part II, Chapter II; Part I, Chapter VII
nodes.description: Ted Baldwin, a violent Scowrer in The Valley of Fear whose attempt to murder John Douglas at Birlstone results in his own death by a double-barrelled shotgun, his body being used to stage Douglas's apparent death.

nodes.label: Cecil Barker

citations.quote: Cecil James Barker, of Hales Lodge, Hampstead.
Cecil Barker's tall, loose-jointed figure was a familiar one in the main street of Birlstone village

nodes.description: Cecil Barker, a tall, loose-jointed acquaintance of John Douglas, resident at Hales Lodge, Hampstead, who is a familiar figure in Birlstone village and is present at the manor on the night of the apparent murder in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Ivy Douglas

citations.quote: she was a beautiful woman, tall, dark, and slender, some twenty years younger than her husband
Jack has been lost overboard in gale off St. Helena. No one knows how accident occurred. —IVY DOUGLAS.

nodes.description: Ivy Douglas, the beautiful and much younger wife of John Douglas at Birlstone Manor, who later sends a telegram reporting his death overboard off St. Helena, in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Ettie Shafter

citations.quote: I'm Miss Ettie Shafter, Mr. Shafter's daughter.
It was the last time that ever either Ettie or her lover set foot in the Valley of Fear. Ten days later they were married in Chicago

nodes.description: Ettie Shafter, daughter of the landlord Shafter in Vermissa Valley, who falls in love with the undercover agent Birdy Edwards and eventually marries and escapes with him in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Inspector Alec MacDonald

citations.quote: Inspector MacDonald of Scotland Yard was ushered into the room.
He was a young but trusted member of the detective force, who had distinguished himself in several cases

nodes.description: Inspector Alec MacDonald, a young but trusted Scotland Yard detective who has distinguished himself in several cases and leads the Birlstone Manor murder inquiry in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: White Mason

citations.quote: White Mason, the local officer, was a personal friend
White Mason was a quiet, comfortable-looking person in a loose tweed suit, with a clean-shaved, ruddy face

nodes.description: White Mason, the quiet, comfortable-looking local Sussex officer who is a personal friend and calls in Scotland Yard and Sherlock Holmes to the Birlstone Manor murder in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Fred Porlock

citations.quote: Porlock, Watson, is a nom-de-plume, a mere identification mark; but behind it lies a shifty and evasive personality.
Led on by some rudimentary aspirations towards right... he has once or twice given me advance information which has been of value

nodes.description: Fred Porlock, a pseudonymous informant within Moriarty's criminal organisation who is driven by rudimentary moral aspirations to send Holmes advance warnings, described as a shifty and evasive personality in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Captain Marvin

citations.quote: Captain Marvin is my name.
the stern blue eyes of Captain Marvin of the Mine Police gleaming behind the sights

nodes.description: Captain Marvin, a stern officer of the Mine Police in Vermissa Valley who pursues the Scowrers and whose rifle-sight gleams behind Birdy Edwards at the climax of The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Brother Morris

citations.quote: An elderly, clean-shaved man with a kindly face and a good brow rose from the end of the table
I am a good Catholic; but the priest would have no word with me when he heard I was a Scowrer

nodes.description: Brother Morris, an elderly, clean-shaved Scowrer in the Vermissa Valley who, troubled by Catholic conscience over the organisation's murders, attempts to counsel moderation in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Mike Scanlan

citations.quote: I'm Brother Scanlan, Lodge 341, Vermissa Valley. Glad to see you in these parts.
Scanlan had been given a sealed note to be left at the address of Miss Ettie Shafter

nodes.description: Mike Scanlan, a member of Scowrers Lodge 341 in Vermissa Valley who befriends Birdy Edwards on his arrival and unwittingly carries a sealed note to Ettie Shafter, in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: John McMurdo alias

citations.quote: I'm Brother John McMurdo, Lodge 29, Chicago.
I never killed a man in Chicago. I never minted a dollar in my life.

nodes.description: John McMurdo, the alias used by undercover Pinkerton detective Birdy Edwards when he infiltrated Lodge 29 in Chicago in The Valley of Fear, claiming brotherhood membership to gain the trust of the Scowrers.

nodes.label: Steve Wilson alias

citations.quote: He's a brave man, but not a crazy one. He passes under the name of Steve Wilson, and he is lodging at Hobson's Patch.

nodes.description: Steve Wilson, a secondary alias used by Birdy Edwards when he lodged at Hobson's Patch in The Valley of Fear during the course of his undercover mission.

nodes.label: Birlstone Manor murder

citations.quote: Mr. Douglas of Birlstone Manor House was horribly murdered last night!
the papers will be full of the Birlstone mystery in a day or two

nodes.description: The Birlstone Manor murder, the central case of The Valley of Fear in which John Douglas is apparently killed at his Sussex manor house, a crime ultimately linked to Professor Moriarty.

nodes.label: Scowrers intimidation and murder campaign
nodes.source_location: Part II, Chapter I; Part II, Chapter IV

citations.section: Part II, Chapter I; Part II, Chapter IV
citations.quote: in ten long years of outrage there had been no single conviction

citations.section: Part II, Chapter I; Part II, Chapter IV
citations.quote: The Scowrers are the Eminent Order of Freemen! ... It's a murder society, that's vat it is.

evidence_refs: corpus/sherlock-holmes/the-valley-of-fear/text.txt#Part II, Chapter I; Part II, Chapter IV
nodes.description: The Scowrers' intimidation and murder campaign, the decade-long reign of terror by the Eminent Order of Freemen in Vermissa Valley, Pennsylvania, in The Valley of Fear, resulting in numerous murders without a single conviction before Birdy Edwards's infiltration.

nodes.label: The Scowrers

citations.quote: I am one of the Eminent Order of Freemen.
The Scowrers are the Eminent Order of Freemen!

nodes.description: The Scowrers, the violent secret society operating under the guise of the Eminent Order of Freemen in the Pennsylvania coalfields in The Valley of Fear, led by Boss McGinty and responsible for a decade of unsolved murders and intimidation.

nodes.label: Birlstone Manor House, Sussex

citations.quote: the ancient Manor House of Birlstone. Part of this venerable building dates back to the time of the first crusade
the long, low Jacobean house of dingy, liver-coloured brick lay before us, with an old-fashioned garden of cut yews

nodes.description: Birlstone Manor House, Sussex, a venerable Jacobean house of liver-coloured brick surrounded by a moat with a drawbridge, where the staged murder of John Douglas takes place in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Vermissa Valley, Pennsylvania

citations.quote: the central township which lies at the head of Vermissa Valley
Vermissa is a flourishing little town at the head of one of the best known coal and iron valleys in the United States

nodes.description: Vermissa Valley, Pennsylvania, a prosperous coal and iron district whose central township is dominated by the violent Scowrers under McGinty, forming the setting for Part II of The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: McGinty's Union House saloon, Vermissa

citations.quote: That's the Union House. Jack McGinty is the boss there.
the bar of McGinty's saloon was crowded as usual; for it was the favourite loafing place of all the rougher elements of the town

nodes.description: McGinty's Union House saloon in Vermissa, Pennsylvania, in Part II of The Valley of Fear, the rough gathering place dominated by Black Jack McGinty where the Scowrers conduct their violent criminal business.

nodes.label: V.V. 341 card found beside dead man

citations.quote: The initials V.V. and under them the number 341 were rudely scrawled in ink upon it.
it would seem that the V.V. upon the card by the dead body might stand for Vermissa Valley

nodes.description: A card inscribed 'V.V. 341' found beside the Birlstone Manor corpse in The Valley of Fear, later decoded to stand for Vermissa Valley Lodge 341 of the Scowrers criminal society.

nodes.label: Sawn-off shotgun (Pennsylvania Small Arms Co.)

citations.quote: Pennsylvania Small Arms Company—well-known American firm
a shotgun with the barrel sawed off a foot in front of the triggers... the triggers had been wired together

nodes.description: A Pennsylvania Small Arms Company shotgun with its barrel sawn off and its triggers wired together, found at Birlstone Manor in The Valley of Fear as evidence of an American-style killing.

nodes.label: Missing wedding ring from dead man's finger

citations.quote: They've taken his wedding ring!
My rings were put on his finger; but when it came to the wedding ring... it would have taken a file to get it off.

nodes.description: A wedding ring absent from the Birlstone Manor corpse's finger in The Valley of Fear, significant because the substituted dead man could not wear Douglas's tight-fitting ring, revealing the body-swap.

nodes.label: Lodge brand: triangle within a circle on forearm
nodes.source_location: Part I, Chapter III; Part II, Chapter III

citations.section: Part I, Chapter III; Part II, Chapter III
citations.quote: a curious brown design, a triangle inside a circle, standing out in vivid relief upon the lard-coloured skin

citations.section: Part I, Chapter III; Part II, Chapter III
citations.quote: It was a circle with a triangle within it. 'D'you know what that means?'

evidence_refs: corpus/sherlock-holmes/the-valley-of-fear/text.txt#Part I, Chapter III; Part II, Chapter III
nodes.description: A tattooed brand of a triangle within a circle on the forearm of John Douglas, marking him as a former member of the Scowrers, which Sherlock Holmes observes as a key clue in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Bundle weighted with dumb-bell hidden in moat

citations.quote: This is what we are after, Mr. Barker—this bundle, weighted with a dumb-bell, which you have just raised from the bottom of the moat.
From within he extracted a dumb-bell... a pair of boots... a long, deadly, sheathed knife... a gray tweed suit, and a short yellow overcoat.

nodes.description: A weighted bundle containing a dumb-bell, knife, boots, and a grey tweed suit hidden at the bottom of Birlstone Manor's moat, recovered by Holmes to expose the staged-death scheme in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Porlock's book-cipher warning message

citations.quote: 534 C2 13 127 36 31 4 17 21 41 DOUGLAS 109 293 5 37 BIRLSTONE 26 BIRLSTONE 9 47 171
'There is danger—may—come—very soon—one.' Then we have the name 'Douglas'—'rich—country—now—at Birlstone—House—Birlstone—confidence—is—pressing.'

nodes.description: A book-cipher warning message sent by Porlock to Holmes in The Valley of Fear, deciphered from Whitaker's Almanac to reveal that a man named Douglas at Birlstone House faces mortal danger.

nodes.label: Book-cipher decryption (Whitaker's Almanac)

citations.quote: Let us see then if we can narrow it down... a large book, printed in double columns and in common use.
An almanac! Let us consider the claims of Whitaker's Almanac.

nodes.description: The book-cipher method applied by Holmes in The Valley of Fear to decode Fred Porlock's warning, reasoning that the cipher key must be Whitaker's Almanac—a large, double-column book in common use.

nodes.label: Fake moat-draining notice to flush out hidden evidence

citations.quote: I'll dictate it, if you like... 'It has struck me that it is our duty to drain the moat'
whoever had hidden the bundle would most certainly withdraw it the moment that darkness enabled him to do so

nodes.description: Holmes's ruse of issuing a notice about draining the Birlstone moat so that whoever hid evidence in the water would retrieve it at nightfall and so be caught, in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Undercover Pinkerton infiltration of criminal lodge

citations.quote: I went to Chicago and was made one [a Freeman]. Then I was surer than ever... So I stayed to look after it.
I joined your infernal lodge, and I took my share in your councils.

nodes.description: Birdy Edwards's Pinkerton infiltration method in 'The Valley of Fear', in which he joined the Freemen lodge in Chicago and then the Scowrers at Vermissa, sharing their councils, to gather evidence against the criminal organisation from within.

nodes.label: Moriarty engaged by Scowrer exiles to track down Edwards

citations.quote: Having an English job to do, they took into partnership, as any foreign criminal could do, this great consultant in crime.
the first word that ever came to us of the business was from one of his lieutenants.

nodes.description: Professor Moriarty's role as hired consultant in 'The Valley of Fear': engaged by Scowrer exiles abroad to locate Birdy Edwards in England, with the commission arriving through one of Moriarty's lieutenants.

nodes.label: Scowrer survivors' sworn vengeance against Edwards

citations.quote: They had sworn an oath on all that they thought holy to have his blood as a vengeance for their comrades.
From Chicago he was chased, after two attempts so near success that it was sure that the third would get him.

nodes.description: The Scowrer survivors' sworn motive in 'The Valley of Fear': a solemn oath to kill Birdy Edwards in revenge for their destroyed organisation, pursuing him from Chicago with two near-successes before Moriarty is brought in.

nodes.label: Trapping of the Scowrers by Birdy Edwards

citations.quote: 'Birdy Edwards is here. I am Birdy Edwards!'
with a sudden shivering of glass, a bristle of glistening rifle barrels broke through each window

nodes.description: The moment Birdy Edwards reveals his true identity to the Scowrers assembled at the lodge meeting, simultaneously springing the police ambush with rifles breaking through every window in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Staged murder at Birlstone: Baldwin's body substituted for Douglas

citations.quote: There were his height and hair and figure, about the same as my own. No one could swear to his face, poor devil! I brought down this suit of clothes, and in a quarter of an hour Barker and I had put my dressing gown on him and he lay as you found him.

nodes.description: The staged murder at Birlstone in which John Douglas kills Ted Baldwin and substitutes Baldwin's similarly built body — disfigured beyond recognition — for his own, to fake his own death in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: John Douglas revealed to be Birdy Edwards, Pinkerton detective

citations.quote: I am Birdy Edwards of Pinkerton's. I was chosen to break up your gang. I had a hard and dangerous game to play.
the man had been known as John Douglas

nodes.description: In Part II of The Valley of Fear, John Douglas of Birlstone Manor is revealed to be the Pinkerton detective Birdy Edwards, who had infiltrated and broken up the Scowrers gang under deep cover.

nodes.label: Moriarty arranged the death of Douglas/Edwards aboard ship

citations.quote: Jack has been lost overboard in gale off St. Helena. No one knows how accident occurred.
I've no doubt it was well stage-managed.
I can tell a Moriarty when I see one. This crime is from London, not from America.

nodes.description: In the Epilogue to The Valley of Fear, Holmes deduces that Moriarty arranged for John Douglas to be thrown overboard in a gale off St Helena, ensuring the Pinkerton detective would never give evidence against him.

nodes.label: Whitaker's Almanac (cipher key)

citations.quote: Let us consider the claims of Whitaker's Almanac. It is in common use. It has the requisite number of pages. It is in double column.
He picked the volume from his desk. 'Here is page 534, column two'

nodes.description: Whitaker's Almanac, the key used to decode the book-cipher message in 'The Valley of Fear', selected by Holmes as the most likely common reference book of sufficient pages and wide circulation for a coded communication.

nodes.label: Birlstone Manor moat and drawbridge

citations.quote: The only approach to the house was over a drawbridge
the drawbridge was not only capable of being raised, but actually was raised every evening and lowered every morning

nodes.description: The moat and drawbridge of Birlstone Manor in 'The Valley of Fear', the only approach to the house, raised every evening and lowered every morning—a security feature that becomes central to the murder plot.

citations.section: A Trap to Catch a Cracksman
